text: Preface Zeng's Preface Wang Ying's Preface Luo Rujing's Preface The Author's Own Preface Volume One Records of a Night Journey in Chang'an The Tale of the Monkey Listening to Sutras The Record of Playing the Piano at Moonlit Night He Siming's Travels in Fengdu The Annals of the Two Sichuan Prefectures Volume Two The Record of the Twin Trees The Tale of Tian Zhu Meeting Xue Tao in Verse The Record of Sword Dancing in Qingcheng The Visit to the Pipa Pavilion on a Moonlit Evening The Story of Luan Luan Volume Three The Record of the Phoenix-Tailed Grass The Strange Tales of Wuping The Tale of Qiong Nu

Records of Encountering Immortals at the Manting Pavilion* *The Biography of Hu Meiniang Volume Four *The Tale of Flowers and Candles in the Fairyland* *The Biography of the Imperial Censor at Mount Tai* *The Tale of the Clay God at the Riverside Temple* *The Tale of the Screen Adorned with Lotus Flowers* *The Tale of the Swing Festival* Volume Five *The Tale of Jia Yunhua's Return from the Dead* Preface by Zeng Recently, the *Qu* family of Qiantang wrote *The Tales of the Lanterns*, which is filled with new and strange stories. People enjoy passing them on and discussing them, and thus the tales became widely popular. My friend Li Changqi, the governor of Guangxi, compiled recent events he had heard or witnessed into a collection and named it *The Tales of the Lanterns Left Behind*. I read it and initially did not have the time to examine it in detail. One night, I lit a large candle and read through the entire book, staying awake until dawn. I understood the entire narrative and its sequence, and it was quite familiar. One day, after finishing my meal, I discussed it with my colleagues. They were all delighted and surprised, saying, "You must have found a remarkable book! What stories are so extraordinary?" Li Changqi then asked me to write a preface for it. The great principles and teachings of the sages are already well-known and widely recited in books. Some are beyond comprehension, while others broaden one's knowledge and provide material for discussion, and these are not to be neglected. Li Changqi is highly learned and talented, with a mind that flows like a spring and accumulates like a mountain. Therefore, his writings are rich, beautiful, and adorned with brilliant language. Readers are all delighted to see the characters come to life and are never tired of reading them. How delightful it is! Li Changqi is related to me by marriage, and we share the bond of having passed the same imperial examination. Because I have read this collection, I have written this preface for it.

In the spring of Yongle Gengzi, during the intercalary month of the lower half of the first month, the Hanlin Academy scholar-official, the Imperial Tutor, and the Doctor of Instruction, who was also tasked with compiling national history, from Yongfeng, wrote this. Wang Ying's Preface I have read *The Remaining Tales of the Lantern* by Li Changqi of Luzhou, which contains stories of supernatural beings and mystical events. I secretly admire Changqi's extensive knowledge and wide experience, his great talent and profound insight, as well as the skill and beauty of his literary works. Someone asked me, "Certain things are mysterious and vague, and gentlemen do not believe them. Why are you pleased with them?" I replied, "Not so! The classics carry the Way, and history records events. There are also the various schools of thought, who use words to compare events, creating a multitude of writings. There are also the diverse traditions of Chinese thought, which record ancient and forgotten events, along with contemporary miscellaneous discussions, witty remarks, and stories of the supernatural. These have varying merits and demerits, but are there not some valuable aspects to them? It lies in careful selection alone. Therefore, discard those who speak excessively without evidence. If the facts are verified and the words are not false, and they are relevant to the world and education, then record them. In this collection, there are indeed some valuable things."

What is described herein, such as the arrogance and licentiousness of the Tang princes, the loyal death of a woman named Tan, and the adherence to righteousness by Zhao Luan and Qiong Nu, serves to make readers learn from and be encouraged by it. As for the other works, their choice of words and themes, their opening and closing, their ups and downs, are also very commendable, which is why I am pleased. Have you not heard of this? In the past, Wang Chong wrote his treatise, which was praised by Cai Yong; Zhang Hua's extensive knowledge was praised by Ruan Ji; and Gan Bao's compilation was praised by Liu Hui. Those who wield the brush and take writing as their duty are something difficult for people to achieve. Ancient people highly valued it, so how dare I not aspire to the ancients and not find value in this? Thus, the questioner withdrew. Therefore, I write this preface to introduce the beginning, so that scholars of the world may know the breadth of Chang Qi's talents and knowledge, and not be surprised by the uniqueness of his writings. Chang Qi has written many poems and lyrics, but these are merely his play. Initially, he served as a Langzhong in the Ministry of Rites, and now he holds the position of Left Censor-in-Chief of Guangxi. He and I are fellow Jinshi graduates.

**Preface by Luo Rujing** The *Tales Told by the Lantern* consists of four volumes, totaling twenty chapters, and is the work of Mr. Li Changqi, the Governor of Guangxi, who succeeded the compiler from Qiantang, Qiu. Mr. Li once passed the Mingjing examination and was promoted to a high rank, and he also served as a famous Jinshi who compiled and edited the central secret archives. He is eloquent and well-versed, qualities that he has possessed from the beginning. Therefore, his writings, which are recorded in writing, are sufficient to broaden one's heart and mind, expand one's knowledge, and enrich one's learning. Often, he searches for and highlights the strange and unusual, writing in detail and recording comprehensively. This is not done without purpose! However, some say that the content contains many supernatural elements, which Confucians do not believe. I say that this is not so! The sacred scriptures and the teachings of the wise, which set examples and establish norms, are essential for maintaining social order. Even minor works, short stories, divination, and agriculture, as well as books on strategy, diplomacy, and divination, all have value in the times.

Let alone what is recorded here, such as the chastity of the wife of the baker, the propriety of Tan's wife, the honesty and uprightness of He Siming, the friendship of Ji Fuxing, the elegant conduct of the two daughters of Jia and Zu, and the wisdom and insight of the two young men of Zhen and Wen—these all concern moral influence and are worthy of being examples for the world. Those records in the *Qi Hua* (Strange Tales), the *Yi Ming* (Records of the Underworld), the *Soushen Ji* (Records of the Supernatural), and the *Yijian Zhi* (Records of the Unusual) that are filled with absurd and fantastical tales, how could they be taken seriously in discussions? As for the records of the local official, they are verified by events and consistent with reason, and they are what everyone enjoys talking about and what our group enjoys hearing. Is it a matter of the supernatural? I have also heard that Han Yu of Changli wrote *Mao Ying* (The Biography of Mao Ying) and *Ge Hua* (The Biography of Ge Hua), and the respected scholars of the past described them as "among the precious fruits, the pear and the apple," merely to provide variety in taste. This compilation is also like that." Someone agreed. Therefore, I arranged it at the end of the scroll, perhaps to provide a laugh for the high-minded discussions of the officials.

In the first month of the year of Yongle's eighteenth year, on the first day of the month, auspicious, Huanlin Xiu Zhuan, Right Minister of the Ministry of Works at the Imperial Residence, friend of the same year, Luo Rujing, writes. Author's Preface In the past, when I was in charge of the construction of Changgan Temple, I had the opportunity to read the work "Rou Rou's Biography" by Guibin Gui. I admired his elegant and graceful thoughts, his gentle meaning, and his skillful words. Inspired by this, I wrote "The Return of the Soul" as a model for it. Seven years later, while working in Fangshan, a guest presented me with the collection "New Tales of the Lamp by Qu of Qiantang." I was again captivated by it and eagerly wanted to imitate it. Although I was busy with worldly affairs, my heart and mind were somewhat distracted, yet I still felt an urge to write. In my spare time, I gathered and collected hearsay, arranging it into twenty pieces, naming it "Tales of the Lamp: The Leftovers," and still included "The Return of the Soul" at the end. Because it was written while traveling away from home, based on memory, without books to verify, I feared many contradictions and did not dare to show it to others. After being released from my bonds, I stayed at a guesthouse near Shuncheng Gate. Scholar Zeng Gongzi Qi visited me, and he happened to see it. He clapped his hands and said, "Is this not what is called writing for amusement?" He then pre

Thinking back to what the ancient sage said: "To eat one's fill all day long without using one's mind. Isn't there something like playing games? Doing that is still better than nothing!" Especially since I have experienced two periods of hardship, and the days of eating my fill are few. Moreover, I have no inclination for games, and instead rely on paper and ink to write and compose poetry. How else can I open up my heart and express my inner frustrations? Although I know that this is close to being humorous and playful, but I cannot worry about that, just as one cannot help but groan when in pain. Why should I be ashamed? Although, in *Gao Tang* and *Luo Shen*, the meaning lies beyond the words. They were all written during leisure time, so they are naturally detailed and beautifully crafted, and even a thousand years later, they are still widely praised. As for me, I am burdened with guilt and have nothing better to do, so I use this as a way to distract myself. I did not do this intentionally in my daily life to invite criticism from the refined. Compared to eating one's fill and playing games, perhaps this is closer? So I did not burn it, but instead recorded the reason for its creation at the end of the book, so that I can reflect on it from time to time, to never forget the worries of the past, and to ensure a good ending. Those who enjoy such things can laugh at it, but why should they take it too seriously? In the first day of the fifth month of the Gengzi year of Yongle, by Li Zhen, courtesy name Changqi, from Luling.

Night Journey to Chang'an At the beginning of the Hongwu era, Master Tang Mingzhi and Master Wen Yuanji were both highly esteemed for their experience, learning, and contributions to governance and literature. Soon after, the Prince of Qin was established in his fiefdom. Master Tang was appointed as the Right Chancellor, and Master Wen as the Left Chancellor, and they accompanied him on his journey. At that time, the world was at peace, and the population was thriving. Chang'an, being the former capital of the Han and Tang dynasties, was filled with historical sites. In their spare time, the two officials would only relax in poetry and wine, gaze upon the mountains and rivers, visit ancient sites, and explore secluded places, never parting from each other. One day, Master Wen said to Master Tang, "All the Han dynasty tombs are here. We are fortunate to have no burdensome paperwork, and we will soon retire. It is the perfect time to compose poetry while enjoying the scenery." The official from Luoyang, Master Wu Qiren, replied, "The Long Mausoleum, An Mausoleum, Yang Mausoleum, and Ping Mausoleum are all on the northern slope of Xianyang, twelve zhang high and one hundred twenty-seven bu wide. The Mao Mausoleum is located seventeen li northeast of Xingping County, fourteen zhang high and one hundred forty bu wide, with a square and upright shape resembling an overturned斗. To the east of the mausoleum is the tomb of General Wei Qing. Slightly to the east is the tomb of Huo Qubing, which is said to resemble Mount Qilian. To the northwest is the tomb of Gong Sun Hong, and one li to the west is the tomb of Lady Li. The mountains and rivers here are majestic and beautiful, different from other places. If you wish to visit, you should start here. Moreover, Xingping is eighteen li away, and it can be reached in one day." The two officials agreed. The next day, they set out, accompanied by Master Wu, on the 20th day of the ninth month. Upon returning, they reached the halfway point, and Master Wu's horse was exhausted, and he could not catch up. So, he slowed down and walked slowly, unaware that it had become dark.

The road was long and dark, nearing the second watch of the night, as birds flew and called, and foxes and rabbits were abundant. Filled with great fear, he moved forward cautiously. Suddenly, he saw a faint light in the distance, thinking that a human settlement was not far away. Urging his horse forward, he arrived to find that it was indeed a civilian house. The double doors were wide open, and the lights were still burning. Period Ren dismounted and tied his horse to a tree in the courtyard. Entering and sitting in the guest area, he remained in silence for a long time, hesitant to knock on the door. He coughed repeatedly to make himself known. Shortly after, a servant appeared from the side door and asked where the guest had come from. Period Ren told the truth, and the servant nodded and left. Not long after, the host appeared, a young man dressed casually with a gentle and refined appearance. He greeted the guest and spoke in simple and appropriate terms, asking about the guest's well-being. After tea, he invited the guest into the main hall, which was elegantly and charmingly laid out. The flowers were fragrant, and the tables and seats were clean and elegant. Seated, the young man called his wife to greet the guest. She was exceptionally beautiful, in her twenties, dressed in everyday attire, and did not use makeup. Her graceful movements in the light of incense and candles resembled those of a fairy or goddess. Period Ren privately thought it strange that an ordinary person could have such a beautiful wife, but he was too shy to ask.

After a moment of hesitation, they set out the food and drink, with bowls and beans arranged neatly. Though the table was not overly lavish, the food was strangely beautiful and exquisitely crafted, definitely not of earthly origin. The young men encouraged each other, showing sincere and earnest enthusiasm. When the wine was half gone, the couple both rose to bow and said, "You are a noble person, with a bright future ahead. I have a small request, and I wish to entrust you to convey it to the world." Period Ren replied, "Who are you, and what is your request?" The young man said, "Do not be afraid, I will speak honestly. I am from the Tang dynasty, and I have been here for over seven hundred years. I have never had anyone come here before. Now you have descended upon us, is it perhaps the will of heaven? I will certainly make my request known to the world." Period Ren said, "I wish to hear it all." The young man blushed and hesitated, wanting to speak but then stopping. His wife said, "What harm is there! I will speak for you. My husband was a baker in Chang'an during the reign of Emperor Xuanzong. When Prince Ning, the future Emperor Xuanzong, built his residence in Xingqing坊, our home happened to be near the prince's residence. My husband was a scholar who knew of the An Lushan Rebellion, so he hid by selling baked goods to avoid notice. I also personally handled the household chores, cleaned utensils, and served as a shopkeeper, not considering it a disgrace. One day, the prince passed by and saw me, and he liked me. My husband could not protect our marriage, so I was taken by him. I followed into the prince's residence, and I vowed to die, remaining silent all day. The prince tried to persuade me in many ways, but I paid no heed. One night, he summoned me, pretending I was ill like Cheng Ji, and I was spared. Like this for over a month, the prince had no choice but to order me to return home.

At the time, the historian failed to record the names of the woman and her husband, and thus their names were not documented. Only in *The Collection of True Events* is it said that: "At the side of Prince Ning's residence in the Tang Dynasty, there was a beautiful wife of a baker, whom the prince took for a year. He asked, 'Do you miss your baker husband?' He summoned him to see her, and she wept like rain; the prince felt pity and returned her." Unaware that I was in the prince's palace for only a month, yet it was said to be a year; I sought death to escape, but it was said that he summoned me to see her; the prince never asked me, nor did he ever summon my husband. To be so grossly slandered, how can I bear it? And in the world, poets and literati have composed *The Baker's Wife's Lament* about my story, all displaying their talents and exaggerating the description, with lines that say, "At that time, my husband lightly gave his word, the golden house and the thatched cottage are far apart."

Alas! Reflecting back on that time, the matter arose from coercion, under the overwhelming power of the tyrannical force, could my husband dare to breathe? Now, to be faulted for my husband's light promise, is it not unjust? The matter I entrust to you is this.” Period Ren said, “If you adhere to your principles, it is truly commendable. It is necessary to write honestly to encourage good customs, and to let it remain unknown, how could one not drink hatred in the underworld and bear pain for a hundred generations? Period Ren is not wise, to be praised for my writing, I will write it out for you. But I fear that if it has been passed down for a long time and people are stuck in their perceptions, if corrected at once, people will inevitably doubt. I wish to know your name, to fill the gap in historical records, is it acceptable?” The young man looked solemn and unhappy, saying, “If my name is revealed to the world, the guilt and shame will be endless, it is not what I desire.” Period Ren said, “Then what should be done?” The young man said, “I ask that the previous statement be corrected, that is enough.”

Period Ren asked, "Historians say that Prince Ning was shrewd in anticipating events, firmly declined the position of heir apparent, and was called the pride of the royal family. Yet, he also engaged in such immoral behavior, is this surprising?" The young man replied, "This is his usual behavior, is there anything to be surprised about? However, among the princes of that time, he was the most studious and learned. Although he relied on his favored status and failed to recognize his own faults; yet, seeing my humble wife maintain herself with propriety, he ultimately could not bring himself to offend. The actions of the other members of the royal family are not worth mentioning. For example, Prince Qi did not set up tables when he had his meals, having the courtesans each hold a dish for him to taste. Prince Shen did not warm himself by the fire when it was cold, placing his hands in the bosoms of the courtesans and changing courtesans frequently. Prince Xue carved wooden statues of beautiful women, dressing them in green robes. At night feasts, he had them hold candles, with numerous women musicians and chaotic music and dancing. The candles were especially peculiar; when guests wanted to get drunk, they would suddenly go out like ink, and they would light up again after the event, and no one knew the trick behind it. Such examples are too numerous to list, all were examples of extreme luxury and debauchery, disregarding proper etiquette and law. If one fell into their hands, there would be no escape. Thus, it is important to know about the king's virtues." After the wine was finished, the couple exchanged poems. The husband's poem said: When I was fifteen or sixteen, I hid among the butchers and vendors. It was better to have no ambitions and remain obscure, Than to speak of learning and seeking knowledge. Throughout the year, I watched the stove and the pan, At every festival, I enjoyed the wine. Purple sugar was poured, dripping like milk,

The newly made white, smooth, and delicate pastry. Greatly suitable for auspicious round baskets, Small enough to fill a dish in circular layers. Emerging from fire without any loss or damage, Held aloft by slender hands like the sun and moon. In times of hardship, a Han dynasty noble once sold his prized possessions, Now I blend in and hide myself. A woman in the household shares the same sentiments, A scholarly figure upholds high moral standards. Since our marriage, we have shared the same hardships, Always able to present a tray with humble vegetables. Harmonious couples are truly hard to maintain, Plain clothes and simple hairpins are still pleasing to someone. Like the broken mirror of the couple from Lechang, separation came suddenly, Like the man who died of grief, my heart was severed in the night. The inner family is not lacking in bright eyes, The outer household's poverty is not a good match? The precious throne and grand plan have already been relinquished, The graceful willow and humble reed still need to be retained? In poverty, one only knows how to manage the well and mortar, The common and mediocre do not understand the affairs of kings and lords. The sword was suddenly able to reunite, The overflowing river can indeed be reclaimed. I wish to wield the brush of Dong to dispel doubts, Just to cleanse the old man of shame and embarrassment. **Her wife's poem says:** My family's gate is but ordinary, A thatched house with a simple gate and walls. Hard work leaves no time for adornment, Gentle and compliant, I only know to follow the rites. Last year, I married a learned neighbor, Well-versed in the classics and histories. I did not seek fame or fortune, Rather, I was content to sell baked goods and live among the common folk. Every day, as the sun rises, the shop door opens, Children and monks come and go in a crowd. Once money is earned, I close the door, We sit close together, drinking from the same cup. Who would have thought we would part like Han Peng, Determined to leap into the water or leap from the tower. Red lotus flowers are pure and hard to defile, A white jade returned remains unblemished.

The opulence of the contemporary era has long since perished, The pure soul has held sorrow for all eternity. Please sweep away the absurd theories, To transmit the story of Liang Hong and Meng Guang. Qi Ren played with it repeatedly, Packed it into his bag. The young man immediately ordered the servant to lead the guest to the east hall and to the bed. In a short while, the distant temple bell rang, and the nearby village rooster crowed. The dawn light was faint, and the morning light was dim. Opening his eyes to see, he saw his body was still wet with dew, and the horse was still eating grass. Looking around, everything was silent and empty. Nothing was seen. He then presented the poem to the two gentlemen, who both praised it highly, believing it truly captured the essence of Tang poetry. They ordered it to be carved on the east side of the county, to ensure its lasting transmission. Qi Ren indeed rose to the Hanlin Academy through his literary achievements, dying at the age of eighty-nine, thus fulfilling the prophecy of great things. Afterward, Tang Gong governed Ji'an, and he often recounted the details like this. ### The Monkey That Listens to the Dharma In the county of Jishui, under the jurisdiction of Luzhou, there is an East Mountain, whose roots span a hundred miles, serving as a stronghold for the region. Its beauty and uniqueness make it look like a painting. During the Tiancheng period of the Later Tang dynasty, there was a monk named Xiu who built a thatched hut at the extreme end of the mountain. The trees were dense, and the path was rugged. For years, human footprints were rare. Only when woodcutters ventured deep into the forest did they see the monk sitting under a pine tree, with a flock of birds carrying fruits gathering before him. The monk took each fruit and ate it. After finishing, the birds flew away. The woodcutters occasionally mentioned this to others, and those interested in the unusual followed suit, visiting the hut to see the monk. At that time, the monk was sound asleep, holding a simple pillow and warming his feet, with a young woman guarding his bed. The visitors found this strange and, in admiration, competed to clear the ground and gather materials to build a large monastery. At the start of the construction, the monk called the craftsmen and warned them: "You who work with your hands, must drink alcohol and eat meat."

The dangers posed by the mountain deity are grave, and one should not lightly provoke them, what do you think?” Craftsmen Qi responded, “I shall abstain from meat and alcohol to undertake this task.” The master agreed. After more than a month, one of the craftsmen suddenly could not resist the craving for meat. He descended the mountain, and after several days, returned. While he was engaged in cutting and carving, two tigers leaped over the wall and entered, standing before the craftsmen. They looked around, roaring loudly. The craftsmen were filled with fear. The master said, “It must be that you have broken your vow; it is best to confess, and I will send them away.” The craftsman untied a cloth bag from his waist and handed it to the master, saying, “I just passed through the market at Laobiao, bought a piece of cooked beef, brought it to eat, nothing else.” The master replied, “That is it.” He then cut the beef into two pieces and fed it to the tigers, patting their backs and saying, “Mountain spirits, go now.” After he finished speaking, the tigers disappeared. The people became even more respectful of him. Consequently, donations of gold and silk flowed in like rivers converging, and grand and solemn buildings were completed in no time.

Upon the completion of the construction, the master expounded the Dharma to repay the donors. He explained profound teachings, and heavenly beings showered flowers. Soon, five wells emerged from the ground below the hall, all filled with rice, flour, oil, salt, and vegetables, taken to feed the congregation, neither lacking nor in excess. The master said, "These five Dragon Kings offered their tribute to relieve poverty; this mountain can be named Longji (Dragon Relief) and the temple Qingliang (Cooling)." Now, four of the wells have dried up, only one remains. In front of the master's hermitage, there were a thousand tall trees, shading the clouds and sun. Beneath the tree was a flat rock, and the master would sit there to recite scriptures every day, as a daily habit. An old monkey lived in the tree, hiding to listen and observing the master closely. One day, the master happened to leave, and the monkey descended, put on the master's robe, and took the scriptures from the stone, reading them. When the master returned and saw, the monkey hurriedly ran away. The master did not ask, nor did he tell the other monks, but in his heart, he recognized, "This has already attained enlightenment."

The next day, a scholar from Xiangzhou named Yuan Xiucai came to pay a visit. The teacher knew him and invited him in. Yuan Xiucai was dressed in a black robe and wore a dark headband, with a simple and rustic demeanor. After exchanging courtesies, he said to the teacher, "My surname is Yuan, my courtesy name is Wenshun, and I am from Xiangzhong. My family is large and numerous, and I do not enjoy pursuing official positions. Only I have aspirations for fame and success, and I sought an official position in the capital. Emperor Mingzong, a non-Han Chinese ruler, became confused and bewildered in his old age. Virtuous scholars and talented individuals could not be promoted. I stayed for several years in the capital but ultimately achieved nothing. Someone who knew me well recommended me to be an inspector in Duanzhou. Thinking about the malarial and unhealthy place, I really did not want to go. He then advised me saying, 'You are in such a difficult situation, how can you still choose a place?' Unwillingly, I took my family to the post. Before a year passed, my wife, concubines, and children all died. Alone and emaciated, I no longer served in office. Traveling between the rivers and lakes, I only sought mountains and gazed at water, disassociating myself from the hustle and bustle of fame; seeking the Way and practicing Zen, discussing emptiness in Buddhist texts. I have heard that venerable elders have established a great Dharma banner, undeterred by the distance, I seek to join a pure community. Frowning and furrowing the brows, I am certainly not like the wine-loving Tao Yuanming; gesturing and pondering, I quite resemble the hardworking poet Jia Dao. If you do not reject me, what more could I ask for?" He immediately took out a scroll to present to the teacher, which was a letter of introduction.

The words say: I humbly believe that to be born with a fistful of dreamlike body is due to bad karma; to be familiar with the misty and hazy path of the Three Gorges is also due to good connections. All beings live between heaven and earth, and are all within the cycle of rebirth. I show respect to the master of Longji Mountain, under the seat of the great Zen master Xiu Gong: his nature is as clear as the bright moon, his eyes see through the illusions of the world. In divination and numerology, he surpasses Tu Cheng; in supernatural abilities, he exceeds Bei Du. The bodhi tree is not a tree, his sharp wit and skill are shared with his fellow monks; the pine and cypress are reduced to firewood, bubbles and shadows are viewed equally in the floating world. Respected by all directions, the four assemblies of believers rely on him. If one is a humble person, they are as insignificant as a hair on the body of heaven and earth, a trace in the mountains and forests. To cry and embrace a tree when sorrow comes, who pities the pitiful one whose heart is wounded like a bird; to seek refuge in the forest when in poverty, how can one spare time to choose a tree with ease. With no home to return to, there is a Buddha to rely on. To mourn the loss of wife and children, to be submerged by fame and achievements. To dance with a sword in front of people, it is not due to having the ability of a monkey with long arms; to pass by a temple and inscribe poetry, suddenly it stirs the desire to return to the mountains. Heaven and earth revolve, random changes almost sink into oblivion; spring goes and autumn comes, managing the prosperity and decay of flowers. He wants to stand out and excel, unless one gives up illusions and returns to the truth. To guide those who are lost, leading them to the path to nirvana; to lead to the shore of awakening, quickly boarding the boat of wisdom. I only wish for compassion, accepting with reverence.

After reading through, the teacher said to him, “You are an exceptionally talented man, well-versed in the internal teachings. It is an honor that you have not looked down upon me, and your presence greatly enhances our monastery. However, there is one matter that is inconvenient, and I dare not remain silent about it.” Xun replied, “What is it? I humbly request you to explain.” The teacher said, “If you were to wear a cap and tie up your hair, in my tradition this would be like a monkey wearing a cap — an absurd pretense. But if you were suddenly to shave your head and put on monastic robes, in your tradition this would be like a man who calls himself a Confucian but behaves like a Mohist — inconsistent and hypocritical. How should one deal with such a situation?” Xun became embarrassed and looked somewhat ashamed. After a long pause, he said, “As long as the heart is devoted to Chan Buddhism, what harm is there in appearing in lay dress? I only hope you will not be bound by outward appearances. If one can be content with eating leftover yams, one’s origin is that of a layperson; if one can complete an unfinished scripture, is that not the act of a practitioner? The Dharma gate is vast and encompasses all things.” The teacher said, “If what you say is true, then you are indeed a ‘morning three, evening four’ person.” Xun replied, “Why do you criticize me so harshly?” The teacher said, “It was just a coincidence.” Thus, he was kept in the western pavilion and assigned to teach the novices.

Xun, though naturally intelligent and quick with words, was playful and mischievous, often acting like a child. Sometimes he would sit cross-legged on a bed, cover his head with a blanket, and make the monks pay homage, saying, "This is the manifestation of the White-robed Guanyin." At other times, he would sit with his legs spread in a niche, paint his face with indigo, and make the kitchen workers show respect, saying, "This is the Great Saint of Hongshan overseeing the kitchen." He would sometimes put a snake in a bowl, calling it taming a dragon; or tie a cat to the seat, calling it taming a tiger. There were many such instances. The monks found it troublesome and reported it to the master. The master smiled and said, "It's his usual behavior; treat him well." The monks then dared not speak, and Xun remained as usual. However, many of the mountain's scenery were inscribed by him, but not all were fully recorded; let's record a few of the best ones. △Title: Explanation of the Empty Temple The ancient pagoda soars high like a jade bamboo, its shadow half-pressing the noisy water under the slanting sun. The old monk sits with a closed sutra, quietly listening to the sound of pines roaring like the sea. △Inscription for the Abbot's Room

A Wind Instrument's Melody Echoes Through the Dark Spring, Red Petals Fall Before the Buddha Altar. White Clouds Deeply Guard the Monk's Bed, Refusing to Allow the Mundane Visitors to Sleep. △Farewell to the Monk Leaving the Mountain Green Pines Intrude on His Robe, Sandals Leave Marks on the Mossy Ground. With a Walking Stick, He Has Paced Here Many Times. The Mountain Monk Has Forgotten the Beauty of the Mountains, Departing into the Mundane World, Never to Return. △Elegy for the Crane Far from the Honorific Pillar, Near the Mysterious Threshold, He Leaves Behind the Transient and Superficial, Staying with the Lazy and Indifferent. The Sound of a Golden Bell Rings in the Evening of Autumn, Two Birds Fly, Bringing Back the Clouds. △Gift to the Monk One Bottle, One Bowl, One Robe, A Few Volumes of the *Lengyan Sutra* Follow Him Wherever He Goes. Sitting Steadily on the Meditation Cushion, Forgetting to Leave His Meditative State, His Body Covered in Fragrant Snow, the Lotus Flower Falls. △The Cloth-Bag Monk The Child Pulls on His Robe, He Doesn't Care. He Sets Down His Cloth Bag and Sleeps, Snoring. The Attachments Are Merely Illusions of Greed, Hatred, and Delusion, True Liberation Comes from Precepts, Concentration, and Wisdom. △The Maiden of the Pines Clothing Made from Oak Leaves, No Need to Cut, The Moon in Autumn Hangs Like a Precious Mirror. On the Crane's Back, He Follows the Queen Mother of the West, Having Once Known Qin Shi Huang. △The Falling Leaves The Peaches of Immortality Ripen Three Times, The Mythical Tree Is Used as Firewood Ten Times. Looking Back, He Remembers His Past Companions, His Remains Buried in a Jade Box in the Depths of the Earth. △The Falling Leaves Ten Thousand Pieces of Frost-Red Reflect the Sun's Freshness, Flying Down to Cover the Mossy Bricks. Indifferently, He Doesn't Send the Novice Monk to Sweep Them Away, Lending Them to Deer in the Mountains to Sleep. △The Swallows Nesting in the Abbot's Quarters The Flowers Are Blooming, After the Rain Clears, Spring Is Returning. The Swallows Build Their Nests in Pairs, Flying Through the Curtains in Pairs. Flying Up and Down, Up and Down Again, Leaving the White Gate of the Wang and Xie Families, Entering and Exiting Near the Zen Gate.

The bell's sound stills the air, The long corridor, in clear daylight, is quiet, Young birds learn to fly near and far, Their soft murmurs are worth listening to. To dwell in the temple is good, Its painted beams and carved pillars hold no lasting nest, Autumn departs, spring returns, Forever I shall remain with the mountain monk. △ *Four Scenic Views of the Mountains* The path is overgrown with moss, few guests arrive, Silken threads drift low, following the soft red that flies. Pine branches shed, golden dust falls, A child hangs the monk's robe on a branch to dry. The wind strikes the bamboo by the window, startling the monk's meditation, A bird brushes against withered flowers, scattering fragrance into the ravine. Half of the *Ruanjue* has been read and understood, With needle in hand, I mend my old robe. A few crows return, a few chimes of the bell, Cool moonlight spreads over the lonely peak. Clear frost alone stains the thousand trees, The bright moon covers the mountain, turning it red. Ten *hu* of room are clear, a hundred *na* of robes are warm, Fragrant incense is burned deep into the night. The Daoist loves to watch the moon on the plum branch, I tell the mountain boy not to close the door. One day, the master suddenly ascended the hall, He ordered the attendant to summon Mr. Yuan, and said to him: "Mr. Yuan, the thirtieth day of the twelfth lunar month has arrived." Mr. Yuan replied: "I also know this." Then the master recited a verse: All laws and gates are ultimately empty, Do not long to howl at the moon or sing to the wind. This time, do a somersault, And leap into the sea of awakening of Vairocana.

Upon hearing the words, Xun suddenly gained enlightenment and composed two verses in response to the master, saying: Amidst the springs, stones, mist, and clouds, amidst water and trees, though the skin and fur differ, the spirit is the same. I labor to hear the master explain the verse of non-being; only upon realizing non-being is one truly empty. A myriad of great principles in the forest, a thousand kinds of tricks in the wooden nest to the south. From now on, I shall tread the path of three lifetimes, but what deeper Zen is there to ponder? After finishing singing, he peacefully passed away. The master gathered the assembly and said, "This person is extraordinary; you must not rush through this matter, but examine it carefully." The monks then gathered to observe closely, and it turned out to be a monkey. The master then explained the previous events, and the assembly all marveled at the strangeness! At the time of cremation, the master personally touched the top of his head and said, "Two hundred years from now, you will still be able to enjoy your practice." By the end of the Southern Song Dynasty, there was a woman from a common household who was pregnant and about to give birth. She dreamed that a monkey entered her room, and she gave birth to a boy who resembled a monkey. When he grew up, he did not wish to marry or take a wife, and he firmly sought to become a monk. His parents agreed and sent him to Longji Temple, where he was named Zongmou. Later, he achieved high spiritual attainment, accompanied by tigers and followed by monkeys, performing miraculous transformations that cannot be fully described. The world came to call him a living Buddha. Indeed, he was able to rebuild Buddhist temples and widely spread the Dharma, such as the Reception Hermitage on Rosary Mountain and Yongning Bridge, both of which he constructed. He was nicknamed Zhuyun, and the monastic community called him Master Zhuyun Zongmou. He had ten volumes of recorded sayings and four volumes of collected writings. His discourse on "Snake Filth" was particularly well-known throughout the land. Until now, Longji Temple honors him as the founder of a new era. On his anniversary, there is still the miraculous sight of tigers circling the stupa. Later generations, calculating from the time of Zongmou's birth, found that it exactly matches the records of Master Xiu, and it is indeed a marvelous event!

Moonlit Night Playing the Guqin Wu Sidao of Si Ming was a learned and virtuous man. In the early years of the Hongwu era, he was appointed as the magistrate of Yongxin County in Ji'an Prefecture. On his third day in office, he paid homage to Confucius at the local school. As he looked around, he noticed a faint human figure near the base of the pillars in the hall, which struck him as strange, and he asked about it. A scholar named He Zhongshan approached and said, "This is the shadow of Zhao, the chaste widow from the Song Dynasty. When the Mongols conquered the south, this area was annexed. Chancellor Wen Tianxiang raised an army to support the emperor and briefly reclaimed the region. Not long after, Liu Pan led Mongol forces to capture the city, and many people were killed. The family of Zhao, including the chaste widow, also sought refuge in the school in haste. The widow hid in the Dacheng Hall, but the disorderly soldiers caught up with her, saw her youth and beauty, and intended to assault her."

The woman shouted fiercely, "I am a noble lady, the wife of a distinguished family. How can you be no better than a dog or pig? Furthermore, my father-in-law died at your hands, and my mother-in-law also perished by you. I wish to have your flesh torn into ten thousand pieces to feed the crows. I have only death to look forward to; how can I be coupled with you, you scum?" The soldier, enraged, also killed the one-year-old child she was holding. The blood seeped into the bricks. From the Song and Yuan dynasties to the present day, the bricks were ground with sand and stones, fired with intense heat, and became increasingly clear and bright. The townspeople honored her with a shrine. Mr. Wu asked where the shrine was, and Zhong Shan led him to the site. But all they saw were rats gnawing through the walls and moss adorning the empty steps. Mountains have become valleys and valleys have become mountains; lamenting that the pure spirit has long departed. Times have changed, and things are different; regretting that only the old house remains. The official then sighed and said, "This is my duty as an official." He then donated his salary to rebuild the hall above the pond, carved her image on the back of a stone tablet, and personally wrote an inscription, which he had engraved on the eaves. Readers were filled with awe, tears streamed down their faces, and the name of the chaste woman became renowned.

The son of the public figure, named Xī and known by the courtesy name Jīzhī, particularly admired the spirit of integrity and righteousness and was excellently skilled in playing the qin. Upon seeing the story of the chaste wife, he admired and yearned for it, composing the "Song of the Chaste Pine" and writing it on the qin. One night, the sky was clear and the moon was bright, the air was cool and the people were quiet. Sitting alone in the pavilion, he stroked the strings, wiped the frets, tuned the strings, and adjusted the tuning pegs. Suddenly, a beautiful maiden entered from outside. Jīzhī was surprised and asked, "What kind of woman are you, coming here without permission?" The maiden bowed respectfully and said, "I am a maiden named Zhōng Bìtáo, a servant of the chaste wife of Song Tán. The lady of the house is chaste and virtuous, praised by the Heavenly Deity, and has already attained a high rank among the immortals. She presides over the place of Lady Wei in the vicinity of Mount Heng and enjoys the music of heaven. The highest deity, fearing that people would disrespect her shadow, ordered the Six Dings to retrieve it. They dressed her in clothes and a hat and had her sit, hiding her in the celestial realms. The office of Virtue and Filial Piety stated that her shadow is in Confucius' ritual hall, placed in its proper place."

Now it must be taken, but this will inevitably be followed by wind and thunder, startling the revered sage, which is not the way to honor the Dao and exalt Confucianism. It would be better to remain in the human world, serving as eternal inspiration. For the world's teachings, this is not a small benefit. The highest authority approves it, ordering the Xuanhu Provincial Office to descend to Fengdu, commanding the spirits of the local school to always protect it, and the Thunder Department should periodically inspect and review. The underworld authorities suggest that the principles of yin and yang value distance and avoid suspicion. The spirits of the local school only need external protection; if close, it is appropriate to use old people. I am fortunate to have no sins or faults, having long served under the teachings, and am appointed to a minor position to serve and protect. Since taking office, I have no place to rest, temporarily residing in the school's land temple. Inappropriately mixed with male deities, this is very inconvenient. I wish to request setting up a separate seat beside the chaste woman, inscribed as 'The Spirit Tablet of Former Attendant Zhong.' Then there will be no suffering, gaining the shelter of a sparrow; spirits have a place to return to, avoiding being mixed with fish and dragons. If you show mercy, grant this request. Ji approved it.

The Virtuous Woman, residing in the southern mountains, was asked, "Did she also come to the shrine?" The lady replied, "She did not come. Since the great gentleman, a paragon of virtue, renovated the shrine, she descended briefly for a short while. That night, all sounds were silent, and the moonlight was as bright as daylight. The lady mother looked down upon the old hometown, seeing that people had changed while objects remained the same. The yellow dust and clear water, the earth and firewood, evoked a profound sense of the immortal's return! She then took up her zither and played a mournful tune. I listened to it, feeling deeply moved, and tears fell like rain. The lady mother turned to me and said, 'You are still lingering in the records of the dead, and I cannot comfort you. Go and fetch paper and ink.' I followed her instructions and brought them. She then dipped her brush and composed twenty seven-character regulated verse poems by collecting ancient lines, giving them to me, and then she tossed her brush into the air and disappeared into the sky." I asked, "Where are the poems?" She replied, "I treasure them as if they were precious jewels. The original manuscript is unattainable. Even if I were to give them to you, the immortal's writings in cloud script, you would not be able to read them. Only to be recited, it is appropriate to record them immediately." The poems are as follows: Flowers press against the railings, and the spring day is long (from "Tang Music" by Wen Feiying), a clear song breaks one's heart (from "Tang Music" by Shen Yunqing). Where do the clouds and rain disperse? (from Wen Feiying) Heaven and earth are both boundless (from "The Drum Blowing" by Song Yong). Already entrusted to the zither to convey secret meanings (from "The Drum Blowing" by Hu Su), not to play the qin for the celestial garments (from "The Drum Blowing" by Song Yong). The old stories of the south should not be revisited (from "The Grass Hall Poetic Remains" by Li Yu), both the peach leaf and the peach root are worthy of sorrow (from "The Poetic Canon" by Song Xiang).

The soul returns to the vast void, the spirit to the springs (from "Three-Body" by Zhu Bao), yet regrets that the beautiful maiden has misled the young man (from an anonymous work in "Gu Chu"). It is because the peach blossoms are greedy for fruit (from Tang dynasty poet Wang Jian), only the plum blossoms should remain unchanged (from Chen Jianzhai in "Shi Tong"). One would not fall behind others in charm or grace (Tang dynasty poet Li Bai), sorrow and joy still startle before the flowing water (from Xu Hun in "Gu Chu"). Why still gaze intently at dusk (from Cui Jue in "Gu Chu"), the lonely lamp has been picked clean, yet sleep has not come (Tang dynasty poet Bai Juyi). The cold insects chirp and the trees are dark green (from Li She in "Three-Body"), the tall buildings on the city wall connect to the vast wilderness (from Liu Zongyuan in "Gu Chu"). The sound of the water clock in the middle of the night urges the dawn arrow (Tang dynasty poet Du Fu), the six streets are bathed in bright autumn light (from Zhang Bi in "Gu Chu"). The entire courtyard is filled with poetic scenes of falling red leaves (from Yong Tao in "Three-Body"), the sad winds and weeping poplars here add to the sorrow (Tang dynasty poet Li Bai). The dance sleeves and bent bows are all forgotten (from a painting of a beautiful woman), in the world, only mice drag their intestines (Northern Song dynasty poet Ouyang Xiu). The clouds imagine clothes, the flowers imagine beauty (Tang dynasty poet Li Bai), youth has passed in the midst of chaos and turmoil (from Liu Wenfang in "Tang Yin"). If fame, wealth, and glory were to last forever (Tang dynasty poet Li Bai), gains and losses, sorrows and joys are all empty (Tang dynasty poet Wen Tingyun). Sunlight flies like wild horses through the window (from Han Wo in "Gu Chu"), the color of trees before the rocks hides the windows (from Wang Wei in "Tang Yin"). Without the colorful wings of a phoenix, one cannot fly together (from Li Shangyin in "Gu Chu"), the fragrant chariot with oil-burnished walls will not be met again (from Yan Shu in "Shi Tong").

One should smile at returning to a simple life without much achievement, as each year brings sorrow during the passing of spring. Climbing fragrant trees while lamenting the fading of flowers, and feeling the warmth of heavy quilts leading to many dreams. The dark clouds from the mountainous region of Guilin are like ink, while the gentle wind and calm waters of the Sichuan River are like silk. One must reflect on their wealth and status, and there is little one can do about the many events of life. A home in a cold pond, with the door closed, is a place where a high and refined spirit is rare in the world. Not using makeup to spoil one's natural beauty, but regretting that black dust has stained one's white clothes. Looking back as the returning geese fly away, and the soul following the flying cuckoo bird. Tears blown by the east wind as flowers fall, and sadness that one's youthful appearance will not return.

Sometimes one wears clothes upside down (Tang Du Fu), turning and twisting thousands of times, too lazy to get out of bed (Tang Cui Yingying). The beautiful bones have turned to fragrant soil (from "Guchui" by Pi Rihou), the humble door has not yet smelled the fragrance of brocade and silk (from "Guchui" by Qin Taoyu). The crowns and carriages of the Han dynasty are all tombs (from "Santai" by Tang Yanqian), the mountains and rivers of the Wei state are half in the setting sun (from "Guchui" by Li Yi). The entire view is filled with ancient waves and tides (from "Guchui" by Xue Feng), the separated person feels even more sorrow here (from "Guchui" by Luo Ye). An inch of longing turns to an inch of ash (from "Guchui" by Li Shangyin), temporarily linger with a folding fan (from "Tangyin" by Wang Shaobo). The guest arrives at an ancient temple under the bright moon (from "Guchui" by Xiang Si), the wind is calm, and the flowers are blooming in the cold pond (from "Guchui" by Liu Cang). The green mountains and clear waters seem the same as before (from "Guchui" by Geng Wei), youthful beauty and white hair alternately urge each other (from "Guchui" by Xue Feng). Indifference is not as bitter as passion (from "Cao Tang" by Yan Shu), would you believe that the sorrowful intestines turn nine times a day (from "Guchui" by Cui Lu).

Appearance may change, but voice remains (Su Shi, *Shitong*); the distant land struggles to summon the souls of the ancients (Han Yu, *Guchui*). Idlely tying willow branches, thinking of distant paths (Fan Zhen, *Shitong*); wanting to write on flower leaves to send to the morning clouds (Li Shangyin, *Guchui*). The window shows the waning moon; where is the person? (Hu Zeng, *Guchui*); the tree is drenched in the scent of wildness; the crane hears it too (Lu Guimeng, *Guchui*). Today, I alone pass through the land of song and dance (Zhao Gu, *Santi*); the delicate frosty moon coldly seeps into the door (Kang Boke, *Cao Tang*). Wind and fire year after year report the dust of the invaders (Li Jiayou, *Santi*); every time I look back, I am filled with deep sorrow (Li Qunyu, *Guchui*). Where are the bright eyes and white teeth now (Du Fu)? Strange clothes and unusual sounds are not close to me (Liu Zongyuan, *Guchui*). A few trees of beautiful flowers in idle daylight (Wu Rong, *Guchui*); a few withered willows cannot withstand the spring (Liu Yuxi, *Tangyin*). The fierce wind blows away the deep red (Du Mu); water surrounds the long mountain, filling one with sorrow (Li Yuan, *Santi*).

Hearing the strings and pipes from afar, half of it is sorrowful (from *The Drum and Flute Collection* by Sikong Shu); the embroidered quilt has been wetted through with tears like rouge (from *The Hermitage* by Kang Bokai). Birds sing, flowers fall, where is the person? (from *The Drum and Flute Collection* by Cui Jue) As the season changes, bees are sad but butterflies do not yet know (from *The Three-Body Problem* by Zheng Gu). The owl perches on the dust for just one day (from *The Three-Body Problem* by Xu Hun), as the snow melts, the magpies have been there for a long time (by Du Fu). Green clouds hang askew, gold hairpins fall (from *The Hermitage* by Yan Shu), standing alone in the vastness, reciting poetry to oneself (by Du Fu). Looking westward at the smoky outskirts, the sunset is dim (from *The Drum and Flute Collection* by Chen Shangmei); the world's path is filled with weapons, and it is a pity to part temporarily (from *The Drum and Flute Collection* by Li Shangyin). Inside the house, the golden screen has paintings that come to life (from *The Sounds of Tang* by Li He), pink clouds, red sashes, and lotus silk skirts (from *The Sounds of Tang* by Li He). Reeds rustle, holding autumn rain (from *The Drum and Flute Collection* by Liu Zongyuan), the Copper Sparrow Pavilion is desolate, locked in the evening clouds (from *The Drum and Flute Collection* by Wen Fei). The old business has been completely consumed by wars (from *The Sounds of Tang*), only the green tomb remains facing the twilight (by Du Fu).

Sorrow doubles the length of separation's grief (from *San Ti* by Li Duan), everywhere known to be cast into darkness (from *Gu Chu* by Zheng Gu). Rain ends, the spirit of the deceased mourns the writer (by Li He of Tang), at night deep, lights and fire ascend to Fan Tower (from *Shi Tong* by Liu Zihui). The old hermit in the mountains still exists (from *Shi Tong* by Su Shi), outside the railing, the Yangtze River flows empty and alone (from *Tang Yin* by Wang Bo). The bright moon easily lowers, people disperse (from *Shi Tong* by Su Shi), crows fly away, leaving the water flowing endlessly (from *San Ti* by Yan Wei). Leaves fill the moss-covered steps, pounding fills the city (from *Gu Chu* by Lu Bi), climbing high to look far, one naturally feels sorrow (by Wu Yuanheng in *Tang Qian Jia Shi* by Hong Mai). Jade branch and moon璧 are like spring yesterday (from *Cao Tang* by Zhang Zhongzong), a cold mat and silver bed, dreams unformed (by Wen Tingyun of Tang). Past events drift endlessly, increasing deep sighs (from *Gu Chu* by Xue Neng), clear sorrow gradually sweeps away the lingering drunkenness (by Su Ziyu of Song). Who knew one night's guest at the Qin building (from *Tang Yin* by Li Shangyin), heartbroken by the sound of rain and wind on green lotus leaves (from *Tang Yin* by Wu Shanghao).

A Woman of Graceful Beauty (《唐音》元稹), her tears like rain, her spring sorrow etched in her fading green-black eyebrows (《唐音》王贞白). The music and singing on the tower are silent (宋王介甫), the mountains and rivers are awash with the blood of fierce battles (《鼓吹》胡曾). A thousand years of separation and hatred, she laments and plays the qin lazily (《鼓吹》谭用之), several years of hidden emotions, hard to express (《鼓吹》薛逢). Looking back on her past travels, they are truly like dreams (《诗统》东坡), the cold tide returns only with the setting sun (唐皇甫茂政). Upon seeing the clear day, her appearance changes (《鼓吹》郑准), every time she is surprised by the seasons, she feels like a wandering dandelion seed (《三体》来鹏). The passage of time and the loss of communication (唐杜甫), the streets are empty and desolate (《鼓吹》张泌). On the desolate embankment, a rooster crows in the dark, signaling the dawn moon (《诗统》王介甫), wildflowers and yellow butterflies lead the spring breeze (《唐音》王仲初). The beauties of history, like the dust of the earth (《草堂》辛稼轩词), only King Xiang of Chu remembers them in his dreams (《唐音》李义山).

In every place, the setting sun casts shadows on grass that looks like moss (《鼓吹》韩偓); in a sunny, warm pond, one wanders alone (《鼓吹》韩偓). The attendants are most eager for the thirst of Sima Xiangru (唐李义山), but wishing to compose poetry, they feel ashamed of lacking Song Yu's talent (唐温飞卿). The music transforms into the sounds of birds in the mountains (《三体》李远), and the empty corridor is filled with wildflowers (《鼓吹》皮日休). Knowing that one's presence is transient everywhere (《诗统》高士谈), do not let gold be wasted (《鼓吹》张祜). Sparse stars fill the clear sky (《唐音》储光羲), and near the door, a cold river flows with a gentle sound (《唐音》戎昱). Often suspecting that good things are illusory (《鼓吹》薛能), it is said that even though it seems emotionless, there is still emotion (《唐音》刘禹锡). Drink and forget past grievances (《鼓吹》纪唐夫), and do not seek fame through writing (《鼓吹》柳宗元). In autumn, seeing the moon evokes thoughts of returning home (《唐音》雍陶), and leaning against a warm brazier, one sits until dawn (唐白乐天).

The gate is surrounded by clear jin trees, free from dust and dirt (from *Gu Chu* by Han Wo), and the white stones are covered with green moss (from *Gu Chu* by Xu Hun). The effects of alcohol wear off, and the breeze is gentle (from *Cao Tang* by Su Shi), while the peach blossoms have faded and the vegetable flowers are now in bloom (by Liu Yuxi of the Tang Dynasty). A deep pool of water is poured into a cup (by Li He of the Tang Dynasty), and the long journey of a funeral banner continues after death (from *Gu Chu* by Zhang Hu). True heroes do not belong to any particular group or cause (by Li He of the Tang Dynasty), and why should beautiful women not turn to ashes (by Pan Pan, concubine of Zhang Jianfeng of the Tang Dynasty)? Outside the door, the landscape remains unchanged (by Zhao Chengyou of the Tang Dynasty), and the lotus ponds with red flowers sway in the green waves (from *Xuan Tang* by Xu Hun, selected by Hong Mai). Falling leaves and scattered flowers cannot be restored (from *Tang Yin* by Yang Sizhong), and what is the nature of clouds and flowing water (from *San Ti* by Li Shangyin)? The fish and dragons are lonely in the cold autumn river (by Du Fu of the Tang Dynasty), and the geese do not return due to frequent storms (by Zhao Chengyou of the Tang Dynasty). The quiet alley is deserted, with no vehicles or horses in sight (by Du Fu of the Tang Dynasty), and the sound of a bell emerges from the mist in the deep summer (from *Gu Chu* by Sikong Tu).

Having finished recording, he still pointed to the foot of each sentence, instructing to meticulously note the source book and the author's name. Ji was amazed and said, "The chaste woman who has attained immortality is already famous. How about her mother-in-law and father-in-law?" The maiden replied, "The divine physician bestowed upon her the immortal elixir of Xuanzhou and the talisman for restoring her form and body. An entire family of a hundred members has ascended to the realm of the ladder of immortals." Ji asked, "What is meant by the ladder of immortals?" The maiden explained, "All those who initially attain the Dao are sent here to practice, then gradually ascend in rank, like climbing a ladder, hence the name ladder of immortals." Ji then asked, "Why don't you go together?" The maiden said, "Because in my previous life I was a female physician, I mistakenly administered medicine, causing harm to a noble fetus, and thus in this life I am punished to be reborn as a woman to atone. Because of this, I am slightly delayed, still separated by two layers of existence." Ji asked, "Then are you also from a good family?" The maiden said, "When I was young, my parents sold me to the Zhao family because of poverty. Zhao, a former member of the Song royal family, bought me to serve as a concubine for his daughter. The girl is the chaste woman, and she is about the same age as I. She showed me kindness and affection, treating me like family. When she married into the Tan family, I accompanied her. At that time, the Tan family was at the height of its prosperity, with officials and nobles in succession. The bed was adorned with embroidered peonies, representing the utmost wealth and splendor of the time. The inkstone was cooled with water from the golden well, and it was sprinkled with countless pearls and jewels."

Everything seen and heard is without exception in accordance with propriety and righteousness; whether young or old, all possess great talent. The main lady is intelligent, virtuous, and skilled in poetry and calligraphy, despite staying within the confines of her home. Whenever she composed poetry, she would copy it for her husband, who would read it and then burn the manuscript, as it was not fitting for a woman to be known for her literary works. My master is naturally accomplished and has a free-spirited and elegant demeanor. His writings flow like a torrential flood, rivaling the source of the Three Gorges, and his oratory is so powerful that it astonishes everyone in the room. I, serving at his side, have heard much of his teachings and have learned much of poetry and propriety despite my lowly status. Alas, the Song dynasty has declined, and the Yuan dynasty is rising. Warlords have emerged during the chaotic period, and I lament the loyal officials who tried to support the fallen dynasty. The land is shrouded in mist, and I despise the traitors who betrayed the state for personal gain. My master's wife died nobly, while I, as a servant, survived through hardship and hiding in the wilderness. The master's kindness is hard to repay, and I can only harbor the intention to repay a favor as Ji Zha did. My frail body is easily weakened, and I have become a ghost of the Yisang. The world abhors decline, and who will remember the ghost of Bi Yu? My path is difficult, and who will bury the weak bones of Lu Zhu? A thousand words cannot fully express the situation, and I must part ways due to the different paths of the living and the dead. So, I departed. The next day, Jizi informed the elders. Wugong found the poem strange and unbelievable, and refused to accept it.

After two months, one evening, Jizhi was drunk and could not sleep. He got up and walked out in front of the pavilion, savoring the fragrance of the cassia tree and admiring the moon's reflection on the moon goddess. Soon after, the former concubine again paid her respects and said, "What I have long desired, I humbly received your promise. I thought you, a kind gentleman, would act bravely in the face of injustice; yet, I have been listening intently for a long time, and have not heard of any action. A gentleman helps others achieve their best—what is there to fear that would prevent it from being accomplished?" Jizhi replied, "My father does not believe you—what can be done? You can tell me one or two things that no one knows, and I will report them to my father. Perhaps there will be evidence, or it might succeed." The concubine said, "Remember when Wen Shengxiang raised an army? The seven major families of Yongxin all died in loyalty to the king, and my master and the East Gate Zhang Yudai family led the charge. On the day the city was recaptured, everyone celebrated, but the lady of the house had a worried look and told my master, 'Although the city has been recaptured, the enemy will surely return, and the people of the city will surely suffer. Our fates are uncertain; in case of misfortune, we will only die, swearing never to be disgraced.' My master tried to reassure her with kind words, but the lady of the house did not agree. My master then quoted Sima Guang, saying, 'If heaven favors Song, this will not happen.' The lady of the house shook her head and sighed several times, took her clothes, and wrote ten poems on them; these are also ancient words."

A Woman with an Imperial Court Hairstyle (妾 of Tang Dynasty official Du Hongjian), she married and lived near her in-laws (《唐音》). Who could have known that the grass would stir and the dust would rise (《诗统》), and that fallen petals would turn red and each would grieve (《诗统》 by Song Dynasty poet Song Qi). With her hairpins disheveled and her jade hairpins untidy (《唐音》), a hundred feelings arise involuntarily (Tang Dynasty poet Du Mu). Where is the place of wealth and prosperity (《诗统》)? The sun sets in the west and the water flows east (《杏坛吟》). My husband's red face and my youthful appearance (《唐音》), I married and never left the front door (《诗统》). Now cast aside on the long street (Tang Dynasty poet Liu Yuxi), only the heavens know the depths of my heart (《诗统》 by Song Dynasty poet Ye Shaoweng). With disheveled makeup and tears streaming down her face (《鼓吹》), her hair is tangled and her hairpins are broken, making it especially cold (Song Dynasty poet Wang Anshi). No longer seeing the beauty of her face, the place of her death is empty (Tang Dynasty poet Bai Juyi), looking east toward her ancestral home, the road is long and winding (《三体》).

The tide rises in the vast sea, wild pear trees in bloom (from *The Three-Body Problem*); the sword chases the startled waves, the jade falls to dust (from *Tang Style*). Fresh blood transforms into grass on the plain (by Song Dynasty poet Ma Zicai); one should not be born a woman (by Tang Dynasty poet Bai Juyi). A hundred years of worldly affairs are unbearable to bear (by Tang Dynasty poet Du Fu); a great building is not supported by a single piece of wood (by Song Dynasty poet Wang Tinggui). Generous in the western wind, tears stream across the chest (from *Poetic Anthology*); only the old heaven knows this heart (from *Poetic Anthology*). Blood spurts from the golden spear, lying in iron armor (from *Military Odes*); the mountains and rivers remain, but the people have changed (from *Poetic Anthology*). Swallows in the hall of the Wang and Xie families (by Tang Dynasty poet Liu Yuxi), now flying to whose door? (from *Tang Style*). Seeing no people, only flowers (from *The Three-Body Problem*); smoke shrouds the cold water, moonlight shrouds the sand (by Tang Dynasty poet Du Mu). Since ancient times, no one can escape death (by Song Dynasty official Cai Xiang); do not blame the spring wind, but lament yourself (by Song Dynasty scholar Ouyang Xiu)! A high bun tilted to one side, adorned with gold and jade ornaments (from *Poetic Anthology*); passing idly through six or seven springs (from *Poetic Anthology*). Today's turmoil is all a dream (from *Poetic Anthology*); heroes have no strategy to protect beautiful women (from *Poetic Anthology*). Rising to look at the color of the sky and earth, which is desolate (by Wang Anshi from *Poetic Anthology*); how can one know that the dream of the dust is longer than the dream of the crane (by Song Yong from *Military Odes*). The soul stained with blood cannot return (by Tang Dynasty poet Du Fu); a new grave is empty, burying old clothes (from *Military Odes*).

The master read it and said, "If so, what have I to lament!" Afterward, the master's wife pointed to the child and said, "I will die, but what of this child?" The master replied, "I already knew it, leave it to fate." Then he tied a gold coin around its neck and said, "If you meet a wicked person, the child can use this to buy his life." They then looked at each other and cried, their tears wetting their sleeves. On the day of the disaster, the gold coin was nowhere to be found, but it was stained with blood and left a shadow of a coin beside the child. However, observers did not look closely, so they did not know. The poem is only remembered by me. These two things are unknown to the world." Ji Zhi recorded it and presented it to his father, but Wu Gong did not yet fully believe. He immediately ordered a rider to go to the temple, fetch water, and wash the brick to verify. The coin's trace was clearly visible. The crowd was then shocked. He then did as he was told, inscribed a tablet, and placed it beside the chaste woman's shrine. Ji Zhi offered wine and food to it. That evening, the woman came to thank him and said, "I am grateful for setting up the tablet and for the honor of the ritual, but I have nothing to repay you. Sir, you have always loved the lute, but the *Guangling San* tune has been lost for a long time. I was taught by the master and still remember it. I wish to teach it to you." She then took the score from her sleeve and gave it to Ji Zhi, saying, "Take care of yourself, I will not come again!" She suddenly left. From then on, his skill at the lute greatly improved, and he became the best in Zhejiang. He kept this tune secret and did not pass it on to others. When Ji Zhi died, the score also became extinct.

He Siming's Journey to Fengdu** He Siming, a man from the Song Dynasty, was known by the nickname "Lan Ke Woodcutter." He was proficient in the Five Classics, especially the *Yijing* (Book of Changes), and dedicated himself to the study of human nature. He strongly disliked Daoism and Buddhism. Whenever he encountered their followers on the road, he would rebuke them, saying: "Among the four classes of people, even if one is not a scholar, being a farmer, artisan, or merchant is surely acceptable. Why would they resort to such things?" He wrote three essays titled *Jing Lun* (Warning Discourse), each of which was thousands of words long, elaborating on the principles of heaven and reason, analyzing and refuting heterodox teachings, correcting and guiding people's hearts, and supporting and upholding the world's moral teachings. The first essay briefly states: "Ancient scholars said: 'Heaven is the principle.' When speaking of its physical form, it is called heaven; when speaking of its sovereign role, it is called the emperor. The emperor is heaven, and heaven is the emperor. It is not another heaven above the blue sky. The palaces and regalia, with their ceremonial robes and jade pendants, like the emperors and kings of the world, are the views of Buddhism and Daoism. Not only this, but there are also the three heavens, nine heavens, and thirty-three heavens; the three emperors, nine emperors, and emperors in all directions. Why so many heavens and so many emperors? Therefore, heaven is not free from the appearance of a hierarchy, and emperors are not free from territorial disputes. Some even regard Zhang Daoling of Han as the Heavenly Master. How can heaven have a teacher? Regard Lin, a woman from Song, as the Heavenly Consort. Does heaven really have a consort? Heaven is the source of principles. Sages follow the example of heaven. Even if Zhang Daoling is a sage, he is still a human ghost. If heaven were to regard him as a teacher, it would mean that heaven is inferior to Zhang Daoling. Lin, after death, is merely a wandering spirit."

If Heaven were to make her a consort, it would mean that Heaven still has desires that it has not forgotten. How could it be called Heaven? They claim that the Daoist Master is superior to Heaven, and they dare not directly call him an emperor, but instead call him a Master, to show respect to Heaven. They do not know that this is not the case, and it is actually disrespectful to Heaven. They say that Lin is a heavenly maiden, and they dare not call her a ghost, but instead call her a consort, to show respect to Heaven. They do not know that this is not the case, and it is actually to defame Heaven. To defame and disrespect Heaven, the crime is unforgivable. They also say: "People in the world only know the Heaven in the sky, so they see the light of the sun, moon, stars, and the manifestations of wind, rain, frost, and dew. Good and bad fortune are the work of Heaven, and disaster and blessing are bestowed by Heaven. This is true. But they do not know that there is also a Heaven within themselves, and one's own Heaven is the same as Heaven. Therefore, the brilliant red gates are the ruler of Heaven, and the clear and serene mind is the emperor of Heaven.

The Three Bonds and Five Constants shine brightly and clearly, are they not like the light of the sun, moon, and stars? Rituals, music, and legal systems are clear and upright, are they not like the teachings of wind, rain, frost, and dew? When one's earthly ruler conflicts with the heavenly mandate, calamity and misfortune are sure to follow; when the heavenly ruler harmonizes with one's earthly ruler, blessings and prosperity will also come. The wise believe this, while the foolish are confused. Stubborn and ignorant people claim that heaven does not hear, committing evil without restraint, yet the moral conscience of their hearts certainly does hear; those who take advantage of luck or chance claim that heaven can be flattered, engaging in excessive and improper worship, yet the moral ruler of their hearts has already rejected them. Ordinary and ignorant people claim that the moral ruler can be deceived, falsely claiming and deceiving. Those who are ordinarily ignorant point to heaven and say it can be relied upon; those who are usually foolish complain to heaven and say it is unaware. Every night they burn incense, yet there are many who cannot confess their wrongdoings; they eat vegetarian food all year long, yet those who know better but repeatedly violate it are numerous. His arguments are close in wording but far-reaching in meaning, like this.

On the sixth day of the first lunar month in the year Ding You of the Zhizheng era (1357), I suddenly fell ill, and the condition worsened over several days. The students followed custom and privately prayed for me. Reflecting and knowing, I taught them, saying: "Though you call yourselves readers, you have not fully grasped the truth. Can ghosts and gods be privately appeased with food and drink? Can human life be bought with paper money? Whom am I deceiving? Am I deceiving Heaven?" That night, I passed away. Only my chest was slightly warm, so they dared not embalm me. The students guarded me around the clock for seven days and nights. They felt my pulse moving and waited. They saw my breath coming out in great gulps. They quickly mashed ginger juice and gave it to me. After a long time, my eyes opened, and by dawn, my breathing continued. Ten days later, I could speak again. Then I called my disciples and told them: "The greatness of the two teachings, the prominence of ghosts and gods, is indeed profound! In the past, my narrow view led me to criticize Buddhism and Daoism too harshly. Now I have lost my position and reduced my salary, nearly unable to survive. You must remember this, my disciples."

The disciples asked for the details, and Sīmíng replied, "The sage does not speak of strange matters, which is true. Yet it is also necessary for you to know that the principle of retribution is not empty. When I was gravely ill, I saw two flies fall before my bed. Upon looking, they had already transformed into people. One wore a blue robe, a yellow cap, and a red headband. He bowed to me and said, 'We have come to summon you.' I asked, 'Who summoned me?' He replied, 'The Inner Court.' I said, 'In this time of chaos and broken roads, how can I go? Moreover, I have no friend in the court.' He said, 'It is the Inner Court of Fēngdū.' I said, 'As a Confucian scholar, I do not know what is meant by the Inner Court of Fēngdū.' The man became angry and put me into a bag, which was like a fishing net, made of fine ropes. I sat inside the bag as two men carried it through the treetops like they were flying. I could feel the tree branches brushing against the bag, making a rustling sound. Then we entered a vast, misty expanse, boundless and endless, with no shore in sight. The waves were surging, and a foul wind blew upon me. The man with the yellow cap carried the bag as if walking on solid ground, and I did not feel any discomfort. After half a day, a path appeared, and I was finally removed from the bag. We passed a place that seemed like a checkpoint. The guards had high noses, deep eyes, curly hair, and beards, resembling Huihui people. I asked the man with the yellow cap, 'What seal?' He replied, 'The red seal.'

There were two men in black robes who led a man and three women to come. The gatekeeper asked again, "What seal?" The black-robed men replied, "A black seal." The gatekeeper said, "One must be very careful. Please look at them." Each produced a tablet, about one and a half inches long and an inch wide, one with a red character and one with a black character, both of which were unrecognizable. The gatekeeper said, "This is correct." He then allowed them to pass through the gate. The yellow-hatted man walked with me along the left corridor, while the other went along the right corridor. I asked, "What place is this?" He replied, "It is the First Gate of Fengdu." At that moment, I realized I was dead. I asked again, "Why are there both red and black seals on the tablets?" He said, "In the underworld, those who are temporarily brought and then returned are marked with red, while those who never return are marked with black." Unconsciously, I exclaimed, "Does that mean I will be reborn?" The yellow-hatted man said, "Even if you are to be reborn, it will be very complicated." Seeing that he seemed to have some affection for me, I asked him to help me, saying, "This journey of mine depends entirely on your two gentlemen’s assistance." The yellow-hatted man replied, "There is a master in charge; how could I do anything?" After walking several miles, we entered the Iron Encircled City. The gatekeeper at the city gate asked questions as before, but with greater intensity. Soon, we arrived at the Hall of the Throne. The yellow-hatted man said, "Although you have no serious crime, the laws of the underworld are still strict, unlike in the world of the living." He unbound the rope around my neck and led me in. First, we passed through the Office of Hats and Clothing, where the official ordered me to remove my clothes and headgear, saying, "They will be sent to your own room for storage." I was left in short clothes and a prisoner’s head covering, still bound with a rope, and walked on.

Upon reaching the ceremonial gate, a man clad in a yellow turban went ahead. Shortly thereafter, he led out five or six others, who seized me and brought me inside, where I knelt at the steps below. The venerable figure before me was dressed in regal attire, and was accompanied by a large entourage of attendants. He asked me, "Are you not the Confucian scholar He Siming from Quzhou?"
