I replied, "Yes, I am." The venerable one said, "What is valued in a scholar is to look upward to the boundless void, to follow the wisdom of the sages, and to explore the principles of nature. He opens heaven and earth, attains the subtle and profound; refines the pure and refined, and channels the primal harmony. He explores the hidden forms within the void, and grasps the root of the movement and stillness of yin and yang. He takes deep silence and clarity as his essence, and sudden transformation as his function. He moves without fixed form, unifying the three into one — this is what is called a scholar, and even ghosts and spirits cannot fathom him. Yet now you stubbornly cling to your own views, composing words and phrases that slander immortals and mock Daoism and Buddhism. Heaven is the greatest, yet you compare it to social hierarchy; the emperor is the most exalted, yet you treat him as a fragmented power. You recklessly discuss the title of Celestial Master, and recklessly debate the name of the Heavenly Consort. Your guilt is great indeed."

In Confucian texts, the concept of "heaven" is discussed in various ways. For instance, the *Spring and Autumn Annals* refer to it as "heaven's king," while the *Book of Poetry* calls it "the sister of heaven" and "the child of heaven." If all these references were to align with your interpretation, heaven would have neither a teacher nor a consort, and how could there be a king, a sister, or a son? Your learning is indeed confined and not thorough, obstructed and hindered. Being confined means being limited to one tool, being obstructed means being stuck in one corner. Not being thorough leads to narrow-mindedness, and being obstructed leads to narrow and biased thinking. Truly, vulgar, corrupt, and absurd people, how can they claim to be Confucian scholars? The provincial governor ordered the retrieval of the register of the surname He, marked my name with a red pen, and then annotated it, finishing the task. The provincial governor said, "You should originally be a sixth-rank official, entering and leaving important places. Because you do not believe in gods and Buddhas, and deceive ghosts and spirits, you are specially demoted to a seventh-rank official." I bowed and thanked, and requested to correct my mistakes. The provincial governor said, "This person pretends to agree but disapproves inwardly, and speaks ill of others behind their backs. He should be made to see the prison, to convince his heart." Several soldiers pulled me down and handed me over to a man in yellow robes to take to the provincial office. There was a treasure pagoda, a monk standing beside it, incense, candles, banners, and streamers arranged brightly. The man in yellow robes bowed twice, and I also bowed. The monk opened the pagoda and took out a large pearl, placed it in a golden tray, and the man in yellow robes held it with both hands as we proceeded, all in a dim and dark place. I asked, "Who is the monk?" He said, "He is the Guiding to the Underworld monk." I asked, "What is the purpose of the pearl?" He said, "It is the wish-fulfilling pearl of the Bodhisattva Ksitigarbha. In the prison, the karma is heavy, and it relies on the light of the pearl to dispel it. Otherwise, the ghost king would eat the hearts and livers of people in the dark, and they would not be able to leave."

Then he first created a prison, called the "Prison for Investigating and Punishing Injustice." He built a long trough with bricks and filled it with piles of charcoal. The flames above were brightly red. He called the criminals to kneel by the trough, took out an iron bar from the fire, as big as a finger, and pierced it into their eyes, piercing through more than ten times and hanging them up, like hanging dried fish. The Yellow Turban said, "This man in his lifetime did not respect his brothers, treating them like distant relatives, and disrespected the great bond of kinship, valuing only wealth and profit, and thus received this punishment." The next prison was called the "Prison for Investigating and Punishing Discord." All were women, mixed with old and young. Each had a hook hanging from their tongue, with a round stone as big as a watermelon hanging from the hook, continuously spinning. The tongue protruded more than a foot, and the pain was unbearable. The southeastern prison was slightly larger, called the Yama's Total Prison. All sorts of people, including scholars, commoners, and various mixed types, were all inside, and no one else was allowed to enter. North of the total prison was a place called "Tik Lou," where people were tied to pillars and carved with a knife like a straw cloak. A small fan was held to fan them, moving slightly. Hot vinegar was poured over them, and they died and then revived. Still, water was poured over them, and the flesh remained as before. This was done more than ten times. Those who are extremely wicked and cruel to the good are punished here.

The hellish realm of Lin Tikou, known as the "filthy latrine" (秽溷), is a place of extreme filth and degradation. The entire realm is a vast pit of human excrement, boiling like soup, with an unbearable stench. Demons use long spears to skewer and boil the condemned, who are plunged into the boiling excrement. They emerge, only to be immediately disintegrated and transformed into maggots. The demons then scoop the maggots from the pot with bamboo baskets, finely fry them, and the maggots turn to ash. The demons sprinkle the excrement on the ash, and the condemned are restored to their original form, a cycle that repeats itself many times. I asked, "What is the purpose of this punishment?" Huangjin replied, "This is the place where the wicked of the world, those who slander and defame the virtuous, are punished." He then said, "There is no need to visit all the realms. Let me take you to see another one!" We left the "filthy latrine" and walked about a hundred steps before entering another gate, which bore the inscription "Punishment for Corruption and Embezzlement." This was another hellish realm. Several naked individuals were chained to the ground, and several ferocious-looking night demons dragged eight or nine famished ghosts towards them. The demons drew their swords and cut flesh from the naked ones' chests and thighs, placing the meat in a pot to cook and feed the famished ghosts. After devouring the meat, the ghosts were cut again until only bones remained. After a short while, the karmic wind blew, and the bodies of the tortured were restored to their original form. There were also iron snakes and copper dogs that bit into the flesh and marrow of the tortured, causing them to cry out in pain, their voices shaking the earth. All of these were high-ranking officials of the world, who had accepted bribes, deceived the world, and stolen fame. Some pretended to be廉洁 while secretly accepting bribes, while others used their official power to interfere in public affairs. All those who deceived others for personal gain were punished in this realm. There were also one or two who were familiar to me.

After viewing, I returned to the Office of Industry and Commerce, handed over the pearls to the monk, and went to the platform to report. The respected official on the platform gave further instruction: "Now you should correct your mistakes and not repeat past errors. If you do not reform, you will be beyond forgiveness." Then he ordered the Yellow Cap to escort me back, and only then could I leave to seek a wandering life. I went to the Office of Robes and Hats to get my clothes. The Yellow Cap said, "The official will wait for you here. We will go to fetch the document and escort you." After a meal's time, they returned and said, "We are taking a shortcut; we will not be following the old road." We traveled together, passing through several gates. One of the gates was newly built, and its sign read "Mayfly." The gatekeeper, knowing I was a scholar, asked me to compose an inscription for the Mayfly Gate. I asked for the meaning of the name, and he said, "All spirits who are reborn in this world pass through here, but they do not stay long, just like the mayfly that lives for only a day." I composed a few lines in response, and the inscription read: "There is a grand gate, stabilizing the thick earth. There is a majestic presence, the gatekeeper. Named 'Mayfly,' it takes its name from its meaning. All beings who are born, depart from here. They do not stay long, soon returning. What is different about this insect, which dies in a day? In the Southern Jambudvipa, time passes quickly. Coming and going, why not rest a little? Please look at this name and understand the analogy. The six realms and four births, escape them early. Wander freely without bounds, attain Tavatimsa. If everyone becomes a celestial being, the gate can be abolished. Hear my inscription, and make a great vow. O spirits, guard and uphold this truth."

The gatekeeper was pleased and let me go. By the second watch of the night, I arrived home. I saw myself lying on the ground, the lamp lighting my head, while my wife, children, and disciples were weeping and crying. Suddenly, a person in a yellow hat pushed me, and I didn't realize I had fallen into the body, waking up suddenly. Later, Si Ming indeed became the magistrate. Wherever he went, he governed with integrity and caution, having no flaws, and was called clean and upright. It seems he was warned in some way. Records of the Two Rivers Prefecture Ji Fucheng of Jingkou was a descendant of Tang Jiwen. During the Jianyan period of the Song dynasty, there was a man named Shen who was appointed as the military governor of Jintan and thus settled there. His descendants have lived in Jintan for generations, being wealthy and influential in their hometown. People called him the Ji Half-State family. Fucheng was born with an unusual appearance, having a double pupil in one eye. He befriended Zhao Defu and Jiang Yanyi, wealthy men of Piling, and they were inseparable friends. Fucheng was bold and generous, brave in doing what was right. The three of them once carried a large sum of money and traded between Fujian and Zhejiang. At that time, Jiang Qiuniang and Tao Yuxiao, courtesans of Wulin, were renowned for their singing and dancing. Zhao Defu and Jiang Yanyi were very close to them, and Fucheng repeatedly tried to stop them, but they continued as before. Only two years later, their bags were empty. They decided to return, prepared again, and set out, buying laughter and entertainment, spending money freely. Another year passed, and they were all spent.

The two privately discussed, sold all their property, and loaded up to go to Wulin. Ignoring their doors, the old and the young, Fu Qing was concerned about it, but he could not persuade them with a hundred analogies. Angered, he went to Fujian, set up a banquet to say goodbye, and at the banquet, earnestly advised, saying: "Since we are close friends, how can I remain silent? Admonitions like medicine and stones, the duty of a friend. Even if my words are light and my person insignificant, I cannot move them. Do the two of you not consider your wives and children?" Then they feigned agreement, saying: "Brother, your words are right; we now know what to be cautious of." Fu Qing settled in Fuzhou, his livelihood going well. After three autumns, he finally returned. When passing by Qiantang, he first visited the two, meeting them on the road. Their appearance was haggard, their clothes ragged, almost unrecognizable. Holding hands on the side of the road, unable to hold back tears, Fu Qing immediately pulled them to the boat, changed them into fine clothes, and had them drink strong wine. Comforted and praised repeatedly, showing both emotion and courtesy. The two wept, saying: "We only did not follow your advice, so we ended up like this, but it's too late to regret. We regret that the courtesans are so heartless, squandering our wealth of ten thousand gold coins. Yesterday, when we passed their door, they acted as if they didn't recognize us and ordered us to leave. Afraid it would bring shame to themselves, they must kill us to end it." Fu Qing explained, saying: "You two have always roamed the flower streets and willow alleys, how could you not know their ways? Why should you be angry? Human life is precious, do not rashly harbor evil thoughts. Just pack up and return home."

If one needs capital, it can be provided here comprehensively. The ancients said that friends should share resources. If one only indulges in drinking and leisurely pursuits, neglecting each other in times of poverty and hardship, even dogs and pigs would not eat their flesh—how can they still be called human?" Thus, each lent them twenty thousand. The two, carrying what they had gained, passed by the courtesan's home again. The courtesan, seeing their clothes and hats neat and their appearance radiant, was quite surprised but treated them as before. Fu Qing urged them to return, but the two lied, saying, "Let me tidy up a bit and wait a few moments. In case there is any trouble, it's better to leave early." Fu Qing said, "Ah, what are you saying! If I leave, you surely cannot move. Even a month or two, you must wait, how could I abandon you?" Soon after, Yan Yi fell ill and lay in the courtesan's home. De Fu visited daily to support him, but also contracted the same illness. Within a few days, they died one after another. Fu Qing went to mourn them with deep sorrow, dressing them in fine silk and placing them in a lacquered coffin, all according to proper rites. He still slaughtered a sheep, poured wine, and set up an offering, temporarily burying them in the Lingyin Temple's monk's quarters. When it was time to set sail, he again brought wine and food to pay respects, composing a poem to mourn them, which reads: "Bound by lifelong friendship, I dare not betray. A cup of wine, double tears flow. May the wandering soul go with the old friend. Do not blame the east wind for the cuckoo's cry."

The fleeting moments of life pass like a shuttle, wasting gold to buy laughter and songs. Do not think of the broken rain and scattered clouds anymore, and together on the lotus seat, worship Amitabha. The moonlit nights and spring flowers in the leisurely brothels, the cool breeze and bright moon in the temple rooms. To know the sorrowful things of this world, it is all like a dream of the South柯. Two famous flowers with particularly charming colors, sorrowful, watching the flower-viewer depart far away. It is like a willow tree on Chang'an Street, dancing the long branches in someone else's hand. The path to the springs is vast, separating life and death, earning a reputation for wandering the rivers and lakes. The neighbors fear to hear the wife and children cry, this sound breaks all human hearts. Tired from dancing and songs, yet unwilling to stop, splendor does not stay for the young. If only I had known there would be no place to bury the white bones, cherish gold to buy a mound of earth. After offering wine, untie the rope. After returning home for a month, immediately went to Pizhou to see the wife and children, explaining the cause of death, detailing the funeral arrangements. He gave four thousand strings of cash to the two families, entrusting his relatives to manage the affairs, ensuring they were not left destitute. He consoled them, saying, "My honorable husband's remains will be taken back to your noble hometown after I pass through Hangzhou, seeking a blessed place for burial. Do not worry." Later, Fu Qing successfully traded in Zhejiang, making tenfold profits. He personally visited Lingyin Temple, opening the coffin, placing the remains in a small wooden box, and bringing them back to buy land in Wuxi for burial. All expenses were covered by Fu Qing. He also invited monks to hold a three-day and three-night water and land feast to offer blessings to the dead, and his noble character and high friendship spread throughout the rivers and lakes.

Suddenly, at the end of the Yuan dynasty, chaos erupted, and people were all agitated. Fu Qing had no plan, so he sat silently at home. Suddenly, Dufu and Yan Yi came together, and Fu Qing forgot his death, meeting them with joy. Yan Yi said, "Why are you deeply contemplating while living in peace, as if you have a great worry?" Fu Qing told him the reason. The two replied together, "It's not a problem. We have already requested a command from heaven, ordering the spirits to protect your family." After speaking, they disappeared, and Fu Qing realized they were dead. From then on, although Fu Qing's household was often in the midst of military conflicts, they rarely encountered fear, and they lived peacefully as usual. In the year Jiyou of the Hongwu era, he lived to be eighty-one and died without illness. Two years later, in the year Renzi, Xu Jianyin, who was from the same county and served as the magistrate of Cangxi in Sichuan, saw flags and armored horses in the mountains, with over a hundred followers, and the scene was very impressive. He thought they were officials from a higher rank, so he stood by the road, waiting for them to pass. When they arrived, it was Fu Qing. Fu Qing looked at Xu and said, "I heard you in Oh Song in this town, and I have wanted to see you for a long time." Then he dismounted and talked with Xu, asking him in great detail about the affairs of the village and his family. Xu was related by marriage to Fu Qing, so he bowed twice and asked, "Uncle-in-law, since you passed away, the mourning period is over. How can it be like this?" Fu Qing said, "Heaven has appointed me as the head of the court of jurisdiction in the two Sichuans because I have some merit. My duties are respected, and all the local deities of Sichuan, as well as the deities not included in the ritual calendar, are subject to my control. The ancient buildings in the village ahead are under my jurisdiction. I have four judges under my command, but two are still missing. I have already recommended Dufu and Yan Yi, and they will arrive soon. You should repair the temple for me, and I will bless the country and its people."

As for you, young man, just arrived in office, if not for my unseen assistance, how could you achieve fame?” Xu bowed his hands in respect and asked for guidance. Fu Qing replied, “Integrity and forgiveness are the two essential virtues. Only integrity can govern oneself, and only forgiveness can approach the people. Integrity nurtures the heart, and forgiveness makes the people easier to befriend. When the people are befriended, transformation follows, and all tasks are completed.” After speaking, he rode away on horseback, as swift as flying. Xu was bewildered, and as he proceeded to a village, he indeed found an old shrine standing on a mountain peak. He inquired of the village elders, who said, “This is the shrine of the Right Minister Gongfu, which has been in ruins for many years. Recently, there have been occasional sightings of someone riding with attendants, entering and exiting within it. It is quite famous for its spiritual influence. We old men plan to renovate its structure, but work has not yet begun.” Xu, the magistrate, was pleased upon hearing this and informed them about his encounter with Fu Qing. He immediately urged them to complete the renovation and also helped with the expenses, entrusting the county official Zou Zhong to supervise the work. Shortly after, the renovation was completed, and the old name was retained. A statue of Fu Qing was placed in the main hall, and statues of De Fu and Yan Yi were placed in the eastern and western wings. Someone was sent to Kuizhou to seek an inscription from Governor Sheng Nangjin, which was carved on a stele to chronicle his deeds. Therefore, his authority and benevolence greatly increased, and his benefits became widely known. People from distant places, facing floods, droughts, diseases, and epidemics, found that their prayers were answered immediately.

After Xu completed his term, he passed by his home on the way, visiting the two sons of Fu Qing, Yuanli and Yuanxin, and first mentioned this matter. Yuanli said, "My brothers had a dream where two people told us that we were honored by your esteemed father to be appointed as the judges of the two provinces' administrative court, and we were to set out the next day, to respectfully visit and bid farewell. Recently, there was someone who came from Piling who could speak about his family, and he also had a similar dream, but none understood what it meant. Now hearing what you said, we realize that our late father was a deity, and for you two, it can be said that we are still bound by ties of life and death." The next year, Xu was reappointed, and he went to the temple to perform rituals. There, the temple was brightly colored and splendid, and at that time, there was a radiance. Offerings of animals, wine, and paper money were made every day of sacrifice, and everywhere, every village, every household, none did not worship with sincere devotion, hoping to receive blessings. Until now, the divine signs are prominent, and the incense and fire never cease.

**The Story of the Twin-Rooted Tree** Shangguan Shouyu, a native of Jiangdu, Yangzhou, served as a teaching official at the Kuizhang Pavilion in Shuntian. He lived near Guo Shitan, a historian and scholar named Jia Xuzhong, who was a close friend of Ke Jingzhong. Jia was skilled in poetry and painting. He owned three ancient zithers named "Qiongyaoyin," "Huanpeiyin," and "Penglaiyin," all of which had been authenticated by Ke Jingzhong. Shouyu also had a refined appreciation for poetry and music, particularly the green qin, and he had a particularly close friendship with Jia. Whenever they had leisure time, they would gather to drink, play music, and enjoy poetry and chess, spending the entire day in pleasant conversation. Jia had no male heir, only three daughters. He once said, "My three daughters can be compared to these three zithers." He then named his daughters after the zithers. Shouyu's son, Cui, was exceptionally intelligent and talented. When he was born, people gave him a copy of *Tang Wencui*, a collection of Tang Dynasty writings, hence his nickname "Cui Nu." At the age of ten, he was sent to study under Jia. Jia and his wife loved him as if he were their own son, and Jia's three daughters treated him as a brother, calling him "Cui She." He often studied and painted with Jia's youngest daughter, Penglai, and they deeply cared for each other. Jia's wife once teased him, saying, "If Penglai were to marry someone like Cui She in the future, she would be satisfied." She told her husband about this, and Shouyu agreed, saying, "That is exactly my intention." They arranged for a matchmaker to discuss the matter, and both families agreed to the marriage. Cui and Penglai were privately overjoyed. However, unexpectedly, Jia suddenly resigned and returned home, and the marriage plans ultimately failed to materialize.

Three years later, 守愚 was appointed as the Governor of Fuzhou. Upon his arrival, he rented a house occupied by residents and obtained a building with three rooms. Opposite the street was another building, which was particularly elegant. He asked about it, and it turned out to be the house of the family 贾. 守愚 immediately went to the neighborhood, where he learned that 琼瑶 and 环佩 had already been married, while 蓬莱 was still unmarried but had been betrothed to the family 林. 粹 heard about this and was very depressed! Although 蓬莱 had been betrothed to another family by her parents, it was not her wish. Knowing that 粹 had arrived, she wanted to meet him but had no opportunity to do so. She often stood fixedly by the railing of the building, looking at him but unable to speak. One day, 蓬莱 wrapped the chess pieces in a white silk scarf and threw them to 粹. 粹 received and looked at them, and on the top was painted a red peach with a poem written on it: ### 1. **Idiomatic Expressions** - **守愚出为福州治中** - "守愚" is a name, and "治中" is an official title, meaning "Governor of Fuzhou." The phrase indicates that the person named 守愚 was appointed as the Governor of Fuzhou. - **僦居民舍** - "僦" means to rent, and "居民舍" means a house occupied by residents. The phrase means "to rent a house occupied by residents." - **对街一楼，尤清雅** - "对街" means opposite the street, and "尤清雅" means especially elegant or refined. The phrase describes a house opposite the street that is particularly elegant. - **问之，乃贾氏宅也** - "问之" means to ask about it, and "乃贾氏宅也" means it is the house of the family 贾. The phrase indicates that the house is owned by the family 贾. - **即日往坊** - "即日" means immediately, and "往坊" means to go to the neighborhood. The phrase means "to go to the neighborhood immediately." - **琼瑶、环佩已适人** - "琼瑶" and "环佩" are names, and "适人" means to be married. The phrase means that 琼瑶 and 环佩 have already been married. - **惟蓬莱在室，亦许婚林氏矣** - "惟蓬莱" means only 蓬莱, "在室" means unmarried, and "许婚林氏矣" means has been betrothed to the family 林. The phrase means that only 蓬莱 is still unmarried but has been betrothed to the family 林. - **悒怏殊甚** - "悒怏" means depressed or gloomy, and "殊甚" means very much. The phrase means "very depressed." - **彼此时时凝立楼栏** - "彼" means she, "时时" means often, and "凝立楼栏" means to stand fixedly by the railing of the building. The phrase means "she often stands fixedly by the railing of the building." - **相视不能发语** - "相视" means to look at each other, and "不能发语" means unable to speak. The phrase means "unable to speak to each other." - **蓬莱一日以白练帕裹象棋子掷粹** - "蓬莱" is a name, "一日" means one day, "以白练帕裹象棋子" means to wrap the chess pieces in a white silk scarf, and "掷粹" means to throw them to 粹. The phrase means "one day, 蓬莱 wrapped the chess pieces in a white silk scarf and threw them to 粹." - **上画绯桃** - "上画" means on the top is painted, and "绯桃" means red peach. The phrase means "on the top is painted a red peach." - **题一诗曰** - "题一诗" means to write a poem, and "曰" means saying. The phrase means "to write a poem saying." ### 2. **Cultural and Historical Allusions** - **守愚** - A name, likely a personal name of a historical figure or a fictional character. - **福州治中** - An official title, meaning "Governor of Fuzhou," indicating a high-ranking administrative position. - **贾氏宅** - Refers to the house of the family 贾, a common surname in Chinese history. - **琼瑶、环佩、蓬莱

The petals of the flower are red like cinnabar and layered like a peony, having been admired by Liu Chen in the past. It is best to plant it in the clouds of Tian Tai Mountain; do not allow common people to cultivate it. The woman understood the meaning of the flower, but upon reflecting quietly, she realized that its fate was already determined, and there was nothing she could do about it. She also painted a branch of plum blossoms and wrote a poem in response, which said: The white petals of the flower hold the essence of spring, as if made of fine silk, and its integrity remains unchanged through the harshness of winter. Even if it were to become a companion to a fairy, what can be done about the solitary scholar who appreciates it but remains isolated? She tied three tuning pegs of a qin with colored cords, dropped them, and sent them back to Penglai. Upon seeing the phrase "solitary scholar of Solitary Mountain," Penglai understood that it referred to her own alliance with the Lin family, where her true feelings remained unspoken, and she could only feel disheartened. Not long after, it was the Lantern Festival, and the custom in Fujian of setting up many lanterns was particularly grand, attracting large crowds. The woman observed that the household of the Jia family would surely attend, so she secretly waited outside their gate. When the night deepened and the area grew quiet, indeed, several sedan chairs were carried forward by bearers, and Penglai, along with her mother and a few others, entered the sedan chairs, followed by maids and servants, continuing without interruption. The woman followed behind, passing through more than ten streets, and realizing she could not see them, she began to recite poetry by the side of the sedan chair: Fate brought us together in a quiet, fragrant street, and I saw a beautiful and unexpected sight in the shadow of silver lamps. Even the colorful sedan chair seemed distant, like the mythical Penglai Mountain, and the paths of the phoenix and the crane diverged, symbolizing separation or different destinies. Penglai recognized the woman as well and wanted to call out to her and speak of her feelings, but the crowd around her was too chaotic, and she dared not open her mouth. Instead, she softly recited a poem from inside the sedan chair:

* Idiomatic Expressions: Identify idiomatic or archaic expressions that cannot be directly translated word-for-word into English. * Cultural and Historical Allusions: Identify references to historical events, figures, or cultural practices that require contextual explanation or adaptation. * Syntactic Ambiguities: Identify grammatically ambiguous constructions or elliptical passages unique to Classical Chinese syntax. * Proper Nouns: Identify people, places, titles, and canonical terms and determine how to render them consistently.

The Dragon Sword, hidden in the depths of the abyss for a long time, is guarded by the giant spirit during the day and the ghosts at night. Dragons and serpents hide, and spirits flee, as the sword's brilliance pierces the sky and reaches the stars. It breaks through the clouds, unlocks treasures, and is a rare treasure in the world. Its unique appearance and the sound it makes when drawn are awe-inspiring, with its blade polished like oil and its hilt adorned with phoenix motifs. The Dragon Sword shines brightly, recently emerging from its sheath, gracefully moving like a flowing lightning and flying frost, with its reflection in the autumn water and the sharpness of its edge. The hilt is decorated with golden beads and precious jewels. The high official recognizes its excellence, hanging it on a jade belt with a gold seal, and its purple flame illuminates the jade pendants, suggesting a significant event. No one dares to stop it as it departs, disappearing into the unknown. The spirit of the sword is as clear and pure as a white bird's tail swaying in clear ice. The male spirit separates from the female spirit, with the female spirit feeling a deep sorrow, and the male spirit temporarily stays in a dust-filled box, wondering when they will reunite. It will follow the wind and rain to find the female spirit in the Yandang region. Even if it is highly valued, it is not a match for the male spirit. The male spirit's counterpart has been lying in a deep pool of water and clouds, separated by great distance, and the male spirit longs for her. The journey across mountains and valleys is filled with hardship and suffering. The heavens send a descendant of Lei Huan to wear the sword in the vast marshes. The sword is drawn with a resounding sound, causing the waves and tides to rise and the day to become dark. It is realized that divine objects have their counterparts and will not be separated for eternity.

Cui read it and said: "The elegant and refined poetic expressions, without the weak and frail spirit of women and girls, exactly match the elegance and style of Li Bai. How could this be matched with an ordinary and mediocre person?" Soon, a great epidemic broke out in Fujian, and the man discussed by Penglai, Lin, died. Jia's couple knew that Cui was unmarried, so they sent someone to inform Shouyu and seek a match. Shouyu was delighted and agreed. After the six marriage rituals were completed, the wedding was scheduled. On the night of the wedding, when the candles were lit, Cui met Shouyu, and it was as if a deity had descended. They each wrote a poem to celebrate the occasion, on February 8, 1359 in the Yuan Dynasty. Cui's poem said: [To be continued with the actual poem if provided.]

When the crabapple blossoms bloom and the swallows arrive, I pluck the first branch of the east wind. Let us share the pillow of mandarin ducks and fulfill our wish for a close and affectionate relationship; do not write sad or sorrowful letters. Peach blossoms dye the handkerchief, hinting at the arrival of spring; it's not too late to paint the yellow willow leaves. There is no need for a double-knot belt symbolizing unity, for the new person is actually an old acquaintance. The poem of Penglai says: When I meet you, I immediately cherish you; if there is a destined connection, it will eventually come to fruition. The old son-in-law becomes a new son-in-law, and bad karma is transformed into good karma. Under the light of a silver lamp, the gentle gaze is soft, and in front of a jade mirror, the young, tender bamboo shoots are graceful. Heaven has already tied us together with a red thread, and from now on, we will be like lotus flowers growing together. Since entering the household of the official, she has been filial to her in-laws and respectful to her husband. Within and without the family, she is universally praised as virtuous. In her spare time, she engages in poetry and verse with her husband, finds joy in music and painting, and has compiled all her works into a collection titled *Xuexue Gao* (Drafts of Snowflakes), with a preface written by her husband. Many of the poems and prefaces are not recorded here, but a few are included to amuse those who appreciate such things: △ *Gū Yuàn* (Grief of a Woman) Pearl-like dewdrops form round clusters, the ice-like skin and jade bracelet are cold. A solitary swallow roosts on the apricot beam, the square mirror conceals a lonely phoenix. The remaining trees are covered in withered yellow, the round lotus leaves are damp and green. The embroidered chest opens to reveal the colors of a painting, and I look out the window with a heart full of sorrow. △ *Bái Zhù Cí* (Two Poems on White Cotton) The crimson red of the foxglove and the purple sleeves are reflected in the pink wind of the peach blossoms. Slowly singing *Bái Zhù*, holding the jade bell, the sweet voice and fragrant melody linger around the curtains, the dust on the beams falls like clouds, and the air is filled with a sense of emptiness. The gentle gaze returns, and the eyebrows are swept with black ink, the lingering sound remains. The song should be listened to carefully, and the cup should be filled again, for the green hair and red face can last for a long time.

### 春晓曲 The sound is as clear as jade striking a jade disc, and the movements are graceful like a brocade sleeve on a red carpet. A swirling wind and flying snow are rare in this world, and the quick, agile turns are as swift as a dancer. Fine dust scatters, and the banquet is quiet, with a dimly lit gauzy lamp. The delicate fabric is wet with tears, as if stained by rouge. △Spring Morning Song ### 秋夜曲 The thin ice reflection in the fragrant pond, and the sweet, delicate bird songs in the curved veranda. The cold, red silk cushion in front of the mirror, and the fading of her eyebrows and green makeup. Her charming appearance, like a young flower blooming, and her deep, tender thoughts, expressed through the imagery of soft, delicate branches. Delicate fingers collect dew from flowers, and she lightly mixes snow with powder. △Autumn Night Song ### 咏蝶 The dew on the fragrant orchid is heavy, and a cool breeze moves through the gauzy curtain. A wooden box containing fragrant silk, and a quilted bedcover, asking who will share it? The shadows of fireflies pass through a sparse curtain, and the smoke from a furnace shaped like an animal rises gently. The extinguishing of a silver lamp, and she removes a green flower-shaped ornament. △Song of Butterflies ### 谢大姊惠鞋 The delicate wings are covered with fragrant powder, and new clothes are dyed with a charming yellow. Who can match such grace? They perch together in the flower room. △Song of Receiving Shoes from Elder Sister ### 咏并蒂荔枝 Lotus petals are sent far away, and the brocade robe still carries the scent of her fingers. The bow-shaped object is so well-fitted that it leaves no marks, and she stands alone in the shade of flowers, watching the formation of geese. △Song of Twin-Lipped Litchi ### 园中咏菜 Plants grow with connected stems, and the divine order is evident. A woman in a secluded room, weary and pale, sees something that evokes strong emotions. △Song of Vegetables in the Garden 满圃绿纤纤，芳苗雨后添。惟应穷措大，咬得寸根甜。

Cui was renowned for his talent, and officials in power had plans to recommend him. Penglai, however, strongly advised against it, saying, "Now the roads are blocked by dust and thorns, and looking toward the capital is like looking at the sky. How can you abandon the care of your parents to pursue the path of fame and achievement? Haven't you heard the words of Wang Ruzhong's wife, who said, 'The high status of Lihufu, how does it compare to the virtue of staying with your family?'" Cui heeded her advice and had no intention of leaving. He declined the opportunity, citing the need to care for his elderly parents. The following year, the magistrate died. Another year later, in the year 1352 of the Yuan dynasty, the city of Fujian was seized by bandits. The city's prominent families fled to remote mountains and valleys, and Cui also took his family to hide. The bandits tracked them down and massacred the entire family. They spared one person, named Penglai, intending to make her their wife. Penglai knew she could not escape and deceived the bandit, saying, "My entire family is dead, and I have nowhere to go. Even if you spare me, how can I live? I wish to serve you for life. Please allow me to bury my deceased husband, and then I will follow you." The bandit leader was pleased and agreed. They went to the place where Cui's body lay. The bandit leader pulled out his dagger and began digging a pit. When the pit was finished, he placed the dagger on the ground and sat beside it, saying, "I am tired! I am tired!" He looked at Penglai and instructed her to take the knife and cover the grave. Penglai immediately raised the knife and cut her throat, saying, "Die together, and there will be no regret." The bandit leader quickly stood up to seize the knife, but she had already choked to death. Angered, he said, "You may die, but I will not let you die together with your family." He then buried Penglai twenty steps away, making sure the two graves faced each other.

In that year, Yan Zhihua became the Governor of Fujian Province. He gathered the militia from various counties to pacify the city, and the people were just beginning to resume their livelihoods. After several more years, those who had fled from the bandits began to fully recount the events of the Penglai incident. The Governor sent someone to inspect the site, intending to rebury the remains according to ritual. Upon arrival, they found that on top of the two graves, each had grown a tree facing one another, with branches and trunks intertwined and inseparable. The messenger returned to report this, and the Governor personally went to inspect the site, confirming that it was indeed true. He then decided not to disturb the graves but instead repaired them and offered sacrifices. The people called these trees the "Twin Trunk Tomb Trees," and the people of Fujian continue to speak of them to this day.

Meeting Record of Tian Shu and Xue Tao** Tian Shu of Wuyang, courtesy name Mengyi, accompanied his father Baolu to serve as a teaching official in Chengdu, Sichuan, in the fourth month of the year 1384 (the 17th year of the Hongwu era). Tian Shu was clear and elegant in appearance, knowledgeable in calligraphy, painting, music, and chess. His classmates often played with him and loved him more than their own blood. He had visited and appreciated famous mountains and scenic spots far and near. He once said, "All my life I have been lazy about seeking fame and profit; as long as I can find good places to climb and look around, that is enough!" The following autumn, Baolu was planning to send him back, but Tian Shu's mother did not want to let him go. She said, "He has only been here for a short time; why should we make him leave? Moreover, the official position is low-paying and the travel expenses are difficult. You should think about it again." Baolu then consulted with his close friends among the students and decided to set up a private school in someone's home. One reason was that Tian Shu could continue his studies and advance his learning, and another was to use the salary to plan his return. The students were very happy that Tian Shu would stay and recommended him to a prominent family near the city, the Zhang family. In the following year, on the 18th day of the first month of the third year of the Ming dynasty, he set up his teaching post. Friends from the school and academy all sent him off. The Zhang family was very pleased and held a banquet, treating him as the highest guest. They also told Baolu, "Your son may stay overnight and can stay in the dormitory." Baolu agreed.

On the morning of the second month when flowers were blooming, Zou returned home after leaving his study. He happened to pass by a secluded and quiet place, where below the mountain were all peach trees, and the flowers were just in full bloom. Zou loved the place and stood there for a while, wandering around. Suddenly, he saw a beautiful woman standing by the flowers in the peach grove. Zou dared not look back and left. Afterward, whenever he passed by, the woman was always at the gate. One day, Zou passed by and accidentally left behind the salary he had received. The woman ordered a maid to pick it up and return it to Zou, who was grateful. The next day, he went to thank her. When he arrived at the gate, a maid went in to report, saying, "The gentleman who lost his gold has come!" She invited him to enter the inner hall, and the woman came out to meet him, smiling and asking, "Are you not the guest teacher at Zhang's mansion?" Zou replied, "Yes," and thanked her for returning the gold.

The beautiful woman said, "The Zhang family is my relatives, and that tutor is the same as mine, so why should we thank each other?" Zhu rose and bowed, saying, "May I ask, what is your family name and social status, and what relation do you have with my master?" The woman replied, "This is the Ping family, an old clan from Chengdu. I am the daughter of the Xue family from the Wenxiao坊, and I married the youngest son of the Ping family, named Kang. Unfortunately, he passed away early, and I remain a widow." They sat for a long time, and tea was served twice. Zhu excused himself to leave, but the woman invited him to stay, saying, "Tonight, please stay at my humble abode. If my master knows you have come here, and I cannot offer a single piece of entertainment, I feel extremely ashamed and uneasy!" She immediately set out wine and food, arranged two seats, and sat with Zhu in pairs. During their conversation, they encouraged and responded to each other's toasts to the fullest extent, and their talk was mixed with jokes and humor. Zhu, considering his connection to the Zhang family, did not dare to let himself go. The woman said, "I have heard that you are a talented and free-spirited person, skilled in writing poetry. Why be so pedantic like a scholar? I may not be very clever, but I also understand poetry. Now that I have met someone who appreciates my art, and we share the rare and perfect understanding of a 'high mountain and flowing water,' why not perform one piece?" She then showed Zhu all the authentic works of poetry and verse from her collection, particularly those of Yuan Zhen, Du Mu, and Gao Pian, which were all bright and fresh as if new. Zhu could not put them down.

The beautiful lady waved her servant to remove the old dishes and bring out new delicacies. Among them were many exotic flavors, which could not be recognized. She took a glass cup and poured wine for Zhu. Zhu spontaneously composed a poem saying: The path leads into a small paradise, where amidst the scattered red petals, I meet a graceful maiden. King Xiang of Chu mistook it for a dream of Gaotang, but it is not the fairy of the cloud and rain on the Yangtai. The beautiful lady said, "It is good, but it is too short and lacks depth to fully express joy; how about using 'fallen flowers' as a theme and composing a joint poem?" Zhu replied, "I will follow your instruction." The beautiful lady sang saying: The beauty of youth is hard to hold back, and the bloom of spring is easily withered. The red flowers on the steps still charm, and the white petals on the ground remain delicate. They fall quickly like bidding farewell to the tree, and they fly slowly as if reluctant to leave the branch. Moss spreads like newly embroidered brocade, and grass layers like skillfully cut silk. The beautiful nature grieves before it perishes, and the fragrant spirit cannot be summoned back. Swallows carry home the old nest, and butterflies chase each other over the precarious bridge. They stick to the pages about to dry the dew, and rush through the curtains with sudden gusts of wind. Even in sunny weather, they still have a demeanor, but after rain, they become even more listless. Bees chase low and mixed with fluff, and fish swallow fine and mixed with water lettuce. Graceful, like pearl shoes stepping, and scattered, like green jade hairpins floating. Birds passing cause sorrowful touches, and children playing fear shaking. Fading flowers float on the rain-filled stream, and withered petals ripple in the wind-blown tide.

Teaching children to sweep along the path (Zhu), entrusting the water to float downstream (Xue). Charming people touch the golden zither (Zhu), brewing tea into the poet's gourd (Xue). Beautiful appearance falling before the tower (Zhu), cold demeanor disappearing in a dream (Xue). Fragrant garden once used as a seat (Zhu), long road sometimes chasing the reins (Xue). Silk fan lady hiding the petals (Zhu), bamboo fence servant guarding the young plants (Xue). Picking and finishing with a casual gesture (Zhu), belt near the ring of fire (Xue). Muddy and still sad (Zhu), empty bottle even more lonely (Xue). Dense leaves creating a thick shade (Zhu), dense stems with plentiful seeds (Xue). Why necessarily divide the mat and the toilet (Zhu), would rather think of grinding nitre (Xue). Fragrant remainder, why be stingy and steal (Zhu), unfasten the pendant, do not bother to invite (Xue). Seductive appearance suitable for the palace forehead (Zhu), foolish affection jealous of the dancer's waist (Xue). Dressing table, do not casually wipe (Zhu), stay with a pitiful night (Xue).

Linking, the beauty took out a small note and wrote on it, then finished writing. By the second watch of the night, she invited him into her chamber, offering him her pillow and mat, and they shared a deep and intimate pleasure. Beside the pillow, she earnestly warned Zhu, saying: "Be careful not to speak of this lightly. If the patron were to learn of it, both of us would lose our reputations." The next day, she gave Zhu a jade paperweight shaped like a sleeping lion, and as she saw him to the door, she said: "Come again only when there is no business; do not imitate the unfaithful." Zhu then deceived the patron by saying: "My mother misses me deeply, and she will surely have me return home to stay the night; I dare not remain here." The patron believed him, and from then on, Zhu often stayed at the beauty’s residence. For over half a year, no one knew of this. They only enjoyed flowers and the moon, raised their cups of wine, played the zither, and fully experienced all the pleasures of the world.

One night, the man named Zhu and the woman discussed poetry. The woman said, "Tang people enjoyed writing rebus poetry, but it is rarely seen in recent times." Zhu replied, "Only you, with your gentle emotions and deep thoughts, can do it in a casual and playful manner. As for me, I am rustic and dull, unable to find words." The woman smiled and said, "Please try to set a topic, so I can learn from you!" Zhu quickly suggested, "How about a poem about the four seasons?" The woman immediately began to write the poem: Tender flowers lean against the steps, willow branches sway gently in the wind. The sunset shines brightly over half the hill, the moon rises over a lone village with a solitary pine tree. The coolness of the green mat brings a chill, the refreshing coolness of spring water from a summer well. The scent of incense rises in thin, clear threads, the bright, round moonlight through the paper window. Snow covers the reeds in autumn, the wind through the willows and the twilight landscape. A lonely lamp and a traveler's dream in an empty building, a solitary goose carrying a letter to a distant homeland. The cold weather and the closing of doors in the morning, the snow and cold wind in the night, with the city gates closed. The warmth of a red charcoal fire around a stove, the clear, light green tea in a porcelain cup. Zhu read the poem and was impressed by the woman's quick-witted and ingenious nature, preparing to write his own response. The woman modestly acknowledged the value of the man's response, using a metaphor to express her gratitude. Zhu praised the woman's poem and expressed the difficulty in matching its quality, then wrote his own four lines of poetry in response.

A fragrant tree blooms with red flowers after rain, and white catkins fly into the curtains, startled by the wind. Yellow adds to the morning color as spring willows stretch out, and powdered petals fall, leaving a fragrant snow covering the pines. Melons float in a jar of cool water to relieve summer heat, and lotus roots are layered on a plate with ice, suggesting a cold, refreshing dish. Slanted stones near the steps, with dense bamboo shoots growing through them, and in a small pond, leaves stretch out, and lotus flowers emerge in clusters. The setting sun with its red glow, contrasting with the red leaves covered in frost, and thin smoke envelops the trees, giving the evening forest an ashen hue. A phoenix letter carries sorrow, too ashamed to seal the tears, and in a dream of a butterfly, one is startled by sorrow, afraid to think of home. The cold wind blows through the snow-covered fishing boat, signaling the end of fishing, and the moonlight shines on the frosty watchtower, where cold sounds echo through the city. The rich fragrance of wine fills the cup, like a rosy hue, and the faint shadow of a plum tree crosses the paper screen, creating a clear, serene image. The beautiful woman reads and smiles, saying, "These are truly wonderful words, but if both rhymes are harmonious, they would be even better." Zhu says, "A gentleman does not wish to surpass others, so I lose one point." Zhu then says, "The mountains and waters of Shu are uniquely beautiful, and since ancient times, they have produced many beautiful women. If we compare them to Lady Zhaojun, Wenjun, and Xue Tao, who is superior?" The beautiful woman says, "Lady Zhaojun was far away and married into the desert, and Zhuo Wenjun selling wine is shameful. They are both beautiful but have short lives, suffering hardships. If you met Xue Tao, it would not be different from today. From this perspective, she is indeed superior." Zhu says, "Xue Tao was a courtesan, how dare I compare her to you. But her talent and beauty are also rare. I once read Qin Zaisi's *Records of the Strange* which states that Gao Qianli governed Shu and once held a banquet, changing a one-character command to 'mouth, like an endless measure.'"

Tao said, "The river is like three rafters." Gao replied, "Why is one curved?" Tao said, "Even a high-ranking official still uses a measuring cup, and a minor official has a curved rafter, so what is there to be surprised about!" The woman was intelligent and generous, truly hard to compare with others." The beauty said, "You know the result but not the reason behind it, such as these kinds of things, they are just playful words. If it is her work 'In the water country, the reeds are covered with frost at night, the moon and the cold mountain colors are all the same, who says that from tonight, a distance of ten thousand miles, the dream of separation is as long as the frontier,' it can rival Du Mu. And she is especially good at making small notes, even today, people in Shu call them Xue Tao notes. And you belittle her because she is a courtesan, you do not know Tao." After drinking, they went to bed, and Shu gave her a set of eight pearl wine containers. The beauty thanked him, saying, "I will cherish it, like a constant presence in the ear of a gentleman." After some time, Shu's mother fell ill, and he stopped teaching to take care of her. It took more than three months for her to recover. The beauty was surprised that he had been away for so long, fearing he had met someone else, and so she composed the 'Annoyed Song' to express her resentment. When Shu's mother recovered from her illness, he returned to the study, and that evening, he went to see Ping. The beauty greeted him and said, "Why have you been away for so long?" Shu told her the truth. The beauty said, "I have not seen you for three months, and now I have not seen you for three months." Shu joked with her, saying, "I have not tasted meat for three months, but I know the taste of meat tonight." While chatting, she took out the previous song to show Shu, and the song said:

A black lead sword cannot be sharpened, nor can a green lotus leaf be made into a garment that can withstand the wind? Even Xu lacquer and donkey-hide glue suddenly disintegrate, how can clear dust and muddy water meet? Look at the green grass and the butterfly in the southern garden, they coexist harmoniously, and the flowers are also pleased. Even when mandarin ducks grow old, they do not separate, unlike the story of Autumn Hu who abandoned his wife! A beautiful woman from the east can weave silk as fine as snow and as strong as a phoenix's loom. Even if there is a hint of rain and clouds, one should not lightly agree, what will happen even if one's teeth are broken? In a deep, quiet alley, the wind and moon are idle, and the tears in a brocade tent and orchid lamp are like blood. Even after many years, the blood spot remains red, and it is still present in the knot of the heart. Zhou loved her talent and beauty, and his affection for her grew deeper. The beautiful woman also valued Zhou's literary talent, and she did not hesitate to pour out her heart. She said to Zhou, "Our previous couplets did not fully express our high emotions. Tonight, we should play soft music and dance slowly, drink lightly, and recite softly, and compose another poem, so that we can see each other as formidable rivals." She then lit incense in a duck-shaped incense burner, served wine with red clam meat, pulled the curtain to look at the moon, and sat together in front of the hall. Zhou said, "In the past, Han Yu and Meng Jiao had works such as 'Couplets in the South of the City,' 'Fighting Roosters,' 'Stone Cauldron,' and 'Autumn Rain,' which were grand words and dangerous rhymes, and were widely praised. For this composition tonight, we should compose a couplet about the moon at night, with fifty lines as a standard. Do you agree, madam?" The beautiful woman said, "That is my intention." Zhou then asked the beautiful woman to start by composing the first couplet.

The moonlight in the courtyard is like a sheet of white silk, the stars in the pool resemble scattered chess pieces. The reflection of the Milky Way in the sky is faint, the seasons change as the Big Dipper moves. Pears and dates hang low from trees, vines and wisteria densely cover the fence. Grasslands are filled with scattered fireflies, a dried-up tree has a tilted bird's nest. Strange stones seem to resemble mythical creatures, fragrant flowers whose colors surpass those of a beautiful woman. A painted basin filled with cool water, a fine silk fan gently waving in the breeze. The game of dice is collected after play, the strings of a pipa are tuned. The sound of crickets near and far in the courtyard, the uneven sound of wind chimes on the eaves. The silver strings of a zither, a toad's skin used as the skin for a drum. A bamboo mat woven in a slanted pattern, a thin summer curtain.

The night birds settle down to rest but are startled and rise again; the startled birds descend but remain suspicious. The place is secluded and quiet, free from the usual hustle and bustle; the distant sound of the city's night watch travels far away. A graceful and elegant woman arrives, and a dignified and knowledgeable person recognizes the value of something rare and precious. A deep connection is fated or divinely ordained, and a solemn oath is so heavy that even spirits cannot deceive or break it. We have encountered a good evening, but it is difficult to meet someone during one's youth. We earnestly repay a long-standing friendship, and we pour out our hearts or emotions completely and thoroughly. The seeds of a lotus flower are used as the pegs of a jade zither, and a wine cup made from a lotus leaf is filled with green wine. A skilled servant is called to cut fish, and a young maid is called to bring a bottle. A crab shell is cracked open, and raw or lightly cooked clams are eaten. Lotus seeds are carefully peeled with delicate hands, and meat is cut with a sharp knife. The wine cups move rapidly during a drinking session, and a clear and slow melody is sung. We encourage and respond to each other in a casual and intimate manner, and we have a mixture of discussions and conversations. We taste cold and crisp fruits, and we sip salty and sour sauces. A beautiful wine cup is filled with amber-colored wine, and a unique glass is held. Bear paws are eaten with ivory chopsticks, and a sweet soup with honeycomb is served. When thirsty, we drink tea, and after a hearty meal, we enjoy cold drinks. Beautiful verses are linked together, and the mind wanders and becomes excited. A singing session ends, and bedding is arranged early.

No need to seek out Tao Ye, nor must you listen to Zhu Zhi! The smooth and charming voice of a skylark, the intoxication of a butterfly by passionate love. When she coughs, pearls condense in her saliva; when she frowns, her eyebrows draw with black pigment. Her hairpin slightly askew, causing gold to slip down, her bracelets cold, causing goosebumps on her skin. A small child truly can jest, Pan Pan is most skilled at understanding poetry. A dream or fantasy involving romantic and erotic encounters, graceful and beautiful words. Walking slowly with a swaying waist, whispering softly with a low voice, taking precautions at night to prevent others from overhearing, bathing in the afternoon to avoid being secretly watched. Removing embroidered shoes with a shy expression, blowing out a silver lamp while smiling, undressing beside a bed made of plain silk, sweat from the face and body, which is left on the pillow. The warmth of jade wrapped in fine silk, fingers as white and slender as spring onions, hair slightly disheveled but still green, the movement of waves in a green curtain. Intimate and playful behavior worth capturing in a painting, the beauty of a person's face being so captivating that it can satisfy hunger, the dust from the socks of a dancer, the greasy buildup in the hair from sleeping. Xun He, a poet known for his high literary reputation, Cui Ying, a woman known for her extraordinary beauty. The beauty of being connected is not as praised, the admiration of being together. Where can one find leaves written on? Who is married? One should be firm and steadfast, like glue or lacquer, the bed and the place of intimacy, only those who know the truth can understand.

Precaution: Do not sow enmity (薛), do not let separation be regretted (洙).** **Orchids and fragrant grass share the same virtue (薛), pines and cypresses share the same long-term aspirations (洙).** Always cherish the joys of home life (薛), always engage in scholarly pursuits for pleasure (洙).** **Mount Tai as a grindstone (薛), do not let this resolve be compromised (洙). One day, as (洙) passed by the academy, he advised (百禄), saying, "Let your son return home daily, as he cannot manage the journey, let him stay overnight at my humble abode, would that not be convenient?" (百禄) replied, "Since the academy opened, he has always stayed at the academy. Before, due to his mother's illness, he temporarily stayed for a season, but he has not returned since. How can you say that?" **(张) was greatly surprised and dared not finish his words, leaving.** That evening, (洙) indeed returned home. (张) secretly sent someone to follow him, but he disappeared halfway. The messenger reported back to (张), who quickly sent someone into the city to ask about (百禄), but (百禄) was not there. (张) thought the young man must be staying at a brothel, but considering there are no brothels here, he found it very strange. The next day, (洙) returned, and (张) asked, "Where did you stay last night?" (洙) said, "At home." (张) said, "No! I have already sent someone to follow you, but I cannot determine where you went, and the academy also did not see you?" (洙) lied, "I went to visit a friend and talked for a long time, arriving home in the evening." **(张) knew he was lying and called for (洙)’s servant to confirm the story.

Zhu shouted, "When you came to my house, you immediately left the city. By the time I return, you will have already left. How can you speak falsely?" The servant replied, "I stayed at Master's house last night and only returned after breakfast this morning. The old scholar was also very surprised and wanted to come and find you." Zhu was very embarrassed and his expression changed abruptly. Zhang said, "If Master has any relatives, you should tell the truth and not hide it." Zhu could no longer conceal it and explained the whole story, and he also apologized, "This was done by my respected relative who kept me here, not that I dared to be disrespectful." Zhang said, "Our family has never had any relatives here. Moreover, among all the sisters, there is no one named Ping. It must be a ghost. Now you should take care of yourself and not go back again!" Zhu nodded in agreement. By the time he arrived, the beauty already knew and said, "Do not blame me, it seems that fate has run its course here." They drank deeply together and recounted their joyful feelings. At dawn, the beauty told Zhu, "From now on, we will part forever, and it is hard to predict when we will meet again. There is nothing to convey my feelings." She gave him a gift of a writing brush made of ink-black jade and said, "This is a Tang dynasty object. Please keep it carefully." They then wept and parted.

Zhang Liaozhu was expected to return that night, so he went out to spy on him, and indeed he was not in the guesthouse. He then went inside and told his wife, "The tutor's behavior in this matter cannot be kept from his parents." He then fully informed Baolü of what Zhu had done. Baolü became very angry, called Zhu back, and beat him. Zhu then confessed the truth and also produced the jade paperweight, jade pen tube, and the couplets and various poems he had obtained. Baolü took them and looked at them, seeing that the pen tube was engraved with "The refined and precious items of the Wenfang of Gaosh family of Bohai." He then said to Zhang, "The objects are already rare and extraordinary, and the poetry is also elegant and free; it must not be an ordinary strange thing." He called Zhu to go together and investigate it. As they approached, he pointed far away and said, "It's there." When they arrived, the scene was entirely different from before, with no houses in sight, only the water was clear and the mountains were green, and the peach trees remained as they were. Zhang said to Baolü, "Yes, this place is said to be where the Tang courtesan Xue Tao was buried. Later people, because of Zheng Gu's poem from Shu that says 'small peach blossoms surround Xue Tao's grave,' planted a hundred peach trees here as a place for springtime sightseeing. My son had a good encounter; it must be Xue Tao."

As for the term "嫁平幼子康," it refers to the name of a street, known as Pingkang Lane. There is no such sign or title for the Wenxiao Ward in the city; however, when "wen" (文) and "xiao" (孝) are combined, they form the character "jiao" (教), which means "teaching" or "instruction." This is what is referred to as the "Jiaofang" (教坊). The Jiaofang was an institution during the Tang Dynasty where courtesans resided. Zhao Yunjian (薛涛) was a courtesan from Sichuan who lived in the Jiaofang. Who else could it be but her? Furthermore, the inscriptions on the tube or scroll bear the mark of Gao's refined collection, indicating that these items were stored over a thousand miles away by Gao Pian, the military governor of Western Sichuan during the Tang Dynasty. When Gao Pian governed Sichuan, Zhao Yunjian was the most favored and treated with great respect among all the courtesans, receiving writing tools such as brushes and paperweights as gifts from him. Additionally, the various documents and writings in his collection, which were also bestowed upon Yuan Zhen and Du Mu, were the most numerous. Yuan and Du once wrote poems to her, such as "The smoothness of the Jinjiang River and the beauty of Mount Emei, conjuring forth Wenjun and Xue Tao." There is no doubt that the spirit or essence belongs to her, and it is certain that the items came from Gao Pian. There is no need to investigate further!" Bai Lu strongly agreed, but he feared that he might ultimately be deceived. He quickly sent the items back to Guangzhou, where he treasured them and often showed them to others. Two years later, Zhu also entered school, became a student, passed the imperial examination in the year Jia Xu of the Hongwu era, and was appointed as the magistrate of Cao County in Shandong. In the end, there was nothing else remarkable about him.

**Qingcheng Dance Sword Record** During the reign of Emperor Toghon Temür, there were two Daoists named Zhen Benwu and Wen Gu Xu, whose origins are unknown. They served under the Prince of Weishun, were proficient in swordsmanship, knowledgeable about military affairs, and had deep strategic insight. They were known as men of both literary and martial talents. Although the prince kept them, he never found them remarkable; only Fan Kou Wei Junmei valued them highly. One day, the prince went to a private garden and summoned the two Daoists to attend him. He then spoke to them in a leisurely manner: "Today, the empire has enjoyed long periods of peace and prosperity. In your view, it is a time to lie on a high pillow and indulge in pleasures, focusing only on music, women, dogs, and horses, without concern for anything else! But in our humble opinion, things are quite different. The emperor is old and senile, and his favored concubine Kui Shi is domineering. Officials like Hama and Xue Xue, who practice the *yanzhai'er* ritual, have corrupted his heart. Bribery is openly practiced, right and wrong are confused, the heavens are signaling calamity, but the emperor does not realize it. The people are suffering, but the emperor is unaware. Military preparedness is neglected, the court's governance is lax, corrupt officials act without restraint, and virtuous officials are suppressed. It is like a single hair pulling down a thousand catties, disaster is imminent, and it is very frightening."

Su Shi once said: "When disorder begins to germinate but has not yet taken visible form, it is called impending chaos." The prestigious relatives of the imperial court and the strategic importance of the Jianghan region suggest that one should seek out talented individuals, select capable generals, train troops, conserve resources, and prepare secretly. If there is a sudden outbreak of unrest or rebellion, one should raise a just cause and lead the charge, relieving the emperor's distress and fulfilling one's loyalty as a subject, reclaiming the lost territories and restoring the former glory of the nation. After achieving success, one should retire from public life without boasting, request to return to one's fief, and guard the southern borders. Let the historians record one's deeds as a great hero, inscribed in the imperial archives for posterity. How great and splendid this would be! The king was surprised and said, "Are you not suffering from madness or delusion? Why do you speak so incoherently? I will have you taken to the county official!" The two men silently withdrew and discussed, saying, "Those who have lost their spirit and vitality, what can we teach them to achieve anything? We should seek out and support heroic figures. Such a person is not worth dealing with! If we stay, disaster will come." They then left a poem on the Yellow Crane Tower and departed. The original poem reads:

Throughout my life, wisdom and strategies fill my chest, my sword gleams like autumn frost, and its aura shines like a rainbow. I am ashamed to use the three-inch tongue of Su Qin, but I aim to support heroes in their endeavors. Gu Xu composed these two poems: With bold courage in my seven-foot frame, would my ambitions make me a timid scholar? The teachings of the old man by the bridge are merely for show, but I have a scroll of the *Lügong* (Book of Military Strategy) for myself. The sword emerges from its scabbard, reflecting cold light, and its blade bears the shadow of blood from enemies. I knew early on that such efforts were futile, yet I was mistakenly treated as a hero by the king.

The king sought them, but they were hidden. Soon after, chaos erupted, exactly as he had predicted. In the year 1345 of the Zhizheng era, Ni Wenjun captured Mianyang. Wishun's son Baonu and the Hunan Yuan帅 Ashilan advanced both by land and water to attack him. They reached Hanchuan, where the shallow water caused their boats to become stuck. Wenjun used fire rafts to burn the ships, and Baonu was killed. The king mourned for him and tried every means to find the two men, but he could not. Chen Youliang heard that they had been traveling between Guang and Huang, and prepared letters and gifts to invite them. They did not come, and instead, they gracefully entered Sichuan. Later, Ming Yuzhen took control of Sichuan and had long heard of the two men's names, but he could not find them. After the Tianchao Dynasty had pacified all the bandits and unified the four seas, Junmei's brother Junyan became the magistrate of Xichong County. Junmei went to visit him. On his return journey, their boat capsized, and all the people on board were drowned. Only Junmei managed to grab a piece of wood, which was washed ashore, and thus he survived. However, his belongings and money were all lost. By chance, he had a few coins in his waistband, which he quickly used to seek shelter at a nearby household, where he found fire to dry his clothes, bought food to satisfy his hunger, and wandered aimlessly, unable to think of a solution. The old man of the household saw his demeanor and knew he was no ordinary person, so he treated him kindly. He stayed there for several days and then went out for a walk. Suddenly, two Taoist priests bowed before him and said, "How cold you are, General Wei!" Upon seeing them, he realized they were his old friends, Zhen and Wen. He told them of his plight. They said, "Do not worry." They took him to their home, which turned out to be Qingcheng Mountain. There were tall walls and splendid houses, deep courtyards, and winding rooms, with several servants standing on both sides. The food and drink were prepared with both aquatic and terrestrial delicacies, and the music and dance reached the height of their splendor. They talked about old times, and it was as if they had known each other for a long time.

They inquired about their whereabouts during the chaos, and the two men said, "After departing from Yellow Crane Tower, I entered Yellow Bull. I had been long hidden in Qingcheng, and suddenly encountered the green eye. The joy and relief were almost indescribable. What is regrettable is the decline of youthful ambitions. Looking up and down at the universe, living a life of uncertainty and instability, living in isolation, feeling ashamed before old friends." Then they drank deeply, and as they became drunk, their spirits soared, and debates erupted. Bennu said, "All matters of the world depend on knowing the signs. The signs are the subtlest aspects of things, the first indications of good or bad fortune. The *Yijing* says, 'Knowing the signs is divine?' It also says, 'A gentleman acts upon seeing the signs, without waiting until the end of the day.' Zisi said, 'A gentleman knows the subtle.' These all refer to the same idea. Since ancient times, there have been many heroes, but how many truly know the signs? I found Zhang Liang in the Han dynasty."

The deeds of Zhang Liang are recorded in history, so there is no need to elaborate further. Why not discuss his virtues? Among Emperor Gaozu's ministers, none surpass the Three Heroes, and Zhang Liang is the foremost among them. Xiang Yu was superior to Emperor Gaozu, but he was defeated by Emperor Gaozu, due to Zhang Liang's strategy. Thus, Zhang Liang is not only the foremost among the Three Heroes, but also surpasses both Emperor Gaozu and Xiang Yu. Moreover, Emperor Gaozu's listing of the Three Heroes was a sign of his jealousy. Zhang Liang understood this, but Xiao He and Han Xin did not. Therefore, they ultimately suffered the humiliation of imprisonment and the disaster of being exterminated. Zhang Liang remained unperturbed and unharmed. The disaster does not occur on the day of the disaster, but in the moment of recognizing the Three Heroes. When the world was not yet settled, Zhang Liang displayed boundless ingenuity. When the world was settled, Zhang Liang retreated as if he were foolish, choosing a small county for his fief, and refrained from speaking first. How perceptive was his understanding? Indeed, he is a true man of virtue.

"Gu Xu said: 'I met a person in Song, whose name is Chen Tunan. The chaos of the Five Dynasties was unprecedented in ancient times. Without a hero rising to bring order, when would the chaos ever end? Chen Tunan saw the signs of the times and had ambitions for great deeds. His travels between Guan and Luo were not aimless. When he heard of Zhao Zu ascending the throne, he laughed while falling off his donkey, thus uttering the cryptic phrase, “The person born in the Year of the Pig has already donned the yellow robe.” By examining the character “已” in the phrase, one can see its meaning. He then swept his sleeves and returned to the mountains, where he lay high among the white clouds, surrounded by wildflowers and singing birds, in the springtime scenery. He rose high and far, leaving no trace. This is what is meant by hiding great skill in the most simple and concealing great wisdom in the most foolish. In future generations, people will know him only as a sage or a hermit! Who can fathom his deepest secrets? Compared to Zhang Liang, he was equally or more remarkable.'"

A saying goes, "When a hero looks back, they become a deity—how can one not believe this?" Jun Mei said, "The two gentlemen have refined themselves on famous mountains, yet they seem to still be attached to worldly wealth. Even after hearing your lofty words, they still seem unable to forget their worldly desires. Is this not a hindrance to their spiritual cultivation?" The two laughed loudly and said, "Jun Mei, your usual discussions are so high-minded, yet your current tastes and interests are so lowly. Those who mechanically follow rules and recite meaningless phrases are the dregs of Confucianism. Those who engage in physical exercises and breathing practices are but the remnants of Daoism. What we call spiritual cultivation is not found in such things!" They then led Jun Mei to view their home, which was filled with luxurious silks and brocades, mountains of gold and jade, each guarded by beautiful attendants. Finally, they arrived at a mountain cliff where there were hundreds of skulls. The two pointed and said, "These are the unjust people of the world, whom we have punished." Jun Mei was so shocked that he stuck out his tongue, and it remained that way for a long time. The next day, a grand banquet was held, with Jun Mei seated at the head table. Two beautiful women carried a tray filled with ten precious pearls and a hundred taels of gold as a gift. Jun Mei could not refuse and simply nodded in thanks. They then drank heavily and became drunk, at which point Jun Mei spontaneously composed a poem:

A hero of the world, a talent of the world, Through countless battles across the lands, dust rises. The white crane of Liaodong leaves empty words behind, The gold of the world builds a futile platform! Grand ambitions have become eternal regrets, The remnants of my work will be left for future generations to lament! The mighty ships of Cao Cao, a million measures, Are all reduced to ashes by Zhou Yu's single fire! Continuing the poem, I say: Heroes are worn down, lamenting the Five Tombs, My hair stands on end, my chest filled with anger! There are still heroes before my eyes, Why bother to discuss the rise and fall after death? The snake that blocks the road has already been cut off, The prophecy of no horse crossing the river is unreliable. Pitiful is this land of the Central Plains, How many wars have there been with tigers roaring and dragons soaring? Such is the style of his poetry, and his character can be imagined. Youmei knows that his poetry cannot be surpassed, so he composed the *Xǐ Qiānyīng* (Happy Migration) tune, holding a cup to thank the two gentlemen, singing it himself to accompany them. The lyrics say: The universe is as it was yesterday, Lamenting the past, how desolate, the talents are sparse. The scenery is all different, the people have all changed, It is no longer the old city. The affairs of the world are like chess pieces, Only those in the game know how difficult it is to make a move. Victory and defeat, like a spring dream, Why be surprised! Lonely, meeting each other, Like萍water in a strange land, drinking freely in the clear night. Speaking of heroes, their lives are like dreams, The sword's edge and the lotus blade are all dull. Seeing through the changes of floating clouds, Do not ask who is stronger or weaker! It is worth sighing, This return, like the white crane of Liaodong.

The next day, you requested to return. The two people said, "In the Tang Dynasty, there was Red Silk; now there is Green Silk. I will send you." When you arrived, there was a beautiful woman, about seventeen or eighteen years old, carrying a bamboo box, and she accompanied Zhen and Wen to escort you to the Qingcheng Road. She turned to you and said, "It will be difficult to predict when we will meet again. Please allow me to perform a dance for you." Green Silk opened the box and took out four white pills, each as large as a chicken's egg, which were the male and female swords. The two people drew them out and extended them, leaping up and down. In a moment, the sky and earth became dark and obscure, the wind and clouds looked gloomy, and only in the dust could one see flashes of lightning, interwoven and entangled. Your legs trembled, and you could not take a step. Looking back at their residence, you saw only steep cliffs and high precipices, with no path in sight. You were unable to breathe, unable to close your eyes, always as if a blade was at your throat, and your heart and courage both fell.
