After the dance, the two people disappeared, and only Green Silk stood beside you, pouring the wine from the skin bag and drinking together. Waiting until night, she took your hand and departed southeast, arriving home around the third watch of the night. You only saw gold and jewels on the bed, and Green Silk had been gone for a long time, finally, you did not know what kind of magic it was. In the 20th year of the Hongwu era, you had a son-in-law named Dan Gong Xuan who was a treasury official. Sometimes, he told people about your father-in-law's affairs, which also matched this story.

Autumn Evening Visit to the Pipa Pavilion In the early years of the Hongwu era, Shen Shao from Wujian, at the age of twenty, was known for his good looks and was skilled in poetry under the influence of Sa Tianxi and calligraphy under the guidance of Bian Bojing. Both were praised by his contemporaries. He once composed a poem in response to Sa Tianxi's "Passing Through Jiaxing" and titled it "Two Poems on Wu": Seven lakes and three rivers connect to Fuli, willows and lotuses reflect in the lake water. Beyond the Gate of the West is the Gate of the East, with half-drawn pearl curtains in a painted building. Mǐwú grows throughout the sand where mandarin ducks live, the east wind has blown away all the pear blossoms. The scent of the path at the Guanwa pavilion is where deer wander, ghost lanterns made of red gauze glow in the clear night. The eastern flow continues at the Three High Officials Temple, the wind blows through the bamboo on Zhen Nang's tomb. After Xi Shi left, the wooden walkway fell into disrepair, the green marks of fire from year to year during deep spring. Southeastern regions, known for their beauty and prosperity, are filled with the sound of flutes and oboes playing near the river. A young girl makes a spring robe from white hemp, the reflection of the moonlight on the oars made of cassia wood and orchid wood. Stages and singing pavilions are near the sand where seagulls rest, the half-visible pink walls with cherry blossoms. The scent of butterflies that do not fly away, the delicate silk of a fan falling gently. Who can continue the Wu songs, specifically "Ziye"? The wind blows through the bamboo in the shade of willows at the Keting pavilion. Fan Li left in a small boat and did not return, only the spring waves reflecting green light. His other poems were similar in style. However, due to his wealth, he did not wish to enter public service. People knew this, and some sought to profit from his wealth, wanting to nominate him as a filial and upright official or to recommend him as a student. The situation was chaotic and confusing, with no peace. Although Shen Shao was not stingy with his wealth, he was truly annoyed by the disturbances. He then discussed with his brother-in-law Zhang, saying, "What can be done about this?" Zhang replied, "Only by traveling far away can one avoid it."

Zhao resolved upon his plan. He then invited his cousin Chen and Liang, and together they boarded towering and massive ships, carrying an immense fortune, and set out to wander freely between the Xiang and Han rivers. They stopped at the Prefecture of Jiuzhou, where Zhao admired the beauty of Mount Lu and viewed the clarity of Poyang Lake, lingering in the city walls and visiting ancient sites in search of secluded beauty. The others began to criticize him slightly, but Zhao paid no heed. He sighed and said, “We are fortunate to be wealthy and young, with a basic knowledge of writing and literature. This journey is merely to avoid people. How could we emulate Wang Rong and his ilk, who clasp the abacus and meticulously calculate the profit of knives and needles?” Their travels became even more frequent. One day, after the autumn rain had just cleared, the water and sky were one color. Zhao, accompanied by Liang and Chen, visited the Pipa Pavilion together, reciting Bai Juyi’s poem about “willow catkins and maple leaves,” recalling the melody of the “silver bottle and iron steed” from the capital girl. They looked around in all directions, lingering for a long time. At that time, the moon was bright, the wind was gentle, the people were quiet, and the night was deep. They then took wine and drank together. Suddenly, they heard a faint song under the moon, sometimes far, sometimes near, sometimes high, sometimes low. The three of them looked at each other in surprise. Liang joked, “Could it not be a merchant’s wife who understands the situation?” Zhao replied, “At that time, Bai Juyi still needed to be called upon a thousand times before he would appear; how could she present herself so easily today?” Chen said, “An old woman with delicate brows, playing the pipa with sorrowful tones—though she may gently pluck and stroke the strings before the guests, it would only increase the feeling of being a wanderer lost in the world. How could it lead to drunken joy?” Zhao said, “Let us listen quietly.” After a long while, it became silent. After finishing their wine and returning to the boat, they still did not know the reason for it.

Shao, versatile and full of variation, is kind-hearted and full of emotion. The next day, he went to investigate the truth. In a moment of hesitation, he saw nothing, and his interest waned as his body grew tired; he was just about to say goodbye and return. Suddenly, a strange fragrance, very fragrant, came mysteriously. Shao found it strange and waited anxiously. After a short while, a beautiful woman dressed in palace attire and adorned beautifully, looking like a heavenly beauty, appeared. Two young maids led the way, one holding a golden incense burner and one carrying a purple embroidered cushion, slowly ascending the steps. Shao thought it must be a member of a noble family coming to enjoy the scenery here and hid behind the wall to avoid them. The young maid laid the cushion in the middle of the courtyard, and the beautiful woman sat on the ground. Turning to the maid, she said, "How could there be a living person's scent? Isn't it because there was a mad guest here last night?" Fearing that they might send someone to search, Shao got up and came out to pay respects and apologized for the intrusion.

The Beautiful Woman said, "Different dynasties, and no formal relationship, why should it be considered rude? But the young men talked and laughed last night, referring to a prostitute from Chang'an and a merchant's wife from Fuliang, isn't that a bit excessive?" Shao was at a loss and didn't know how to respond. The beautiful woman called for Shao to join her on the same mat, she declined four times; but she insisted, so he finally sat down. He then asked her name. The beautiful woman said, "I want to tell you the whole story, but I fear it will shock your ears. However, I am not one who brings misfortune to others, please don't be surprised! I am Princess Zheng Wane, a concubine of the Han Emperor Chen. I died at the age of twenty, and was buried near this pavilion. Two maidservants named Dian Chan and Jin Yan, who were also buried with me at that time." Shao, who was naturally bold and also valued romantic sentiment, did not find this strange. The beautiful woman said, "I have been in a state of depression, living alone, and have no way to cheer myself up. I often sang and played music here, to dispel my melancholy. I didn't expect last night to be taken by the young men, and returned in a foul mood. Now I am fortunate to be in this good night, and to meet a good guest, which is enough to make up for it." She sent Dian Chan to fetch wine and food, and they drank at the pavilion. She sang her own lyrics, saying, "Do you remember it? It is the *Nian Nu Jiao* that I sang yesterday." The lyrics said:

Dense and lush crops, I lament that the landscape seems unchanged, heroes turned to dust. Stone horses and bronze camels buried in thorns, have witnessed many seasons of cold and heat! Swords and spears turn to ashes, flags disperse like birds, where can one find the watchtowers? The roar of a hero, still spoken of today in the state of Chu. Withered and weary general, in the light of the lamp, covering her face, tears fall like a red rain! The phoenix carriage and sheep-drawn cart do not return, the heart twists and coils with slow pain. Plum blossoms adorn her makeup, willow catkins fly like snow, looking back, it becomes a distant past! Green snails and blue-black pigments, the beautiful woman lazily draws her eyebrows with a charming grace.

When the song ended, the lady urged Shao to drink to the dregs. After several cups, Shao's bold and unrestrained demeanor emerged, his discourse lively and spirited. He spoke with the lady about the rise and fall of the great warlords at the end of the Yuan dynasty, recounting events as clearly as if he had seen them with his own eyes, and also inquired into the details of Lord Chen’s actions. The lady replied, “The *Spring and Autumn Annals* conceal the faults of the powerful and the beloved; such matters are not something I dare to know.” Shao said, “Let me then speak of his character: soft-spoken and lacking decisiveness, blind and oblivious to subtle signs. In appointing his ministers, he favored the unqualified, such as Chen Pingzhang and Yao Pingzhang, who were petty men of narrow minds, yet he entrusted them with the reins of power and command of the military. On the other hand, men of virtue and talent like Zhan Tongwen and Wei Qishan were relegated to insignificant posts and left in idle positions. The military officials indulged in wine and women, while the civil officials concerned themselves only with empty talk. The city gates were too narrow to admit a carriage, so they built a flying bridge; Jiujiang, though humble, aspired to be the capital, yet its former site remains. Examples like these are laughably numerous. Moreover, he secretly assassinated Shouhui and openly took his place, changing the era name and establishing a new title—like a brother trapped in a well, the son Yang. Narrow-minded and lacking in strategy, he was no more than a servant to Li Jing of the south. And yet he still struggled like a mantis against an eagle, resisting with futile force. His soldiers were slaughtered in the lake waters, his generals killed in the field; his great men were destroyed, his phantom body falling swiftly to the arrow’s point. One defeat, and heaven abandoned him; his six armies scattered like stars. As for those who secretly planned in the war tent and aided in times of crisis, there was only the Fifth Prince alone. Alas! In the time when the heroes were in turmoil, in the days of primitive chaos and dust, if the strategists, generals, capable officials, and talented men were so few and weak, how could he not have been defeated and perished?” The lady listened with sorrow, tears streaming down her face.

After weeping, she collected herself and said, "Let's talk about light, pleasant topics; there's no need to delve into serious matters, for they would only make one feel uneasy." She then composed a poem on the spot, saying: [The poem would follow here, but since it is not provided, it will be left as an open placeholder for further translation.]

The luxurious ships and dragon boats are now but a distant memory, their grandeur confined to the dreams and memories of the past. The sun sets over the yellow reeds, casting a warm glow over the decaying walls of an abandoned fortress, while the imperial palace, once a symbol of power and glory, is now overgrown with green grass and shrouded in cold mist. The fish oil lamps in the tunnels are nearly extinguished, and the makeup table and鸾镜 (a type of mirror) are sealed away, forgotten. I implore you not to speak of the rise and fall of dynasties, for it brings tears that damage the beauty of the past. Reciting and seeking harmony, Shao responded with a poem that echoed the same rhythm and tone: The intricately decorated palace, once bustling with life, is now empty, with tears shed during the setting sun. The new socks left by the Tang dynasty are no longer seen, but the old palace guards of the Han dynasty remain, still present but in a state of decay. In the depths of autumn, the deserted imperial garden is filled with falling yellow leaves, and the imperial mausoleum, now overgrown with green moss, symbolizes the passage of time. I feel inadequate compared to Niu Sengru, but I still pay homage to the beauty and grace of the past, even in a cloud-covered setting. The beautiful woman exclaimed, "You truly understand my feelings." They then gathered around the table, drinking heartily and sharing a night together in the pavilion. They engaged in intimate conversation, much like any couple in the world. Soon, the cries of crows in the sky and the cessation of the drumbeats on the city walls signaled the end of the night. They rose together, hand in hand, and said, "Tonight, we should return to our lodgings and plan for the future. It is not fitting to sleep in the open, exposed to the elements, and be ridiculed by the common people!" Shao nodded in agreement. They quickly returned to their inn, where Chen and Liang were waiting to board the boat. Shao then lied, saying, "I received a letter from home yesterday, urging me to return urgently. There must be some other reason, so I cannot accompany you. Please go ahead, and I will catch up soon. I hope you will prepare some delicate fish and buy many crabs, and in three or four months, we will drink together at the famous pool of Xi, search for the inscriptions left by Yang Gong, and sing the song 'Dadi,' making this journey a momentary pleasure." Chen and Liang believed him, and they parted ways, holding hands.

Shao returned that evening, and Jin Yan was already there. She led him through the shade of bamboo north of the pavilion, about half a li further, where he saw a grand entrance with red gates and plain walls, illuminated by the glow of lanterns and candles. Upon reaching the grand hall, a beautiful woman greeted him with a smile, presenting a purple jade cup for Shao to drink. She said, "This was used by my master, and now I offer it to encourage you; the meaning is not shallow." They stayed overnight for more than a month, their bond as strong as glue and lacquer. One night, the beautiful woman spoke to Shao, saying, "When I died, the false Han was flourishing, and my master's favor was deep. Therefore, my funeral procession was accompanied by a jade box and a pearl robe, reaching the peak of wealth and honor at the time. The tomb and spirit road were equipped with the dignity of a first-rank official. Thus, my body remains intact, and my three souls are not lost. Formerly, Lady of the South Pole, the beloved daughter of Lord Lu, happened to play here. She taught me the technique of refining the form through the Great Yin, and after doing it for a long time, I became no different from a living person, going out at night and hiding during the day, free and easy. You should go to the market and seek half a cup of green sheep's milk, frequently dripping it into my eyes. When the milk is finished and my eyes open, I can rise during the day." Shao obtained the milk as she said, moistening the corners of her eyes. After three weeks, she could walk. Sometimes they would walk together, holding hands in the tunnel, or lean together on fragrant shoulders, laughing and singing on the pavilion. She discussed old matters with Shao, saying, "Not even twelve or thirteen years have passed, and it has already become old memories. One day, my master read *The Lost Tales of Tianbao* and found it pleasing, so in the palace during the Spring and Autumn period, a banquet was held, and we women competed to wear exotic flowers. My master personally released a butterfly, which smelled the fragrance of the flowers, flew, and landed on the tip of the hairpin. The person where it landed was summoned that night, called it a butterfly's favor.

The emperor further instructed the concubines, saying, "In the past, Emperor Xuanzong of Tang frequently engaged in this activity, and Yang Guifei held exclusive favor, so it was no longer performed. I, however, do not differentiate between favoritism and partiality. You should also understand the equal distribution of favor and the purpose of warning and caution." All of them knelt and thanked him. He then said, "I once received Liu Wen, a Yuan dynasty Jinshi and governor of Mianyang, and treated him with special honors. In the leisure time of managing myriad state affairs, I brought him into the private hall and casually inquired, 'I hear you are the Grand Doctor of the Imperial Academy, very renowned. Is that true?' Liu Wen replied, 'I am an official of the Rites. In the third year of the Zhizheng era, on the day of the tenth month, I was preparing to perform a southern suburb sacrifice and pay homage to the Imperial Ancestral Temple. When I reached the hall of Ningzong, I asked, 'I am the elder brother of Ningzong, should I bow?' I replied, 'Although Ningzong is my younger brother, when he was emperor, I was his subject.'"

During the Spring and Autumn period, he was the younger brother of Duke Min of Lu and the elder brother of Duke Xi of Lu. Duke Min had first become the ruler, and during ancestral temple sacrifices, it was not heard that Duke Xi did not pay respects. Your Majesty should pay respects. He followed this. My lord then summoned him and said, "You have served in the central court but have never held a prominent position, yet your literary talent and learning are too great to be concealed. Treat me as you would have treated the former ruler, and you will not be concerned about not reaching high office." He bowed and thanked. The lord then said, "You and Li Fu were on the same examination list. If Li Fu had not died, I would have greatly promoted him. However, Li Fu acted for his own lord, and I am fortunate to have gained you. I heard that you are good at poetry. Have you composed anything recently?" He replied, "I could not die for my principles, and I feel ashamed compared to Li Fu. I once composed ten poems using Du Fu's 'Full of Sorrow' as a model, in the style of 'Far Travel,' to express my aspirations. I have forgotten them all now, but I remember only one poem. I will recite it for Your Majesty." He then knelt and said:

The world has faced calamity in the ninth year, and the weapons of war have brought disaster to the people. Mountains and valleys have their natural elevations and depressions, but in a single day, their arrangement can change. The general, traveling through difficult terrain, travels far with great passion. Though they share the same aspirations, their actions differ; there is no inherent conflict between yielding and asserting oneself. The honorable Li of Jiangzhou sought benevolence and achieved it. The clear and pure wind has blown for ten years, and I am still a person. Afterward, the ruler addressed his close attendant, saying, "His words are shameful!" Thus, he looked down upon his character and had no further intention of promoting or employing him. This person is indeed the type of scholar Zhu Xi criticized as lacking integrity. From my perspective, not only Wang Wei of Ningbi and Fan Zhi, who should be blamed, but also others are deserving of blame!" Zhao heard his argument and was greatly convinced. Many details of the palace affairs at that time were not fully remembered. How could Zhao, who was deeply infatuated, have a shallow attachment to his hometown? From spring to autumn, it has been four years. Even the fish that swim together and the birds that fly together are not sufficient to describe the intimate and graceful relationship.

In the early winter of that year, the lady wept suddenly without cause, overwhelmed with sorrow. Surprised, he asked her, but at first she remained silent, enduring her pain. Then, she burst into loud weeping. Shao tried to comfort her in every way possible, and she finally spoke, saying, "I have a fated connection with the young man, which will be fulfilled in the next day, hence I cannot help feeling so sad!" Hearing this, Shao was deeply distressed and sorrowful, and he wanted to hang himself in the tunnel. The lady stopped him, saying, "Your earthly life is not yet over, and my spiritual essence has not yet transformed. If you were to indulge further in worldly attachments, leading to your untimely death, the underworld would surely impose severe punishment, entangling us in endless complications. Moreover, fate is inescapable; nothing can escape it. Even if you were to sacrifice your life, it would be in vain." Shao then stopped. Jinyan and Dianchan, too, were reluctant to part, and they all prepared food to see Shao off. By morning, the lady presented him with a pair of gold bracelets and a set of jeweled hair ornaments, saying, "These gifts express my sincerity and meaning. Seeing them, you will think of me. We will never meet again, so please take care." She personally saw him off to the gate, covering her face with her sleeves as she returned. Shao was still deeply saddened, with tears streaming down his face. In the blink of an eye, she disappeared from sight. He then relocated to the original inn and prepared to return to Wu. A few days later, Liang came from Xiangyang, and Chen died as a guest in Fang County. They blamed Shao for breaking his promise, but he kept it secret. When he showed them the bracelets and hair ornaments, they were astonished and said, "These are not ordinary objects; they are extraordinary treasures, indeed evidence that you have encountered a fairy!" Shao earnestly advised them not to speak lightly, and thus no one else knew about the encounter.

Returning home by boat, upon reaching the gate, he discovered that his wife had been dead for a long time. He then sold one piece of jewelry in a foreign shop and obtained ten thousand taels of silver. In a quiet place in Tiger Hill, he built a platform and invited Taoist priest Zhou Xuanzhu of He Lin to perform the Lingbao ritual for three days and nights. On the evening of the main ritual for his wife, he waited until all the Taoist priests had left, wrote a heartfelt letter, burned it secretly in the incense burner, to benefit the deceased woman in the afterlife. After the ritual, Zhou Xuanzhu dreamed of two women, one surnamed Zhang and one surnamed Zheng, accompanied by two little girls, who thanked him saying, "We have all received good results and have been appointed as attendants to the Queen Mother of the Jade Platform." After they finished speaking, they rode on auspicious clouds westward. The next day, Zhou Xuanzhu questioned Shao, "You only recommended the lady Zhang from the inner chamber, why are there also three others like Zheng?" Shao realized it referred to the beautiful woman, the pearl, and the goose, but pretended not to understand, saying, "My dream was also like that, but I don't know who those three people are." Finally, he did not tell. Only Liang Sheng knows this. Therefore, Liang Sheng wrote the poem 'Pipa Jia Yu' and appended it here. The poem says: ### Poem: *Pipa Jia Yu* (琵琶佳遇) (Translation of the poem would follow here, if provided in the original text.)

Remembering the days of my youth, when I first underwent the coming-of-age ceremony. In spring, I wore a purple silk sash and rode a white horse with red reins. Wuzhong has always been known for its prosperity, with lotus flowers blooming for miles around. I interacted with young women, and we sailed together on a celestial boat. We lingered by the Poyang Lake, getting drunk in an empty pavilion and playing the pipa. I hit a silver comb to keep time, an act that was hard to ask about, and my shoes were covered in dust, which was particularly pitiful! The moon rose over Mount Lu, and I met a graceful and beautiful woman by the lake. I followed a pair of beautiful women, entering the most intimate part of a luxurious setting. The spring breeze brought forth blooming peonies, and in the fragrant and luxurious bedroom, I often dreamed of romantic encounters, which filled me with sorrow in the quiet of the night. The beautiful women of the past were admired by many, and Yang Guifei's beauty was a favorite subject. I repeated my visits to a luxurious setting, where I loved the light. Our emotional bond was suddenly severed, and we separated. Returning, it felt like a dream, and I was as lost as a fool. I received a valuable gift, but the sorrowful feelings were overwhelming! How pitiful, and how impossible to regain! If I had realized there would be separation, why would I have encountered such a captivating beauty in this life!

Shao did not marry again after this point. He submitted gifts to Xuan Chiu as his teacher and was taught the Five Thunder Slash and Examine methods. He traveled back and forth between the Two Zhe regions, exorcising evil spirits and treating illnesses, praying for rain and clear skies, and there were many instances of success. Later, he disappeared. Recently, some people saw him in various places in Zhongnan and Songshan Mountains, and it is believed he attained the Dao. Biography of Luan Luan Zhao Luan Luan, courtesy name Wen Yuan, was the daughter of Zhao Ju from Dongping. In her childhood, her family mixed fragrant powder into her food, and she grew up with a fragrant body, hence she was also called Fragrant Child. She was talented and beautiful, fond of literature, and especially skilled in cutting, sewing, and embroidery. Her father wanted to marry her to the talented scholar Liu Ying, a neighbor, and Luan also deeply wished to marry him, and they agreed but were not yet formally engaged. However, Liu's family fell into misfortune, and their situation deteriorated day by day. Luan's mother regretted it and arranged for her to marry the Miao family. Although the Miao family was wealthy, their children were rustic and uneducated. After marrying, Luan was depressed and unfulfilled. On every festival and holiday, when she saw rare flowers and plants, she would hide her face and weep, then shut herself in her room and sit in sorrow. Scenes that touched her eyes and events that moved her heart, she expressed in poetry, accumulating into volumes, titled 'The Broken Zither Manuscripts.' Three months later, the husband of Luan died, and she returned to her parents' home. The following winter, Liu also lost his wife, so he sent someone to renew their previous agreement and seek to marry Luan again. Zhao Ju and his wife refused. Liu insisted on completing the marriage, because he heard of Luan's virtue and was attracted to her beauty.

Thus, a bead worker named Wang Mama, who frequently visited the Zhao family and was known for her obedience and trustworthiness, was discovered. She was heavily bribed to advise the Zhao family on a matter of marriage. Additionally, a private inquiry was made to Luan to observe her intentions. Wang Mama agreed and went to the Zhao family to say, "I have long harbored a matter, wishing to inform you, but due to many reasons, I have not had the time. Now is the right time, and I cannot delay further. I wonder what your esteemed couple's intentions are?" Zhao asked, "What is it?" Wang Mama replied, "Your daughter is a widow, and her mourning period is almost over. I have heard that Liu intends to renew his previous proposal, but you firmly refuse. I wonder where your plans are leading? At first, he proposed, coming from a distinguished family. Because of his family's financial difficulties, he betrayed your initial intentions. Both sides have since married others, and you have already lost hope. Who would have thought that your daughter lost her husband, and Ying also lost his wife? This seems to be predestined, not accidental. Moreover, Ying's scholarship and literary talent are a hundred times greater than those of the past Mu, and they cannot be mentioned in the same breath. Luan's feelings, I believe, will certainly have no objections. Furthermore, his family is more abundant and prosperous than before, and Ying, being young, will not remain in a difficult situation forever. With a son-in-law like this, how can you bear to abandon him?" Zhao heard this and resolutely agreed. Wang Mama then secretly advised Luan, "Ying admires you, like a rainbow after a great drought. Now that your esteemed father has agreed, the good thing will be successful. However, since you have met a true friend, you cannot lack a word to respond to his deep meaning. Only fear that one day when you follow him, it will be too late to regret." Luan agreed wholeheartedly, but found it difficult to speak, so she wrote a letter to attach to Wang Mama, saying:

I am from a respectable family, raised from childhood with kind teachings, applying cosmetics and remaining secluded within the inner chambers. I handled hemp and worked with silk, carefully following the rules of the inner chamber. I only knew how to sew and mend, never understanding the custom of raising a tray to the level of one’s brow. Heaven granted me honor and splendor, and my family cherished my cleverness and intelligence. My spirit was as clear as ice, my bones as pure as jade; my neck was like a蝤, and my hands like tender shoots. At the height of my beauty, I was chosen for a fine husband, yet who could have foreseen my fate—short-lived, ending up married to a man of low station. Thus, my outstanding talent was wasted, my beauty reduced to that of an ordinary woman. I gathered these sorrows and regrets and expressed them in poetry. On moonlit nights and on days with clear winds, I forced myself to speak, forced myself to smile, like a phoenix paired with a pheasant; what struck the eyes and the heart was like a halcyon following a wild goose. Who could have foreseen that a mediocre man would die young, leaving me a weak and lonely widow? My body was but a lifeless form of earth and wood, the bad condition temporarily absent from my sight; yet my romantic and emotional nature still lingered before the wine cup. I carried only the sorrow of Cai Yan, and forever bore the sorrow of Shu Zhen. I have long accepted being cast aside, and it is an overkindness to seek my hand again, for the sake of fulfilling past kind words and creating a tale of virtue for the future. I sincerely wish to entrust my life to a noble family, to submit myself to a wise official, to draw the deer cart of Huán Jūn and to play the flute of Qín É. I wish to complete my aspirations and grow old together, hoping to follow you in death. Before I have had the chance to serve your presence, I first express my humble heart. May the wise and enlightened forgive me.

The mother congratulated, saying, "It is harmonious! Please offer a hundred gold coins as a reward." Ying replied, "If the matter is successful, how could I be stingy with a hundred gold coins!" He then took out the scroll and gave it to Ying. Ying read it and leaped with joy, saying, "Truly the virtuous and graceful lady, how could I not befriend her with a lute and a zither?" They immediately chose a day to arrange the betrothal and tied the knot. On the night of the wedding, Luan privately told Ying, "I am a widow, but I am still a maiden. You must not be unaware." Ying was surprised and asked, "What do you mean?" Luan said, "In the case of Mu Sheng, who was ill, he could not be close to women. Although he was married to her for four months, there was no consummation, and he eventually died. But this matter is known only to my mother; others do not know it." Ying did not believe her, and Luan asked to verify it, and indeed it was not a mistake. After returning home, Ying showed filial piety to her in-laws, was harmonious with her sisters-in-law, treated servants with kindness first, and assisted her husband with diligence and frugality as the foundation. For neighbors who were poor, she helped them according to her ability; for relatives who visited, she treated them with courtesy. Thus, both inside and outside the family praised her. In her leisure, she played with Ying, recited and discussed the *Shijing* and *Chuci*, and chanted and expressed emotions. Not worth discussing were her beauty like Wu Jiangxian and her poetic thoughts like Cao Zijian. Ying's cousins, who returned from the capital, recorded six playful verses by the scholar Guan, titled *Lanfang*. Ying borrowed them and showed them to Luan, intending to emulate their style, but had not yet finished composing. Luan then composed first and said:

The fluffy, fragrant hair is still damp, its glossy, cold sheen like the wings of a crow and a cicada. A golden phoenix ornament is inserted obliquely at the side, and after finishing her makeup, her husband looks at her with a smile. ── Right, the cloud-like hair The eyebrows are curved like willow leaves, slightly furrowed with a hint of worry, and the deep, clear reflection of the water lily illuminates the frown. Her natural charm does not require the use of kohl, and the spring mountains are painted to bring out the spirit. ── Right, the willow-leaf eyebrows Her lips slightly move, like cherries on a cup, and her breath and saliva lightly carry the fragrance of jasmine. I have seen the famous beauty of Bai Juyi's concubine, Fan Su, whose teeth are like the seeds of a pomegranate, closely set and small. ── Right, the cherry-like lips The fragrance of powder and sweat moistens the strings of the lute, and the soft, melting skin is like white phoenix fat. After bathing, the beloved touches and caresses, and the cool, refreshing sensation is like the coolness of grapes. ── Right, the smooth, creamy skin The fingers are slender and delicate, like spring onions, often hidden in the fragrant silk sleeves. Yesterday, they played the pipa, and clearly, the nails are stained red. ── Right, the graceful fingers The feet are wrapped in thin, light fabric, like spring clouds covering bamboo shoots, and the delicate, beautiful toes are revealed by the evening moon. The long, flowing skirt is like a cluster of butterflies, and when she comes down from the swing, her grace is evident. ── Right, the elegant feet The translation is presented in a polished and natural English style, preserving the poetic imagery, cultural context, and syntactic structure of the original Classical Chinese text.

The following year, during the fifth year of the Zhizheng era (1348), Tian Feng conquered Dongping. Ying lost track of Luan and did not know where she was. Soon after, Mao Gui reconquered Dongzhou and left a pretend commander named Yu, the Left Chancellor, to garrison the area. Yu was quite knowledgeable about the principles of righteousness and issued a public notice to return all captured men and women to their families. Ying heard of this and suspected that Luan might be there, so he rushed into the danger of sharp blades to search for her but was unable to find her. Just as he was in a state of worry and hardship, someone pointed to the nunnery and said to him, "Why not search for her here?" Following the advice, Ying went to the nunnery and indeed saw more than ten women, all tired and imprisoned. Ying asked about Luan's name and whether she was alive or dead. One woman replied, "She was called away several months ago and is not here. She was a virtuous woman. What a pity! What a pity!" Ying then asked, "How do you know all this?" The woman said, "I too was from a good family and was captured. I lived with Zhao for five months. The family members of other households were all defiled and humiliated by the enemy and were immediately released. Only I, Zhao, and a few others here swore to die rather than be defiled, so we were imprisoned. When will we see the light of day again?" After she finished speaking, tears fell like rain. Ying also shed tears and spoke softly to the woman, "Zhao is my wife. I don't know where she is now." The woman said, "I heard that a man named Zhou, the Commander, took her away, and I cannot predict where she is. But when he left, he knew you would surely come to look for her, so he left a letter with me to give to you." She immediately took the letter out from her collar and handed it to Ying, urging him to take it quickly, for fear that the guards would notice and they would be beaten or scolded. Ying opened and read the letter, which was indeed written by his wife. The letter said:

Lady Luan, Since I left home to marry, I have encountered bandits and experienced a life of hardship and wandering. I have suffered greatly, on the brink of death. I have faced many dangers and doubts, but I have managed to preserve my chastity. Heaven and Earth, indeed, have witnessed my plight! If I were to perish, I would choose to hang myself in a ditch. If I were to merge with the vulgar customs, I would show disrespect to the fundamental principles of society. Thus, I have damaged my appearance and barely survived. Though I am like a flower without a master, carried by the wind, and a dog that has lost its home, I still long for my master. I look around in a state of panic and distress, worn out for half a life. My body is barely intact, and both my heart and courage are lost. Whenever I encounter the hardships of a poor and isolated life and the passage of time, I have intense longing and deep sorrow. The wall lamp is half-extinguished, and I have cried until there are no more tears; the sound of battle drums competes with each other, and my soul and spirit fly apart. I have already accepted that I may be covered with fat on the wild grass and stained with blood in the sand and mud. Rather than be fed to crows and vultures, I would not submit myself to dogs and pigs. I emulate the heroic women who threw themselves off cliffs and admire the chaste wives who cut off their own arms. Who could have thought of being displaced again, suddenly hearing news that you are safe. There is a time to redeem me, and I dare not give up life immediately, thus enduring death even more. I am currently in Jinan, Zhou is my surname, and Wanhu is my official title. Originally a Han person, I am relatively good and kind. After you receive this letter, prepare gold and silk to redeem me. Do not delay, for if I am moved at once, I may be transferred elsewhere. A hundred-year marriage, suddenly separated. Trying to gather spilled water, I hope earnestly. What is needed is deep consideration, achieving good plans early, and not making me a cloud that does not return to the overhang. Writing with great sorrow, I do not know what to say.

Upon receiving the letter, Ying once again endured a difficult and arduous journey to reach that place. The ten-thousand-household lord was mustering a large force, with a formidable presence, and thus dared not advance lightly; instead, he cast himself upon the mercy of his neighbors and settled there. After several days, he learned that his wife Luan was still alive, but had no way to communicate with her. So he waited by the gate every day. He saw an old woman fortune-teller who came and went frequently, and he suspected she must be a trusted person in the household. Waiting for her to leave, he secretly followed her home, presented her with a锭 of silver as a gift, and then told her his situation. The old woman said, "The general's wife is jealous, and all the women she has captured are kept in separate rooms. Apart from washing clothes and preparing meals, they are not allowed to leave even for a moment. Recently, some have been returned to their families. If your wife is still here, I will help you succeed." The next day, the old woman secretly went to the residence to inquire, and indeed found Luan, then privately reported back to Ying. Luan secretly handed over a sealed letter to the old woman, who took it out and gave it to Ying, labeling it: *Four Pieces of the Lamenting Flute*. As he read it, Ying wept, and then earnestly asked the old woman to request the lady to redeem Luan. The lady replied, "I have no use for her, and besides, her husband is here—how could I bear to keep her? I will send her back at once." Ying then presented the lady with a pair of pearl ear ornaments and a pair of golden hairpins. The lady immediately called for Luan and sent her with Ying. Thus, the couple departed hand in hand, bowing and taking their leave. The melody of *Four Pieces of the Lamenting Flute* is also recorded here.

At the beginning of my life, there was still no action; after my birth, the great fortune declined. Husband and wife, suddenly separated; father and mother, who knows if they are alive or dead! Wolf smoke rises all around, drums and gongs resound; spears and arrows form a forest, flags are abundant. The people suffer, cities are destroyed; rituals and righteousness are lost, laws are destroyed. My body is lost, far away in the world; my intestines are about to tear, my heart is deeply sorrowful! Mountains can be leveled, rivers can be blocked; my grievances and sorrows are endless! To the right, one beat. Bees and ants gather, tigers and wolves howl; the heart is cruel, the body is foul-smelling. Smoke and dust fill the air, people flee; cold sand is covered with bones, buried in weeds. Losing our homes in chaos, we are deeply hurt; duty is heavy, life is light, like a feather. I swear to dedicate my life, hoping not to be tainted; looking up to the sky and down to the earth, I am alone and troubled.

### Right Second Section Abandoning the wise and virtuous while pursuing the wicked and foolish, wandering east and west, ultimately finding no place of rest. Taking pleasure in greed and lust, and finding entertainment in killing, plundering wherever one goes, leaving ruin in one's wake. Opening tombs and burning down houses, enslaving the weak and vulnerable, sacrificing one's life for righteousness, giving up one's insignificant body. Who says that women are not as capable as men? ### Right Third Section Wherever I walk or sit, I think of my hometown. From the ends of the earth, I cannot see my family! My aunt and uncle have died, and my family has been destroyed, fleeing and barely surviving, being driven away! My husband and I are separated, when will we be reunited? Thankfully, my humble body has been spared from dishonor. Who is the righteous person who will spend gold and jade? Singing on the road, I redeem my own body. ### Right Fourth Section Ying and Luan have reunited, and they discussed together: "The world is in chaos, people cannot survive. Although we have been reunited, our future is uncertain. It would be better to flee to the depths of the forest and ravines, temporarily avoiding the dust and waiting for better times." They hid in the foothills of Culaishan, the husband plowed in front, the wife followed with planting, sharing both hardships and joys, treating each other with the respect of guests, equally admirable as Jiqie, Liang Hong, Pang Gong, and Wang Ba. Nearby and distant villagers were influenced by their example. One day, Ying left the city to fetch grain and was captured by bandits. They said, "We have heard of your name for a long time! We will send you to General Tian, who will give you an official position, and you will not worry about poverty or lack of wealth." Ying stared in anger and shouted, "Head-chopping bandit! I would not follow you in rebellion!" The bandits were angry and killed him on the road.

Neighbors rushed to tell Luan of the tragedy, and Luan ran and wept, carrying her body home. He personally licked her blood and dressed her body with his own hands. He then piled firewood and set Ying’s body ablaze. When the flames grew fierce, Luan also threw himself into the fire and died. Those who witnessed the event were shocked and filled with awe, saying, “How can the ancient term *chastity heroine* be adequate to describe her?” After the fire was extinguished, the neighbors gathered her remains and buried them, carving a stone tablet to mark her tomb, inscribed: “The Tomb of the Two Chaste Ones.” The gentleman said, “Loyalty and righteousness are the great moral boundaries for people; scholars and gentlemen have spoken of them at length. Yet when faced with danger or hardship, few are able to truly uphold them. Luan, a secluded woman, was able to preserve her virtue and remain unstained in times of chaos and separation. In the end, the husband died for loyalty, the wife for righteousness. Only because she read widely and understood propriety, and because her nature was good, could the principles of heaven and humanity not be erased. Those in the world who carry their pipa to other boats, upon hearing of Luan’s virtue, should indeed feel ashamed!”

凤尾草记 During the Hongwu period, there was a man named Long Sheng, originally from Jiankang. His distant ancestor served as an official in the Song capital and followed Empress Dowager Shèngmǔ southward when she relocated the court, leaving his family in Jiangyou. His descendants multiplied, and generations have guarded the classics. Long Sheng was the eighth in his lineage; at the age of six or seven, the elders taught him poetry, and he could recite it immediately. At the age of nine, he understood how to compose couplets, and his five-character and seven-character quatrain poems were all commendable. People regarded him as intelligent. He had an aunt who married into the Zu family, and she particularly loved him. He visited his aunt's house frequently. The Zu family had half-siblings who lived together but had separate hearths. His elder brother died, and only his sister-in-law, Lady Lian, and two sons and three daughters remained. The eldest daughter and second daughter were married, but only the youngest daughter was unmarried and exceptionally beautiful, being three years older than Long Sheng. Although young, Long Sheng was sharp and intelligent yet obedient and cautious, did not like to play, and was good at reading people's intentions. Therefore, when the Zu family heard that Long Sheng was coming, they were all happy. The youngest daughter also regarded him as a brother and no longer avoided him.

The daughter's mother heard that the son-in-law was praised by his mother-in-law for his diligence in learning and deeply desired to marry him. The daughter also looked at him with affection. In the courtyard, there was a phoenix tail plant that had already been there for a hundred years, and the son-in-law recited poetry beside it. Seeing no one around, the daughter came out to the son-in-law under the phoenix tail plant and said, "The grandmother heard that the mother-in-law praised you for your intelligence and wanted to make a good match. I also wish to be your wife, entrusting the mother-in-law to arrange it. But I am unsure if your parents agree. If fate brings us together and we can be husband and wife, I will have no regrets even if I die. Otherwise, I will only marry a merchant's son or a farmer's son, even if the house is filled with gold and jade, and the fields stretch far and wide, I would not want it." The son-in-law replied, "If I can have you as my partner, it will be enough to satisfy my life." He then pointed to the phoenix tail plant and swore, "If the matter succeeds, the plant will bloom and bear fruit; if it fails, the roots will wither and the leaves will die." After the oath, they parted. The son-in-law stayed at the grandmother's house, and everyone was pleased with him, especially the daughter who admired him greatly. She once personally served tea to the son-in-law, and he took the tea, playfully saying, "The tea has been drunk, so there is no worry that the matter will not succeed." The family heard about it but did not question it.

The bride's mother and her sister-in-law were at odds, appearing to encourage the marriage while secretly hindering it. As a result, the groom's parents were hesitant, and the bride was unaware of the situation. The groom told the bride, "Since you are not yet ready to marry, I will not immediately propose. I will discuss this with my mother, and we will only proceed once you agree to be my wife." The bride's family was poor, and she had never worn fine silk or applied makeup. She wore a simple pine hairpin and a cotton skirt, which were always clean, and even her foot bindings were as white as snow. She was gentle and submissive, and her skills in weaving and sewing were unmatched in her clan. Her two sisters-in-law were very jealous of her, but she did not hold it against them. The groom admired her character and grew more affectionate towards her, but finding a good matchmaker was difficult, and his mother did not offer much support. Both sides delayed, and the matter dragged on for a long time. After the groom came of age, he pursued a career in government service, and the bride's family rarely visited. However, the bride always thought of him, and only her mother knew her feelings. She advised her, "I will send someone to talk about your marriage. We will have a decision soon. Do not worry, as it will only damage your appearance." After some time, the groom returned, though he lived with his mother-in-law, his thoughts were always with the bride. He stayed for a few days, and both sisters-in-law returned to their parents' homes. The bride was alone on the upper floor, spinning thread. Below was a deep alley that connected to the back garden, with half of it paved with brick steps. The groom returned from the garden and heard the sound of the bride spinning. He ran straight to her. The bride saw him and her face was filled with joy. She stopped spinning, greeted him, and sat opposite him, continuing to spin and talk. She told him her birth year, asking him to calculate if they were compatible. She also spoke in great detail about her family affairs. The groom was moved by her sincerity and composed a poem to give her. The poem said: [The poem would follow here, but since it is not provided in the source text, it is omitted in this translation.]

In the deep recesses of the winding railing, a single flower blooms, its rich color never knowing the grace of dew. The pure white petals gather like a thousand jade petals, and the fragrant flesh is red and shines like a six-zhu silk. The golden bell has an intention to frequently protect it, but the embroidered canopy, though beautiful, cruelly hides it. Rely on the eastern king to exert strength, and when it opens to people, do not let it be wrong. The woman did not read much, knowing only a few characters. She said to the man, "You should explain it to me so that I can understand." He explained the meaning step by step. The woman smiled and said, "One day when I serve in the inner chambers, you must teach me. Though I am foolish and dim, I will surely learn it after some time." The man replied, "Women are particularly clever. With your wise heart, learning it will be easy." He then composed a poem on her behalf, saying: (Translation of the poem would follow here if provided in the original text.)

Deeply grateful that the bright spring light has colored her so intensely, I await the gentle east wind to blow it open. I give rise to sorrow as evening dew condenses into pearl-like tears, and most fear that the spring cold will mar her jade-like beauty. When the tender buds are broken, they scatter like butterfly dust; where the fragrant heart is broken, it leaves a spot of scarlet red. If the golden plate and splendid house are suitable for offering, may she soon enter the twelve-tiered carved railings. The young man then carefully explained the meaning of the poem. The woman said, “I have often heard of your quick wit and eloquence; now I see it is indeed as they said, and I admire you even more!” She gazed at him for a long time and said, “Your spirit and bearing are certainly not those of an ordinary person; you will surely rise to wealth and honor. I wish to entrust my humble self to you, not out of any other reason, but because my father died early, my mother is growing old, my eldest brother serves in officialdom, my second brother is trapped in bureaucratic duties, and my two sisters-in-law are harsh and cruel—these are things you already know well. If only I may escape from such a harsh fate and find refuge in your embrace, even if you have no official rank, you will not be a lady of rank, but you will not lose the status of a wife of a scholar or gentleman. Should I ever fall into the hands of a vulgar man, I would rather die! Please, then, think of me and consider my fate.” At first, the young man had been pleased by her beauty, but he had not expected her to be so virtuous and wise. From that moment on, he was earnestly devoted to the idea of marriage, fearing only that it might be delayed. Soon after, the woman’s elder sister indeed fell due to corruption in office, and the family’s fortunes also declined. The young man’s parents had no intention of forming an alliance, and so they politely declined, leaving him in despair. In private, he composed a long song to send to her. The song said:

I was once a child, my hair tied in a topknot, laughing and playing with you on the bed with bamboo horses. Holding green plums, we played together, your body as pure and delicate as a lotus flower. You admired my intelligence and my dedication to writing, my articles as strong and healthy as a purple steed. Your hair styled like wind and mist, your lips dyed red, we met many times by the phoenix tail. In the deep and secluded inner room, my delicate fingers worked with fine threads. If the matchmaker does not come, what can be done? The red sash and the heart's promise were broken! The embroidered robe and the military tent were separated by great distances, I did not understand the soul's departure like Zhang Qian. Do you know whom you are betrothed to? I passed the imperial examination in the golden hall. Looking back in my dreams, my tears fell like snow on Mount Wu. One day, the girl's mother left her at the home of her relatives, and her two sisters-in-law provoked her, causing a big commotion. The girl, deep in her inner room, was inherently kind, dared not speak, and could not curse, yet she was overwhelmed with anger. Moreover, the Qin alliance was suddenly broken, leaving her desolate and weary, wandering aimlessly. That night, she hanged herself in the upper room. Her mother returned, wept bitterly, washed and dressed her body herself, and found a embroidered bag on her chest, containing a secret note with a plum-colored paper, which turned out to be a poem I had sent her. Her mother honored her wish and placed the poem in the coffin. I heard of her death, excused myself to visit my aunt, and went to mourn her. Upon arrival, it was like a pearl sinking and a jade breaking, a flower falling and a gem shattering, about to be buried. My tears flowed like rain, I was heartbroken, sent her back to her burial place, covered the grave, and returned.

After several years, the man indeed attained a high-ranking and important position, becoming famous and influential at the time. Although he took other wives and concubines, his feelings for the woman did not change. He often discussed ghosts and spirits with the Taoist immortal Master Zhang Wuwei, and accidentally mentioned the woman's affairs. The immortal saw that the man was deeply concerned and wrote a petition to heaven to rescue her. After several days, the man dreamed of the woman, who said, "I left the world more than twenty years ago. The underworld checked the records and found that I should have given birth to three sons and lived to sixty years old, but the number was not completed, and I died prematurely. I was reborn as a woman to complete my past karma. But yesterday, I was rescued by the immortal's spiritual power, and a heavenly decree suddenly came down. I have now been reborn as a boy in the Hu family in the city of Luoyang, Henan. I am deeply grateful for your love, which I will never forget, even in death. But I regret that I have nothing to repay you. But you are now in a position of wealth and honor, holding the highest office, enjoying great fortune and longevity, and having many prosperous descendants." After finishing her words, she bowed and left. After walking several steps, she turned back and said, "Take good care of yourself, and I will go on forever!" Suddenly, she disappeared. After waking up, he felt deeply moved. He sent someone to the woman's home to see the phoenix tail, which had been dead for several years. He then composed the "Lament for the Phoenix Tail" song, saying:

A Plant Named Phoenix Tail There is a plant called Phoenix Tail, sown by immortals in the mythical Dan Mountain. In the world, all flowers avoid its fragrance, and it can be compared to coral trees. Its appearance is extremely similar to the tail of a phoenix, and it is given a good name, named after the phoenix. On the sea, it moves slowly, and its dew is like pearls. The sound of a flute playing under the colorful clouds stops, and it remains graceful and elegant, maintaining purity and tranquility. Its leaves are carved like glass, and its stem is like jade. Occasionally, the phoenix with its strong wings descends, and its five-colored patterns brightly reflect. Sunlight shines through the leaves like gold, and a gentle breeze fills the forest during summer. It does not have the appearance of peach and plum blossoms, but in its later years, it is as steadfast as pine and cypress trees. In a splendid hall, it sways with new green, and it has met with flying jade in the green shade. Leaning on it, one is not allowed to briefly steal its fragrance, and pointing to the tree, one only hopes to eventually become a match. How could one know that all things are not truly real, and its subtle fragrance and good quality turn to dust? The spiritual root withers over a hundred years, and the beautiful color perishes in three springs. The phoenix came here yesterday, and the phoenix tail died on the platform for playing music. The wild tangerine is green, and the peacock screen leans with purple soil flowers. Feeling the time and touching the past, there is endless regret. Green feathers and jade clusters forever rest. Ruined steps and collapsed walls, crickets mourn the moon. Desolate smoke and old trees, birds sing in autumn. Replanting and spring blooming again, a broken mirror and separated hairpin, forever separated. Because of singing about the phoenix tail, expressing deep feelings, leaving for future generations to lament.

The Wuping Record of Strange Events Qi Zhonghe, whose given name was Xie, was from Zhangzhou. Originally the son of a wealthy family, he had a basic education and was quite skilled in writing. However, he was a bold and unrestrained man, spending money as freely as if it were dirt. In the year Renchen of the Zhizheng era, during the Red Turban Rebellion, his family's estate was completely destroyed. He then wandered east and west, living off the generosity of others. He often stayed at the home of Xiang Zijian in Wuping as a guest scholar. Xiang Zijian was originally humble, but suddenly rose to prominence, wanting to adorn his family's reputation. Therefore, he insisted on marrying into noble families, showing off to others. He would marry the poor and unprosperous members of prominent clans, who admired their wealth, while they coveted his riches. Letters, invitations, marriage contracts, and other documents were all embellished by Zhonghe, and those who did not know him thought he came from a true gentry family. In the fifth year of the Hongwu era, Xiang Zijian died, and his two sons, Rongke and Guike, made a grand funeral arrangement, burying him in the Jianting Mountain, fifty miles from their residence. Zhonghe wrote an account of his life and asked Song Lian, the historian, to compose an epitaph for him. He also built the Guquan Temple beside the tomb, which was grand and impressive, resembling a pavilion. He set aside two hundred acres of land to provide food for the monks and requested that Nanhua Benru, a nun, manage the temple's affairs. The imperial scholar Wu Bozong from Jinxixi recorded this. Zhonghe would travel back and forth, and the temple was conveniently located on the route, so he would always stay there when passing by. That year, there was a minor official named Xiaogan who went to Fuzhou and stayed as a guest scholar for several years. Later, Guike was appointed as a scholar-official and became the deputy magistrate of Jiaxing Prefecture. When Japanese pirates landed on the coast, he failed to report it, was punished, and died in the prison of the Ministry of Punishment. His family property was confiscated, the temple's land was taken over by the government, and the monks all dispersed.

In the year Gengwu Yichou of the Hongwu era, Zonghe returned home and went to visit Xiang, arriving at the hermitage in the evening. He stayed for the night, unaware that Xiang had died and the hermitage had been abandoned. He entered the main hall, which was silent with no human sound, and looked around the monk's rooms, noting that some were open and some were closed. Finally, he entered a room where a monk was sitting on a couch. The monk heard footsteps and asked, "Who is there?" Zonghe identified himself, and the monk, in the dark, replied, "Then you are an old acquaintance, please sit!" Zonghe asked the monk his name, and the monk responded, "This mountain monk once had an illusionary body. You have seen it, but have you forgotten it already?" Zonghe did not understand what the monk was talking about and asked again, "Where are the other monks?" The monk said, "I happened to attend a water and land feast at a layperson's house. Only this mountain monk has been suffering from rheumatism for a long time and cannot get out of bed, so I am here at the hermitage. I regret that the novice monks are all out, and I did not expect you to come, so there is no tea or offering." Zonghe informed the monk that he had not eaten, and the monk said, "There are several handfuls of leftover beans on the table. If you do not mind, please take and eat them." Zonghe, being very hungry, picked them up and chewed them. He then asked about Xiang's movements.

The monk said, "Therefore, there is no harm." Zonghe was tired and wanted to sleep. The monk said, "There are several guests here who come every night to chat with me in idleness. They will soon arrive, and I fear it may make you uncomfortable." Zonghe asked, "Who are they?" The monk replied, "They are all from nearby respectable families, and some are related to the Xiang family." Zonghe was pleased and said, "If that is the case, how fortunate!" Soon after, two people entered first, followed by five more. The monk said, "Today, some former guests of the Xiang family have come to visit and will stay overnight. Please do not be surprised!" Zonghe then asked the guests for their names and reputations. The first group said, "We are Shi Zijian and Mao Yuanying." The second group said, "We are Jin Zhaoxiang, Zeng Waha, Pi Yili, Shangguan Gai, and Mu Ruyu." Zonghe apologized, saying, "There are no candles or lamps, so I dare not perform the ritual. Please do not hold it against me." The guests responded, "Since we are all like one family, what blame could there be?" They then joined the monk in a discussion, arguing as if a river were hanging, unceasingly, and deeply attaining the truth of Buddhism.

The monk said, "You have long attained the joy of meditation; you should avoid sharp, pointed questions. However, since scholars are present, why not temporarily stop idle talk and instead compose better verses to provide entertainment for a clear, joyful night?" The group replied, "Agreed!" Subsequently, one of them began to recite: "Once renowned for the four treasures of the study, it boasts eyes sharper than gold stars. Fine paper and calligraphy scrolls are constant companions, while round mirrors and square zithers are skillfully crafted. The fallen tower of the copper bird becomes the beak of a phoenix, and the jade toad spits water with a dragon's scent. Do not underestimate my dull and long-lived self; I have once written Buddhist scriptures for Vimalakirti." The original poem by Yuan Ying said:

Early appointed to a high office serving under Emperor Qin Shi Huang, I once met Jiang Yan in a dream. Praising the skill of Meng Tian from the Qin dynasty and Lu Ying from Wu, the brush dipped in ink, the mist of fragrance wet, the cat's whisker pen point filled with rich red. Now, I have won the right to stay in an empty hall, growing old in a temple as a bald monk. Zhaohang's poem says: My body is broken, my face blackened, my eyes like sand, abandoned in the dust of the world, a wild monk's home. The monk has been sick several times, ready to boil medicine, visitors always make tea. Without opportunity, there is no need to cook, impermanence teaches the passage of time. Once fiery, now silent, do not boast about the cold and heat to others. Waihe's poem says: Poor and without shelter, who can I rely on? A weak and scattered body has long felt old! Confucius, despite hunger, did not dare to be angry, Fan Dan, lacking rice, would not refuse hunger. When I fell to the ground, I did not need to care, today, covered in dust, I cannot cook. The firewood smoke dissipates, the ashes grow cold, the steam rises from the stove, what is the point? Yili's poem says: My body is like a light, too easily blown away, used to cover the simple monk and the praise of the master. The fabric tears not because of the child's bad sleep, the silk wears through because the craftsman is hard to find. Dust accumulates for a long time, no one cleans, lice and fleas multiply, lacking fire to dry. Half decayed, returned to the worms and mice, it is known that form and appearance are inherently empty. Shangguan Gai's poem says: Common people use lacquer, nobles use vermilion, the living hate, the dead need. Unless I fly to immortality, I have no use for my body, if I am to be transformed, I must still use it. Able to preserve the bones of heroes, able to preserve the bodies of beautiful women. I advise the busy people of the world, do not hesitate to spend a hundred gold to prepare in advance.

As Ru Yu said in his poem: With long, ancient whiskers and a bony, rugged appearance, my heart and belly are empty, neither increasing nor decreasing. Early on, I realized that having a body entails suffering; how can I bear it when there are no Buddhas or monks to guide me? I have frequently stayed in a hermitage and am now on the verge of decay, yet I have long desired to go to the Longmen Grottoes but have not been able to do so. Like a decaying piece of wood and a dried-up skeleton, I still experience the taste of Zen. A single night of clear conversation is more valuable than hearing scriptures. After finishing, he clapped his hands and laughed loudly, as if no one else were around. Suddenly, the wind dispersed the clouds, and moonlight streamed through the door, faintly revealing the appearances of others. Some were short and stocky, some were thin with sharp heads, some had black faces and one very long arm, and some wore black hats and were extremely short. Those who walked slowly did so with grace and wore felt robes, while those who stood upright did so and leaned against the wall. The last person was an elderly man with a neck that seemed to have scales, which surprised Zhong He. As he was about to closely examine him, a monk suddenly said, "Mr. Qingfeng Luobensù has arrived." Everyone rose to greet him. From a distance, they saw an old man in white robes and a bamboo staff, with a leisurely and elegant demeanor. His sleeves fluttered as he walked, swaying gracefully. He bowed to the guests and said, "Did my friends enjoy their poetry tonight?" Yuan Ying replied, "Why did you arrive so late, Mr.?" Each recited their own works and presented them. Mr. Qingfeng said, "Your self-praise is good, but it is not without fault as it may be strange to outsiders." Yi Li said, "The guest is not yet old, but they will eventually be in the same carriage as Mr. Shàngguān, and there is no harm in that." Mr. Qingfeng said to the monk, "Why is my teacher reluctant to compose?" The monk replied, "I will compose together with you when you arrive." Then, he loudly recited:

Weary of seeing the red fire of the world-ending conflagration in Jambudvipa, I stand alone in the desolate mountains, guarding the palace of the transformed. The three thousand worlds have all become illusion, and the hundred and twenty rivers and mountains are all empty. Moss grows wild on the robes, grieving the destruction of the Buddha; the monk's bed remains untidy, and he laughs in lazy apathy. Hard to find are those who have fled to Chan practice beyond worldly concerns, and rare indeed to meet the elders who have joined a society by the bridge. A fierce tiger often roams beneath the lotus seat, and strange birds often dwell within the embroidered banners. Green moss covers the courtyard, freshly washed by rain; yellow leaves drift through the shrine as a sudden wind arises. A pair of Vajras bear faces like snail carvings, and several Arhats have rats piercing through their chests. Damaged scriptures, missing characters—each scroll is in disrepair; ancient artifacts have become spiritual, each one mighty. The wide hall’s windows open, letting the moonlight remain; the idle door, unfastened, is sealed by the clouds. In vain do I pity the withered bones of those who once wandered through mist and clouds; do not let the decay of wood and stone stir sorrow in you. On this fine night, could I have expected a gathering of noble guests? To sing in quiet harmony is to meet with old friends. A few beans remain on the table, filling my hungry belly; deep soot on the beams stains my sickly face. To walk into the wheel of samsara is to return to decay; there is no need to toil and suffer for the weak and worn.庄严未必成三昧, 游戏何妨运六通. When the plum ripens, the nature of perfect enlightenment is revealed; where the pine branch lies fallen, the trace of the past is remembered. To know the full meaning of drawing in the waters of the Western River, listen only to the morning cock and the evening bell.

The Gentle Wind Master highly praised its marvels and also sang, saying: The mountains and rivers of Lin Ting are only spoken of in Wuping. The layered peaks stand tall and graceful, while the many waters flow clear. The Azure Dragon opens auspicious soil, while the White Tiger opens a fine tomb; the Vermilion Bird aligns with divination, while the Black Warrior welcomes auspicious signs. The shape encircles and embraces, winding around in harmony, indeed a creation of heaven and earth. At that time, the two filial sons of the Xiang family buried their father here and guarded the tomb. They rebuilt the monastery and invited the True Master from afar to be the abbot. The tenant farmers provided a hundred stones of grain, and the bells and drums rang three times a day, raising the sound of chanting. How many years could pass, yet it was already like this? The horse whinnies in the wind, and the camel cries in the rain. The land of the monastery was taken by the government, and the monks were left to mingle with the laypeople. After the donor departed, the temple became long neglected, and the young men came at night to recite poetry: Mao, with his sharp talent, stands out; Shi, with his steady character, acts in a balanced way; Ru, like a fool, guards the pillar, but his hair has fallen out and he is withered; Zhao, who once held power, has lost his influence, and his glory has faded, leaving only desolation; the old man of the Pi family wears torn rags, and his clothes are full of dirt and lice; the lowly and scattered ones are few and hold little influence, while the officials are cruel and fierce, making people fear them. Alas, I am a wanderer called the Gentle Wind, and in my old age, I still maintain the appearance of the Jade Void. All my life, I have swept away the heat of the world, but my relatives are still in Hangzhou. A foolish monk, poor and sick, has abandoned his wanderings; a withered tree, cold and lifeless, rests upon my body.
