After a brief while, the moon set and its light faded, the distant rooster crowed to announce the dawn, and the guests suddenly dispersed, not knowing where they had gone. Zhonghe stepped outside and saw a desolate, empty hermitage. Returning to search for the sick monk, he found only a clay statue. Looking at the date inscribed on the back, he realized it was the statue he had seen when he first stayed in the hermitage, now worn away. He finally understood the mountain monk's words, "This is an illusionary body, which you have witnessed." Entering another room, he found only a broken inkstone propped against the door and a worn-out brush lying on the ground, with mouse droppings piled up on the desk. He thought about the leftover beans he had eaten, realizing they must have been the source. There was also a worn-out quilt and an old fan, a steamer covered in dust and almost broken, a pot with no handle and half worn through, a wooden fish hanging from the pillar, and a coffin lid leaning against the wall. Zhonghe was greatly frightened and ran out of the door. Walking for miles, he finally came across a house and sought refuge there. The host said, "This place is deserted and full of strange occurrences. Where did you stay last night?" Zhonghe explained everything. The host replied, "How dangerous it was! Your life was at risk."

He was then told: "The Xiang family has suffered misfortune; their tomb has collapsed and been destroyed. The family entrusted a piece of wood for a coffin there, which was recently split and used as firewood, leaving only the lid behind. The stones and animal hair you encountered, are they not inkstones and brushes? The metal and clay, are they not pots and steamers? Leather, when treated with respect, is inscribed with characters; wood, when treated as if it were foolish, becomes a wooden fish. The official's cover became a coffin, and the plain cloth became an old fan. These items you saw have been turned upside down and have caused confusion. When they say there is a relative of the Xiang family, they are referring to the coffin. The coffin is an old possession of the Xiang family, hence the term 'relative.' Zhonghe remained silent, extremely nervous and fearful. He returned home that day and indeed fell seriously ill. Recalling the words 'I will travel with the official,' he realized he would not recover and refused medical treatment. His wife and children urged him repeatedly, but Zhonghe said, 'Life and death are predetermined, and the objects have already foretold it. Taking medicine and seeking medical treatment is only self-torture!' After another half a month, he finally died. Alas! Can one not call Zhonghe a broad-minded and open-hearted man?"

The Biography of Qiong Nu Qiong Nu, surnamed Wang, courtesy name Runzhen, was from Changshan. At the age of two, her father died. Her mother, surnamed Tong, brought Qiong Nu to marry into the wealthy family of Shen Biguai. Shen had no children of his own and loved Qiong Nu more than his own child. At the age of fourteen, Qiong Nu was excellent at poetry and lyrics and also proficient in music theory. Virtue, speech, appearance, and talent, all four were present in Qiong Nu. People from far and near competed to offer marriage proposals for her. At that time, in the same village, there were Xu Congdao and Liu Junyu, who proposed marriage especially urgently. Xu Congdao was of noble lineage but poor, while Liu Junyu was from a humble background but suddenly rich. Xu Congdao's son was named Tao Lang, and Liu Junyu's son was named Han Lao. Both were elegant and well-groomed in appearance, and they were the same age as Qiong Nu. Shen Biguai wanted to agree to Liu Junyu but looked down on his lowly lineage; he wanted to agree to Xu Congdao but worried about his poor family situation. He hesitated and wavered, unable to make a decision.

One day, he consulted with knowledgeable relatives, and they devised a plan: "Just seek a good son-in-law, without considering other factors." Mr. Biqu asked, "But how can we know if he is good?" They replied, "It's easy! Prepare a feast, especially invite two young men, and also invite elders who are good at discernment to secretly observe them. First, observe their demeanor and capacity; second, test their literary skills. Choose the best one and follow him. What difficulty is there in choosing a son-in-law?" Mr. Biqu agreed wholeheartedly. On a flower-filled morning in February, he opened a banquet to entertain guests. All the well-known scholars and gentlemen from the village gathered in the courtyard. Junyu and Congdao also brought their sons. Despite his composed and dignified appearance, Old Han was somewhat stiff in his greetings and bows. In contrast, Tao Lang had fresh and clear features, spoke in a scholarly manner, dressed simply, and moved with ease.

Among the guests was one named Gengyun, the clan leader of the Shen family, renowned for knowing people. Upon seeing the two young men, he silently discerned their merits and demerits. He then publicly declared to the crowd, "My nephew will surely be noble, and he has a daughter of marriageable age. Mister Xu and Mister Liu, who wish to seek a good match, are both excellent in character and appearance, but it is uncertain which one will be the match." The nephew stood up and replied, "If this matter is handled by the elders, it will be good." Gengyun said, "In ancient times, there were customs such as shooting at screens, pulling threads, and setting up mats, all of which were used to select a husband. I differ from these methods." He then called the two young men forward and pointed to the four paintings hanging on the wall: "Spring Morning with Early Flowers," "Night Moon and Late Sleep," "Moon in Hand from Water," and "Scent of Flowers on the Clothes." He said, "Younger Brother, please express your thoughts and try to compose verses. Winning the eyes or taking the clothes, all depends on this one attempt." How unfortunate that Han Laosheng, living in a wealthy household, is lazy about poetry and books, and upon hearing the command, looked around for a long time without responding. Tiaolang, however, calmly took up his brush and completed the task in a moment. When he presented it, Gengyun praised it repeatedly. The poem read: The dawn breaks through the petals of the peach blossom, Heavy dew falls like snow from the fragrant tea plant. Vivid purple shades the embroidered window, Soft red hues cast a slanting shadow on the swing ropes. The sound of the well wheel startles me awake from my dream, I rise to comb my hair, but have no time to stand before the dressing table. Laughing, I call the maid to hold the bright candle, First to see if the crabapple blossoms have opened. Right: Spring Morning with Early Flowers.

**Right: Love for the Moonlit Night, Delayed Sleep** Her fragrant shoulders slightly drooped as she loosened her golden hairpin; the heavy gate stood silent, locked deep into the night. The pale moonlight, just emerging from the coastal margin, had already pierced through the cracks in the crimson curtain. She walked back and forth in silence, leaning on the balustrade, as the stars of the Three Stars and the Dipper set, and the cold wind and dew grew heavy. A child softly called her to return to bed, yet she lingered behind the rose trellis, still gazing. **Right: Love for the Moonlit Night, Delayed Sleep** The silver pond overflowed, and the moonlight poured forth. Chang’e descended into the realm of Fengyi. The shimmering pearls on the water seemed almost touchable, and the shape of the moon, like the tips of a rabbit’s ears, was clearly visible, as if one could count them. The beauty herself drew water to wash her hands, pale as spring onions, when suddenly she was startled to see the moon reflected in her palm. Her companions by the stream laughed and spoke together, as if her fingertips had lifted the Palace of the Cold Moon from the water. **Right: Holding the Moon in Hand** At the sound of the bell, the east wind blew fiercely; she stood long by the cluster of red and purple flowers. With her white hands, she reached for a branch, fearing it might prick her, and with her golden lotus-shaped shoes, she hesitated, reluctant to step on the damp moss. After picking the fragrant blossoms, she closed the fragrant hall, and the rich, sweet scent filled the embroidered room. Bees and butterflies fluttered in through the window, flying back and forth around her silk robe. **Right: The Scent of Flowers on Her Clothes** When Junyu saw Han Lao and attempted to speak, he was at a loss, and this greatly shamed him. So, father and son left the banquet without finishing their meal. Then, all those present united in praise, declaring Shaolang to be excellent, and thus the proposal for his marriage was decided. Within a month, a date was chosen to send the betrothal gifts. Soon after, because of his status as a beloved son-in-law, they wished for frequent visits, and so he was invited to reside in a house to study and advance his learning.

Tong's family member was slightly unwell, and Tao entered to inquire about the illness. While Qiongnu was attending to her mother with medicine and soup, she was unaware of Tao's arrival and was unable to avoid him, leading to their meeting at the mother's bedside. Tao, upon seeing her, was struck by her exceptional beauty. He secretly felt delighted and enclosed a sheet of red paper, sending a maid to deliver it to Qiongnu. When she opened it, she found it blank. With a smile, she composed a poem in response to Tao: "Scarlet sunset paper illuminates the face with a blush, What is the matter, jade man, with too much emotion? Elegance is not without good verses, But the two characters of longing cannot be written." Tao, holding the poem, boasted about it to Hanlao. Hanlao, however, was jealous of Tao's growing affection for Qiongnu and accused him of being too emotional. He then falsely accused Tao of wrongdoing, leading to Tao and his family being exiled to Liaoyang and Shenzhen, respectively. At the time of their departure, they were heartbroken, and those who witnessed the scene could not help but shed tears. They then parted ways, and news of each other ceased. Later, Tong's family fell into decline, and only Tong's mother and daughter remained, living in a humble inn by the roadside, selling wine to passersby. Although they were in hardship, Qiongnu's beauty had faded, but her youthful grace remained extraordinary. A Wu commander, admiring her, wished to marry her as a concubine, but Tong refused, as she had already been promised to another.

Wu, knowing the reason, sent a matchmaker to say to her: "Your husband, Xu Lang, has been guarding the borderlands of Liaodong; his fate is uncertain. Even if he is safe and sound, how could he possibly come here to fulfill this marriage? Why not return to my noble family, where you and your mother can enjoy comfort and luxury, and not waste your life in vain?" Qiong Nu firmly refused. Wu then sent an old matchmaker to convey his message, and also threatened her with the authority of the government. Tang Shi, fearing for her safety, discussed the matter with Qiong Nu, saying: "Since we left Shao, five years have passed, and we have been separated by vast distances, as if we were on opposite ends of the earth. True to the saying, 'You are in the North Sea, and I am in the South Sea, and our paths will never cross.' Your situation seems utterly hopeless. Moreover, your father has recently passed away, and we are now living in a foreign land, where powerful families look upon us with suspicion. They are forcing us to accept this marriage proposal. As orphans and widows, what can we do to resist?" Qiong Nu wept and said: "The Xu family has suffered misfortune, and it began with me. If I were to leave and marry another, it would be unjust to abandon them. Humans are distinguished from animals because they possess integrity and trustworthiness. To abandon old affections and form new ones is to forget integrity. If one forgets integrity, they are no better than dogs and pigs. I would rather die than do such a thing!" The colorful phoenixes have parted, the literary mandarin ducks have scattered, and red clouds block the way to TianTai. The old courtyard, with its painted beams covered in dust! In vain, the swallows from the heavenly capital, facing the east wind, seem to lament their sorrow! The master has left, but the house, burdened with the weight of his kindness, returns to its emptiness. The weary woman from Jingyang, without meeting Liu Yi, finds it difficult to write a letter. Lamenting the loss of the golden hairpin and the precious mirror! The young man from the distant land of Liaoyang has departed; when will he return? The lilac tree, holding its flowers until death, would it ever open its blossoms to another?

That night, she attempted to hang herself in her room. Her mother noticed and rescued her, and it took a long time for her to regain consciousness. Wu the commander heard of this and became angry. He ordered his subordinates to smash her brewing equipment and drive her away to another residence, intending to humiliate and torment her. At the time, there was an old postal official named Du, also from Changshan. During the days when Shen Bigui was still alive, they had gotten along well. Pitying Tong's loneliness and misery, Du lent her a room in the posthouse where she could settle down. One day, several guests dressed in military attire arrived at the posthouse. Du asked where they had come from, and they replied, "We are low-ranking officers from a garrison in Liaodong, sent to recruit troops in the southern seas. We are temporarily staying here for the night." Coincidentally, Tong happened to be standing by the curtain, and among them was a young man who was especially honest and cautious, unlike a typical soldier. He looked at her several times, and his sad and pitiful expression was evident. Tong was moved and immediately went out to ask, "Who are you?" He replied, "My name is Xu Tao. I am from Changshan in Zhejiang. When I was young, my father once arranged for me to marry the daughter of Shen Bigui, a fellow townsman. However, the marriage did not take place due to family matters. Shen was exiled to the southern seas, and I was stationed in eastern Liaodong. We have not heard from each other for several years. I just entered the posthouse and saw your appearance, which greatly resembled my late mother. That is why I cannot help but feel sad, but I mean no harm." Tong asked again, "Where is the Shen family now? What is the name of the girl?" He said, "Her name is Qiongnu, also known as Runzhen. When we were betrothed, she was fourteen years old. Now, she should be nineteen. However, I have forgotten the state and city where they reside, making it difficult to find her." Tong then went inside and told Qiongnu. Qiongnu said, "If so, it must be fate."

The next day, Tao was summoned to a room and questioned in detail. It turned out to be Tao Lang, who had now changed his name to Zilan and had not yet married. Lan Lady wept bitterly and said, "I am your mother-in-law; your father-in-law is already dead. My mother and I have been wandering here, escaping certain death to be reborn, and I did not expect to see you again today." She then informed Du Jun and Tao's companions, and everyone sighed and lamented, thinking it was a predestined connection. Du Jun then gathered money and prepared gifts to complete the marriage with Tao. On the wedding night, joy could not suppress sorrow. Qiong Nu expressed her inner feelings, unable to bear the pain. She then recited Du Fu's poem "Qiang Village": "At the end of the night, holding the candle, looking at each other as if in a dream." This line seems to have been written for today. Tao comforted her earnestly, saying, "Just don't be too sad, and enjoy the intimacy. Wait until next year, and take you back to Liaodong together. Then the joy of a harmonious relationship will be forever preserved." Soon after, Tao's companion, Ding Zongqi, a loyal and kind person, said to Tao, "You are just married; do not leave immediately. For the military campaign, he does not need to go. We should go to each office to submit documents. You should take good care of your home and stay here. When the official business is done, return to Liaodong together." Tao prepared a feast to bid farewell, and the others set off.

Unexpectedly, Commander Wu discovered the matter and, under the pretext of desertion, captured Tao in prison, beat him to death, and buried his body in a kiln. He urgently instructed a matchmaker to frighten the family of the woman, saying, "He is already dead. You can give up hope. I will choose a day to come and fetch your daughter in a sedan chair. If you do not comply, we will resort to violence." The matchmaker, seeking agreement, returned to report. Qiong Nu made her mother agree. After the matchmaker left, she told her mother, "If I do not die, I will surely be humiliated by violence. I will wait until night to take my own life!" Her mother could do nothing to stop her. That evening, Inspector General Fu of the Imperial Censorate arrived at the post. Qiong Nu looked up to heaven and cried, "My husband's injustice has been cleared!" She then filed a report. Fu submitted a memorial to report the matter. After two months, approval was granted, and an official was ordered to interrogate the case, but the body could not be found. During the investigation, a whirlwind rose before the hall. The official prayed, "If the spirit of the deceased knows, guide me there." As he finished speaking, the wind began to swirl, leading the way with the official's horse, rushing directly to the kiln, blowing away the ash, and revealing the body. The official sent officials to examine the body, and the wounds were clearly visible. Wu confessed his guilt. The official ordered the local magistrate to bury Tao outside the city. Qiong Nu wept and followed, then drowned herself in the pond beside the grave. The official reported to the court, and the Ministry of Rites honored her grave with the inscription "Tomb of the Virtuous and Righteous Woman." The family of the woman also received official support and was provided for throughout their lives.

**The Record of Meeting Immortals at Manting Pavilion** Du Zengcheng, a reclusive scholar from Baqiu, was residing in Jianyang. He had a noble and lofty spirit, and he cherished the mountains and forests. He owned a small boat, in which he kept a writing desk, a tea stove, fishing gear, and a wine jar. He often wandered leisurely along the Nine Bends of the Clear Stream, and people also praised him for his distinctive and admirable character. One day, during the eighth month, after the rain had cleared, a cool breeze filled his sleeves. Du Zengcheng followed the current, drifting along the river, and let himself be guided by the flow. Soon, his boat came to rest by a rock. Looking up at the rock, he saw green vines and emerald tendrils, red osmanthus and dark bamboo, with lush shade and a faint, pleasant fragrance. He tied up his boat and stepped ashore, walking leisurely without a specific destination. Suddenly, a stone gate appeared before him, and the path was flat. Du Zengcheng realized he had entered a strange and unusual place, and he was delighted and excited to move forward. He felt the warm and bright sun, the clear and fresh air, and truly, it was a different and favorable place. After about two li, he entered a large city. The city was grand and imposing, with strict guards and a golden plaque that read, "The True Realm of Manting Pavilion," indicating that it was governed by the deity of Wuyi Mountain. After another li, he saw tall trees, beautiful houses, and high walls, with flowing water, flying flowers, and the sounds of roosters and dogs. He saw a distant building with a high-pitched roof, overlooking a clear pool, and the sign read, "The Qingbi Daoist Temple." Du Zengcheng approached the temple, where monkeys and cranes were tame and docile, and the fragrance of orchids and lilies was浓郁. Under the shade of willow trees, two children stood. Du Zengcheng bowed to them and asked where they were. The children replied, "The Master Qingbi has been waiting for you for a long time."

"Entering to report, he soon reappeared and led 僎成 forward. Passing through several places, they encountered misty windows and foggy halls, far removed from the human world; jade trees and crystal forests, as if in heaven. Finally, they arrived at a pavilion, where a figure with a blue-green headband and a wide sash, of dignified and elegant appearance, sat in the middle. 僎成 bowed twice. The figure said, 'Do you know of Du Bo Yuan of Jingzhao in the human world? I am he. You are my clan's descendant. Remember this well.' 僎成 knelt and thanked, 'I am too late to receive your teachings.' After a while, the figure asked about the descendants of various noble families, including Yu, Yang, Fan, and Jie. 僎成 responded clearly and concisely. The figure seemed pleased. Soon, a young boy brought in a pot of tea made from a hundred flowers, which 僎成 drank, barely feeling hungry. As evening fell, he was given a separate room for the night, with paper bedding and linen curtains, a stone pillow and bamboo bed, the wind and dew chilling, making it impossible to sleep. Only the moonlight through the window and the snowflakes entering the room made it clear that one must be spiritually and physically strong, with firm resolve and determination, to endure such conditions. The next day, the figure summoned 僎成 for a meal, presenting a plate of venison and a bowl of sesame seeds, which were fragrant and delicious, truly extraordinary. After finishing the meal, he was about to leave when the figure said, 'This is a gathering place for immortals, where various elders are playing. Tomorrow, they will gather at my abode, and I will ask for their poetry and writings to send you back. Wait a little while.' 僎成 was overjoyed beyond expectation."

The next morning, indeed there were seven people who arrived, all dressed in broad, loose robes and tall, majestic hats, adorned with jade ornaments. Each had a dignified and extraordinary presence. Qingbi rose to greet them, bowed deeply, and then sat down. Zhencheng stood like a crane, bowing with clasped hands, holding his breath outside. One of the immortals suddenly turned to him and asked, "Why is this child here?" Qingbi replied, "He is my nephew, Zhencheng. I once lived in the world, repeatedly declining official appointments, and devoted myself to writing. Now, all my works have been lost, except for the *Commentary on the Spring and Autumn Annals* in forty-eight volumes, which contains all my life's efforts. This is something all of you are familiar with. I once stored it in a stone box, locked with a golden key, and hidden in a cave on the north side of Fubao Peak. Recently, due to the disturbances caused by dragons and sea monsters, the water surged and opened the cave, exposing the box. I am deeply afraid that ignorant people might steal it, as fate has not yet allowed it to be passed on to human generations. Therefore, I summoned you to seal it up." They then discussed the merits and flaws of the various commentaries. One of the immortals said, "The *Spring and Autumn Annals* is the work of Confucius himself, unlike other classics. Yet, scholars try to interpret it through narrow perspectives, rigidly assigning meanings to individual words, which is not in line with the mind of a sage. Generally, the sacred texts record both constant and changing matters, making it difficult to interpret them with a single viewpoint. For example, the first entries are about kings and envoys, followed by titles and fiefs, which are constant. However, when it comes to hosting meetings, leading armies, plotting rebellion, or conspiring to overthrow, these are matters of change."

Though it begins by establishing laws, it is earnestly devoted to the Zhou dynasty; the king is necessarily called the "Son of Heaven," and righteousness is necessarily called the "king's righteousness." The spiritual power of Kings Wen, Wu, Cheng, and Kang is solemnly present before us. To restore order and correct wrongs is done for the sake of the world and future generations — yet to suppose that it was written solely for Lu, could this be the intention of a sage? One sage said, "What was the view of Master Boyuan?" Qingbi replied, "Ancient scholars said that when the Three Commentaries were composed, the *Spring and Autumn Annals* became scattered. Though they are scattered, the Three Commentaries themselves are not to be lightly criticized. The *Gongyang* and *Guliang* Commentaries focus solely on interpreting the text, while the *Zuo* Commentary focuses solely on narrating events. It was not until the Tang dynasty scholars Dàn and Zhao that they began to dissect every detail, clarify the principles of interpretation, and unify the essential insights of all three into one."

Lu Chun personally inherited the teachings of the Zhao school and wrote three books, *Zuanli*, *Bianyi*, and *Weizhi*. His writings can be described as clear and bright, and his scholarship can be described as pure and refined. The works of the scholars of the Song dynasty are all clear and straightforward, with precise language and closely reasoned meanings, leaving nothing unsaid. However, Hu Kanghou, who focused on admonition, had a slight degree of forced interpretation in his discussion of "King Gaozong's Revenge." Therefore, Zhu Xi once said: "Hu Kanghou's interpretation of the *Spring and Autumn Annals* is about 70-80% correct, but it has not reached a state of complete clarity." There is good reason for this. Zhang Qia's commentary and Wang's *Yan Yi* and other works all reveal new insights from previous scholars, discussing their subtleties, but they are not yet complete. The ultimate authority lies in the teachings of Cheng Yi. Afterward, a banquet was set up, with ritual vessels and dishes arranged. The food included yellow essence and black fungus, and the music featured red strings and green silk. Fragrant wine was passed around, and the attendants and servants performed their duties with respect and diligence, not daring to speak or cough out of respect. After the meal was over, incense was burned again, and tea was served once more.

A green-clad boy held a brocade scroll and unrolled it on a stone table. The host, named Zhencheng, instructed the seated guests to bow in reverence. He then said, "My nephew's arrival brings great fortune! This encounter is truly a result of our predestined connection. Might the immortals have any thoughts or intentions? I humbly request a few verses as a gift, so that I may take them back to the human world as a rare and precious possession. This would also be a great and virtuous act for literature. May I ask if you would agree?" They all smiled and replied, "We have not spoken in the language of the world for a long time, so we do not know what to say." Thus, the clear green color was personally inscribed with the three characters "Man Ting You" at the beginning of the scroll. The Daoist master Fang Fanghu then drew the "Man Ting You" picture next. The White Immortal Master Yuchan of the Purple Sky wrote a grand and eloquent preface titled "Man Ting You Xù." The text of the preface is too extensive to include here. The immortals then composed poems one after another, as swiftly as rain and wind. Master Wu Quanjie of the Xianxian School led the way by saying...

A Man of Abundant Blessings In the past, he served in a high-ranking position in the imperial court, performing rituals on a purple altar at night to the divine ruler of the sky. He had received divine favor and the imperial edict, but the true spiritual realm remained empty, leaving only a place for ascetic practice. Beyond the material world, the mist and clouds were truly enjoyable, while worldly wealth and honor had long been forgotten. His father's writings and legacy remained, and the stone chamber, when opened, should be carefully stored. The Outer History of Zhenju, Zhang Boyu of Juzh, also wrote: A temporary farewell to a trip to Wuyi Mountain, to visit a secluded and mysterious place. While traveling, he carried a thousand-year-old crane, and upon return, he controlled a five-colored dragon. Excessive commentaries on scriptures truly became a burden, and the truth, being subtle and mysterious, could not be sought through faith alone. The scenic area with many beautiful sights offered the possibility of hiding and cultivating oneself there. The Outer History of Shangqing, Xue Xuanqing, followed with these lines: Dressed in a green lotus robe with clouds and mist, he mistakenly entered the abode of the Outer Historian of Xuanzhou. The green bird recently conveyed a message from the Queen Mother, and the azure dragon remotely guided the carriage of the Wood Man. Meeting was only regretted because of the separation between the divine and the mortal, and returning did not worry about the distance by water or land. The differences between Confucianism and Taoism are not a definitive theory, and facing the wind, he sighed deeply for you. The Water Moon Hermit of Lake Mountain, Zai Yuan, whispered: The writings of this respected person surpass those of ancient people, and giving and taking, choosing and rejecting, are all divine. The brush of a sage, capturing the qilin, has long been extinct, and later scholars who engage in plagiarism cannot trace back to its true essence. Only this person stands out, refining common examples, discarding absurd and forced interpretations. Unusual writings are not allowed to be passed down through the ages, and the dark tunnel is re-educated and sealed with stone. This respected person is already among the immortals and Confucian scholars, personally engaging in the ancient style of Han dynasty clerical script. It is known from afar that these writings are placed in a humble thatched hut, and every night, rainbow light passes through the purple void.

The Immortal True Person of the Open Office, Wang Xiyue, sings: The Master of Wuyi resides in the Heavenly Abode, shutting himself in to study and interpret the classics. People of the East Sea vie to honor Guan Ning, and scholars of the South State compete to praise Xu Ru. Revering the king and disdaining the hegemon, how much effort is needed? The words are stern and the meaning is righteous, clearly distinguishing even the finest details. The wicked have already been executed by the axe and halberd, while the virtuous still receive honors and praise. Once cherished like gold and jade, it is firmly locked and sealed, buried at the foot of the mountain. The traces of spiritual power were hidden on this day, but when will the recitation and reading be carried in one's belly? The ghost guard was careless, and the dragon escaped, flowing away in one day. The Master greatly feared and called his clan, filling the rocky depths with earth and stone. Thereby achieving the realm of pure emptiness, it is best to sever worldly ties and gain deep insight. Do not cling to the fires of hell in the human world; the illusory body is like the shadow of a bubble. The Immortal Sage of the Jade Toad, a great scholar of the Song dynasty, is a noble official of high rank in the Yuan dynasty. The calligraphy of Xuanxi is ancient and hard to find, and the writings of Boyu are now nonexistent. Old in the lakes, mountains, water, moon, and mist, among the immortal guests, poetry is even better. The brush strokes are like a tiger lying down and a dragon leaping, flying like a shuttle of a thousand measures of pearls and jade. All the officials are from the Song and Yuan dynasties, riding phoenixes and phoenixes as immortals. After a thousand lifetimes and ten thousand calamities, it is hard to see, but how is it that they are all close to each other in one day? I humbly hold the position of an open office, abandoned the dust in the Zhizheng era. Frequently traveled between Wuyi and Tianmu, alone with your father early on. When he returns, strive and do not waste time, for the flow of time is like a shuttle. Few old graves on Beimang Mountain, heard that there are now more new graves.

The poem was completed, and all wrote with their own hands in one stroke, the writing flawless, requiring no revision. As they were enjoying the poem, suddenly a Daoist practitioner named Li Yucheng, a scholar named Zhao Siqu, a Daoist practitioner named Zha Guangju, and another Daoist practitioner named Zhang Xinfu arrived. Bo Yu exclaimed, "A strange thing! A strange thing!" and then presented the poem to the four of them for their comments. Zha Guangju first composed a poem in response: Riding a crane from Liaodong, a thousand years later, the peach blossoms bloom again. Who is this young boy like Dongfang Shuo? Stealing down from the peach tree. Zhang Xinfu's poem said: Those who attain the Dao all become guests of the fairy islands, having achieved immortality, they have become guests of the fairy realms. How could they still have thoughts of the mundane world, and write *Cloud Songs* to gift to the common people? Li Yucheng's poem said: The sage retracts his gaze and breath, cultivating tranquility and nurturing the subtle and hidden. All things are like straw dogs, and this body is truly as if abandoned. The Great Dao has no beginning or end, and the times and fate have their ups and downs. I entrust this message to the student of Dao, try to look into the secret.

Xuyi also followed and wrote, saying: The distant mountains, their bluish-green hues lingering afar, and the weak water, unable to bear the weight. The flowing sound is heard as if carried by the wind, and the swift-moving carriage is stopped by the flying canopy. Because they encountered a person of the world, they casually asked what era it was. After writing, Qingbi smiled and bid farewell, and the immortals escorted them out. Zhencheng bowed and accepted the scroll, then took leave to return home. Qingbi sent someone to escort him out of the cave, and he disappeared suddenly. Looking back at the four mountains, they were dense with undergrowth, only the brocade scroll shone brightly in the bag. He returned to find a small boat, still moored in its original place. After returning home, Zhencheng went to search under the box in the jade cabinet. Indeed, there was a leaning pine tree by the cave entrance, with a stone box sealed very tightly. It was being washed by the water and rocks, on the verge of falling but not yet fallen, lying horizontally at the base of the pine tree. Zhencheng suspended a rope down the rock face, filled the gap with earth, and then added stones. From then on, his appearance became radiant, and he walked as if flying, presumably due to eating strange delicacies. After several years, he abandoned his wife and children, carrying the traces of the immortals, and wandered through famous mountains, rarely interacting with people. Only the high-ranking scholar Lu Dazhai, who was close friends with the Dragon and Tiger, was shown the scroll and told about it. Lu Dazhai copied three characters on the rock of the immortals and recorded the poetry and writings, similar to those of a heavenly master. The heavenly master sought the scroll but could not obtain it. After Lu Dazhai's death, Zhencheng was filled with sorrow and lack of support, and he also transformed in the mountains. On the eve of his transformation, the wind and thunder seized the scroll. The next day, he passed away, and his body remained unchanged for seven days, with his eyes and limbs not decaying. Those who knew about it believed he had encountered the immortals and achieved a spiritual transformation.

**The Biography of Hu Meiniang** Huang Xing was a postal station attendant in Xinzheng. One day, he went out and returned at night, feeling tired and resting under a tree. He saw a fox pick up a human skull and wear it, bowing to the moon. Suddenly, the fox transformed into a woman, about sixteen or seventeen years old, who was extremely beautiful. She was crying on the road in Xinzheng, crying while walking. Huang followed her from behind, observing her. The fox did not expect to be seen by Huang, so she acted cute. Huang thought to himself, "This is a valuable commodity that can be kept." He then asked, "Who is this woman, and why is she walking alone at night?" She replied, "I am from Hangzhou. My surname is Hu, and my name is Meiniang. My father was transferred to Shaanxi, and we were robbed by bandits in the village. My parents and siblings all died at the hands of the bandits, and all my possessions were lost. Only I hid in the tall grass and survived until now. Now I am alone and have no place to rely on, and I will throw myself into the water to die, so I am crying here." Huang said, "My family is poor, but we still have enough food. My wife is kind and good, and she can accept you. Can you stay with us?" The woman bowed and thanked him with tears, saying, "Seeing your kindness, you are truly like a second father and mother to me." She followed Huang to his home, and he repeated the previous words to Huang's wife. The wife saw that the woman was gentle and compliant, and she treated her well, but Huang never mentioned the reason.

At that time, Scholar-official Xiu Yu, from the Fujian region, was recently appointed as the magistrate of Yaozhou. Passing through Xinzheng, he and the magistrate of Xinzheng, Peng Zhihe, were cousins, and thus he visited Peng Zhihe. Peng Zhihe hosted him at the inn. Huang Xing served as a servant at the inn and saw that Yu was young, carefree, and not a proper gentleman, and he carried a lot of valuable luggage. He then said to his wife, "I can escape poverty now." Therefore, he wanted to influence Yu and often ordered Mei Nian to draw water from the well, making Yu see her. Yu indeed liked her beauty and immediately asked to marry her as a concubine. Huang Xing said, "If you must marry my daughter, you cannot do so without ten times the dowry." Yu was not stingy and spent all his wealth to make it happen, taking her to his post. Mei Nian was naturally intelligent and gentle in nature, treating the older and younger alike, winning everyone's favor. From the wife of the governor down to the wives of other officials, each received a piece of green silk and ten sheets of rouge. Whether abundant or modest, it was always appropriate. In her spare time, she would personally spin and weave silk, staying deeply within the inner quarters, never stepping beyond the threshold. When Yu had doubts, he would consult her, and she would analyze everything in detail, fully understanding the situation. Yu was amazed to have such an inner helper, and among his colleagues, they also believed her to be a virtuous woman.

Not long after, the provincial government heard of Xiao Jun's talents and issued an order commissioning him to urge the delivery of grain to the various prefectures. Mei Niang spoke to Xiao Jun, saying, "Dedicate yourself to public service and do your best for the state. Household affairs, I can handle. Just take care of your precious body, to repay even a tiny fraction of your kindness, and do not burden yourself with family matters." Xiao Jun nodded in agreement and took his leave. Proceeding on his journey, he stayed at the Chongyang Palace. The Taoist priest Yin Danran saw him and privately told Xiao Jun's attendant Zhou Rong, "Your official aura is very strong, and if not treated, it will lead to grave danger." Zhou Rong reported this to Xiao Jun, who angrily retorted, "What kind of Taoist is this, daring to speak nonsense?" By the end of winter that year, Xiao Jun completed his task and returned to the state office. By the end of spring, Xiao Jun fell ill, with a sallow complexion, a thin body, disoriented behavior, and a panicked demeanor. His colleagues sought medical treatment and administered medicine, but all efforts were in vain, and no one knew the cause of his illness. Zhou Rong suddenly remembered Yin Danran's words and reported them to the governor. The governor asked Xiao Jun about it, and Xiao Jun replied, "Indeed!" The governor then said to the vice-governor Liu Su, "Xiao Jun is ill, and everyone says it's due to a spirit. We cannot stand by and do nothing." Liu Su suggested, "Why don't we invite Master Yin to treat him?" The governor immediately prepared letters and gifts and sent Zhou Rong to the Chongyang Palace to invite Yin Danran. Yin Danran said, "He did not believe my words, which led to this situation. But it is the Taoist's duty to help people, so is it worth a trip?" He accompanied Zhou Rong there, and the governor came out to greet them, asking for help in treating Xiao Jun's illness.

Dān Rán dismissed everyone and told the governor, "I have known this matter for a long time. The person in his household is an old fox spirit from the northern gate of Xīnzhèng, who has transformed into a woman and deceived many people. If it is not quickly removed, the disaster will be unpredictable." The governor was startled and said, "Xiao Jun’s wife is widely known for her virtue; how can such a claim be made so suddenly?" Dān Rán replied, "Wait until tomorrow, and you will see." He then set up an altar in the back hall of the state office. The next day at noon, Dān Rán held his sword and wrote a talisman, immediately summoning the divine generals. In a moment, Generals Dèng, Xīn, and Zhāng stood sternly before the altar. Dān Rán burned incense and vowed to the gods, saying, "Governor Xiao Yù is bewitched by a fox spirit. I ask you, gentlemen, to eliminate it immediately." He then wrote a proclamation, handed it to the generals, and sent them away. The text of the proclamation read:

上清杀伐雷府分司，照得：二气始判，而天高地下，自此奠其仪；三才已分，而物化人生，亦各从其类。念幅员之既广，慨狐魅之滋多。缉木叶以为衣，冠髑髅而改貌。击尾出火以作祟，听冰渡水而致疑。所以百丈破因果之禅，大安入罗汉之地。再思多佞，难逃两脚之讥；司空博闻，能识千年之怪。况萧裕乃八闽进士，七品命官，而敢荐尔腥臊，夺其精气。投身驿传之卒，作配缙绅之流。恣乌合而弗惭，怀豕心而未已。绥绥厥状，紫紫其名，过可文乎？言之丑也！郡城隍失于觉察，权且姑容。衙土地乃尔隐藏，另行究治。其青丘之正犯，论黑簿之严刑，押赴市曹，毙于雷斧。使虎威之莫假，庶兔悲而有惩。九尾尽诛，万劫不赦。耀州衙速令清净，新郑驿永绝根苗。长闭鬼门之关，一准酆都之律。布告庙社，咸使风闻。 The Office of Punishment in the Upper Pure Thunder Palace decrees: When the two primary forces (yin and yang) first differentiated, heaven became high and earth low, thus establishing their order; when the Three Talents (heaven, earth, and humanity) were distinguished, all things and human life followed their respective categories. Considering the vastness of the territory, one laments the proliferation of foxes and demons. They weave leaves into clothing and wear skulls to alter their appearance. They strike their tails to produce fire and cause mischief, and listen to ice crossing water to create suspicion. Thus, Master Baizhang broke the cycle of karma in his Zen teachings, and Dashi entered the realm of Arhats. Reishin, who was overly flattering, could not escape ridicule from his two legs; Sikong, with his broad knowledge, could recognize strange phenomena from a thousand years ago. Moreover, Xiao Yu, a scholar-official from Fujian with a seventh-rank appointment, dared to recommend you, a stinking demon, to steal their vital energy. They took on the form of postal servants and mingled with the gentry. They freely gathered without shame, harboring the heart of a pig and not ceasing. Their appearance is despicable, and their names are vile; can such actions be justified? They are indeed shameful! The city god failed to detect this, and so we temporarily tolerate it. The local land deities hid you, and so we will investigate and punish them separately. The chief culprit from Qingxiu will be judged by the strict laws of the Black Register, dragged to the marketplace, and killed by the thunderbolt axe. To ensure that the tiger's power is not abused, we hope to deter even the timid rabbit. All nine-tailed foxes will be exterminated, and they will not be forgiven for a thousand kalpas. The office of Ya Zhou must be quickly cleansed, and the New Zheng Post Station must be forever rid of its roots. They will be permanently closed off at the gate of Ghost City, following the laws of Fengdu. This will be announced in temples and shrines, so that all may hear of it.

Shortly after, dark clouds rolled in like ink, and heavy rain poured down as if from an overturned pot. A thunderclap shook the earth, and Méiniáng was struck dead in the bustling market. The guards and officials went to investigate and found that she was a real fox, with a human skull still attached to her head. The families quickly retrieved the gifts she had given them and examined them. The green silk was revealed to be several layers of banana leaves, and the rouge was just a few peach blossom petals. They showed these to Yù, who finally felt relieved. The magistrate ordered the dead fox to be burned and buried in a remote place, with an iron tablet placed over it to prevent her from returning. Then, he took cinnabar, crab roe, and seal incense for Yù, and with a sweep of his sleeves, he returned to the mountains, leaving without a care. Yù recovered from his illness and then informed the magistrate about his marriage to Méiniáng, sending someone to Xinzheng to inquire about Huáng Xīng. Huáng Xīng had already moved to a new residence, and his household was now prosperous, no longer serving as a postal worker. It was said that his wealth came from the dowry provided by Yù. Yù briefly explained the truth of marrying a fox to the people. The inquirer returned and reported everything to the magistrate. The townspeople then believed that the fox was skilled at deception, and they were amazed by the mysterious and calm technique of Dàndàn.

The Record of Heavenly Lanterns In the second year of the Tianli era, during the year of Jisi, the scholar Wen Xinmei from Yuqian went out for a walk. Halfway through his journey, suddenly two messengers appeared, dressed in simple robes and wearing straw sandals, walking side by side. They bowed deeply before him and said, "The Master of Huayang invites you respectfully after bathing and dressing in clean clothes." Wen Xinmei hastily declined, saying, "I am but a humble scholar from Tianmu; Huayang is a sacred realm of the earth's core. The realms of immortals and humans are separated; how can one reach such a place?" The messengers replied, "We have prepared a carriage for you; please do not refuse!" They then accompanied him, and indeed, a bamboo palanquin was waiting on the side of the road. After Wen Xinmei got in, they carried him away like a bird in flight, and in an instant, they arrived at their destination. The messengers led Wen Xinmei inside. The master wore a jade crown and a silk robe, holding a bamboo tablet as he welcomed them, and said, "I have overstepped my bounds by inviting you; I humbly accept your visit. Please do not blame me for being compelled." He treated Wen Xinmei with respect, and they sat together in the hall. After tea, the master poured wine, and a feast of delicacies was laid out. He personally held a cup in front of Wen Xinmei and said, "I have long lived in this heavenly realm and have been longing for leisure. However, I still care about matters of marriage. My youngest daughter is now of marriageable age, and we have arranged her marriage to Lake Tai. We will take our second son as her husband. The auspicious day is approaching, and the wedding gifts have arrived. Everything is ready, except for the reply letter, which we have yet to find someone to deliver. We have heard of your fame, especially your talent, and we specially invite you to assist us." He then ordered the attendants to fetch a brush, inkstone, and fine paper, placing them on the table. Wen Xinmei wrote as if guided by a divine hand, his thoughts flowing like a spring, writing continuously without stopping, seemingly without much effort. The words he wrote were: [The text of the poem or literary work would follow here, but it is not provided in the source text.]

In the blessed land where yin and yang harmonize, the celestial realm aligns the destinies of the two saints; the dragon pool, with its deep years, establishes a bond of a thousand years in the aquatic realm. Relying solely on the rabbit's brush, I respectfully cover the phoenix's seal. I respectfully address the ruler of the Lake of the Thunder God, the benevolent and auspicious King, and his family: the pure essence of heaven and earth, the cold brilliance of stars and constellations. Truly becoming a true immortal, receiving the essence of the heavens from the upper realm; holding the position equal to that of the rivers and seas, receiving a distinguished title in clear times. Implementing the benevolent and tranquil influence of rain, embodying the way and preserving the use of wisdom and action. Even the smallest streams must be included, broadening its capacity through inclusiveness; all streams converge, gathering their shores into the boundless. Long-standing reputation for loyalty and devotion, early recognition of the nourishing power of water. Administering affairs in the Fish Scale Hall, with a solemn and harmonious order; retiring to the Tortoise Shell Palace for feasts, with graceful dances and beautiful music. Holding a position linked to the heavenly splendor, enjoying a temple feast in the distant Wu region. The people devoutly worshiping incense and fire, the world venerating the divine power. Blessings and fortunes are shared, merchants and farmers equally rely on it. I am devoted to simplicity and humility, embodying the law of modesty. Holding a position at the Golden Gate, managing life and death in the lower realms; holding the balance in the Dark Province, fortunate to attend court close to the clear light. Having established friendly relations with neighboring lands, I still admire the splendor of the prosperous regions. My son, with a distinguished reputation, is indeed a young man in fine robes; my daughter, gentle and obedient, is not a rich girl from a high-rise building. Kind and respectful, I admire the noble young man; solemn and elegant, I feel humble to marry the princess. Considering myself, who am I to refuse such a match? Suitable for his family, suitable for his home, the initial engagement is solemnly observed. Returning a peach with a plum, the generous gift cannot be adequately repaid. Eternal youth, forever fragrant.

The elder had finished reading, and he praised it repeatedly, then stayed overnight, joining the splendidly illuminated gathering. He then sent messengers with letters to invite all the immortals from nearby caves and palaces, creating a grand and magnificent spectacle. On the day of the event, they gathered in a line, with an overwhelming number of carriages and horses, and a splendid display of flags and banners, something unprecedented in the world. The elder wore a ceremonial crown with nine tassels, carried a map of the Five Great Mountains, and donned a red, frost-like robe, receiving guests respectfully in a separate hall. Soon, a thousand horsemen and riders, with rhythmic drumming and horn blowing, arrived. They were followed by green canopies and embroidered banners, and the guests, dressed in embroidered robes and ceremonial garments, were led into the tent with great respect. The lights were bright, and the music was melodious. A servant ran to report, "The new groom has arrived at the door." The groom's entourage rose to greet him and led him into the private area. Suddenly, a message was sent from inside, urgently requesting a poem to hasten the bride's makeup. However, the groom's attendants found the task extremely difficult. Dozens of people came and went continuously. The groom realized that his trusted advisor, Xian Mei, was present and privately sent someone to ask for help. Xian Mei immediately composed the following poem and handed it over: Before the jade mirror stand, the green hairpins droop, The ivory comb slides down to the bed. With golden hairpins and phoenix ornaments, she is fully adorned, Come out early to see through the red brocade and embroidered curtains. With eighteen hairpins, she is full of youthful vigor, Unaware of the long night, the wind and charm are all in Zhang Sheng's pen, Leave the double eyebrows to be painted after the makeup is done. The matchmaker led her in, and everyone cheered. They saw a hundred rows of red-dressed women, two lines of painted candles, loud music, and a gentle fragrance, leading the groom into the bridal chamber for the wedding ceremony. The attendant forgot to bring the scattering text, and everyone was startled. The groom whispered to the matchmaker, asking her to send someone to ask Xian Mei for help. Xian Mei immediately composed the following text and handed it over:

Considering the primordial chaos, when the formless void had yet to be divided, and the clear and turbid had not yet separated, there was no distinction between hard and soft. Since the beginning of creation, the concept of pairing and matching has arisen; where the ultimate Dao resides, the great marriage is especially solemn. May the newlywed groom from Zhenze and the virtuous maiden of Huayang, born under auspicious celestial influences and endowed with true beauty from birth, be praised. Her grace in ritual, music, and literature is truly worthy of comparison to Wu Cailuan’s elegance; her virtue, appearance, speech, and morality make her eminently suitable as the wife of Wang Junjiang. Like a red peach blooming from the sacred spring, and a red leaf inscribed with poetry upon the flowing stream, their union is a match made by heaven, with the divine aiding in its fulfillment. The workings of nature are inseparable from the dual forces of yin and yang, and the subtle mysteries of the Dao originate in the union of husband and wife. In the secluded chamber, embroidered curtains and fragrant green silk bedding fill the air; her radiant attire includes a robe washed in fire and embroidered with a square collar. She lifts the veil, adorned with dew-like ornaments, and shares a cup of wine from jade goblets. A richly embroidered cushion lies beneath her soft silk socks, designed for the delicate beauty of her golden lotus feet; with deep black powder, he gently draws the shape of her crescent moon-like eyebrows. Two families united for a hundred years, one pair in perfect harmony. Their union is already harmonious in marriage, and their intimacy is as fitting as the harmonious sounds of the zither and the lute. In the ritual of offering fán and píng, they proceed with careful reverence in the ancestral rites; as they play with the jade and the tile, they are destined to receive the blessings of the mat and bedding. Can their union be compared to any famous flower? Are they not as rare and united as twin blossoms on a single stem? Graceful as the morning phoenix, harmonious like the swan of spring. The sound stirs the curtains, the silk light as dragon scales; in the dream, upon the pillow of the mandarin duck, her breath carries the fragrance of the chicken tongue herb. The rare encounter has been fulfilled in the wedding ceremony, and with the custom of the bed cover being cast, blessings are now proclaimed. Let us sing and recite words, to add to the joyous occasion.

Eastward, the wedding bed is set, surrounded by luxurious curtains and drapes as the spring breeze flows (Tang Li He). The red cherry blossoms hold the purity of snow (Tang Li Shangyin), and the essence of the bride shines brightly at the center (Tang Li He). Westward, music and dance keep people entertained as the moon sets quickly (Tang Chu Guangxi). The lotus flower awakens with fresh and vibrant energy (Tang Li He), and her graceful posture in the wind enchants the spring (Tang Yong Tao). Southward, the bride sits in the wedding sedan chair, adorned in a spring-themed dress (Tang Li Shangyin). Her hairstyle is slightly disheveled, as if she has just woken up (Tang Bai Juyi), and the heart-wrenching beauty of spring is present in the south (Tang Wei Zhuang).

In the north of the bed, clouds adorn the half-open walls with a pale white hue. A soft voice whispers to the young man, and the beauty of spring makes it hard to sleep. On the bed, two women in red dresses smile at each other. Light clouds and rain gently touch the high tower, and upon waking, the new moon has risen without notice. Below the bed, the night is filled with a full moon and east wind. A cold bamboo mat and silver bed prevent dreams from forming, and beautiful wine and clear music fill the song room. I wish that after the bedspread ceremony, the couple will be happy, and the household will be harmonious. A sip of nectar, just like the strange encounter of Pei Hang; five pairs of white jade, which can indicate the hidden virtue of Yong Bo. Even if the stone crumbles and the sea dries up, the sky will be long and the earth will last forever. Crickets chirp in order, and the feet of the unicorn are strong! How unfortunate that the groom's attendants use Fuzhou dialect, are unskilled in reading, and call out "Wen Xiucai." Upon entering the inner chamber, jewels and silk shine, with many women having rosy cheeks and soft necks. Except for the bride and groom sitting on the elephant bed, it is impossible to tell who the bride is. A beautiful and graceful recitation, with proper intonation and rhythm, is well-received by the audience. The ceremony is completed and the person leaves. Soon, the groom sends a matchmaker to deliver two pieces of ice silk and two pearls, which the person receives and then attends the banquet. The food served is not ordinary, and its names cannot be recognized.

The elder informed all the guests and praised the talent and temperament of Xin Mei. He then said, "This auspicious ceremony is rarely encountered over a long period. Today, scholars and immortals have graciously come to grace the occasion. I wish to leave behind precious writings to honor this heavenly abode. May I ask if this is acceptable?" Xin Mei then presented the poem "The Divine Lanterns of the Heavenly Abode": In the beginning, the primordial elements separated, creating the spiritual land. Steep cliffs and high walls cut off the paths of traffic. Deep and strict, it does not allow ordinary people to enter. Secluded and beautiful, it is only suitable for the grand palace to be open. Layer upon layer, grand structures stand, with painted beams reaching the sky and hanging golden plaques. The elder, with his ornate canopy and central axis, assists in governance, holding the power of life and death in Penglai. Gods and spirits naturally differ from ordinary people, and marriage originally lacks desire and passion. Yin and yang, movement and stillness, contain the breath of life, showing a couple that is not vague or uncertain. Who is suitable as a match for such a noble individual? Residing in a place respected by all rivers, Zhenze is highly esteemed. On this good day, many vehicles arrive, and precious trees and branches instantly become two. Dragons and phoenixes are cooked, and a banquet for guests is set up, with drumming and bell striking creating a thunderous sound. My humble self, ordinary and lowly, humbly serves as a scribe, equally dividing profit and wealth. Gladly, I am among the scholars and officials, not expecting to be close to the attendants! Servants in the courtyard continuously bring precious delicacies, and guests have various mixed discussions and lectures. A mole drinking from the river feels ashamed of a full belly, and a small bird from Lu feels ashamed of being well-fed! Fortunately, we see the flower candles and present a new poem, to be appreciated in the heavenly abode for thousands of years.

The guests passed the object around, all praising its rarity and wonder. After the feast ended and the wine was finished, they were helped out. The next day, the father-in-law, in the inner hall of Xuanqing, specially treated the new son-in-law, and appointed Xinmei to accompany him at the table. Xinmei firmly declined, but the father-in-law and son-in-law both invited him, so he sat down. After three rounds of wine, a beautiful woman presented two pieces of red silk and two bolts of brocade as a gift. Once the feast concluded, she sent the two attendants to escort him out. When he returned home, his family was surprised and worried, as he had been missing for half a month. Xinmei collected all the items and sold them, thus becoming a wealthy household. His descendants were numerous. They were called the Wen family who met a fairy. The people of Yuqian still praise them to this day. The Biography of the Imperial Censor of Mount Tai Song Gui, courtesy name Mengzan, was from Yidu, Shandong. His family was of farming background, but his father began to study and became a scholar. Song Gui was handsome and tall from birth, and became dignified and solemn as he grew up. He was diligent in his studies, memorizing thousands of words each day. Living in poverty, he supported himself. He lived in the countryside, teaching as his profession. He did not do anything that was not righteous, and people respected and feared him. The provincial officials recommended him for filial piety, diligence in farming, and did not receive a response. The Grand Scholar Aruqunsali spoke of his adherence to principles, quiet retreat, and lack of desire for official advancement, suggesting he should be used to encourage those who rush for office, but again, there was no response. Song Gui remained indifferent to all of this. He was strict and resolute, unable to tolerate others' mistakes. He would often criticize them directly, causing their faces to redden and their hair to stand on end, showing no leniency. But people also respected his guidance and teachings, and no one held a grudge against him.

On the 15th day of the eighth month in the autumn of the 20th year of the Zhi Zheng era, while Cui was at home, he suddenly saw dark clouds gather, enveloping his house. Banners, flags, and symbols of authority surrounded a divine figure, appearing like a high-ranking official of the mortal world. The figure called out to Cui, saying, "The Lord of Mount Tai has heard that you are well-versed in the classics and have a refined character, unmatched in the world. He specially summons you to serve as the Inspector of Justice in the region of Mount Tai." Cui was unable to understand the reason and bowed in submission, accepting the command. The divine figure then proclaimed: From the Eastern Emperor of Mount Tai, Lord of the Great Temple of the East: It is said that offering silk to attract the wise is difficult, and I often struggle to find true talent; maintaining the proper order and enforcing laws, you are fit for the role. Considering this position of oversight, you truly bear the trust of intelligence. Seeking talents from the common people, you ascend to high positions. Scholar Cui, you are impartial and without bias, strict and decisive. You diligently explore the depths of the classics and contain the essence of the *Yijing* (Book of Changes). Content with poverty and happy with simple food, you find joy in the simple and humble. Honor and glory often come after death, and you are appointed to a high position in the imperial court. Always serving the emperor in the role of inspector, you await the hearing of just and righteous words. Expecting to surpass the achievements of Fan Pang, you are willing to stand among the likes of Huandian. With a stern face, you make flatterers feel cold, and with swift and forceful words, you break the hearts of the wicked. Do not disappoint the choice of a noble selection, think of repaying the special knowledge. Alas! The axes and halberds descend from the heavens, the salary has not yet reached the mortal world; standing in embroidered robes in the heavens, your name is even more revered than Mount Tai. Admonish you, the learned scholar, accept my new command, and you may take the position of Inspector of Justice.

After hearing, Gui bowed twice and said, "The imperial decree is stern; how dare I evade it? I only ask for a little delay." The divine being nodded and turned back to leave. Gui knew he would die, so he immediately arranged his affairs, bathed, changed clothes, and by midnight, he passed away. After several years, his friend Qin Zhen, who had been dismissed from his post as the governor of Minzhong, returned to Taian Prefecture and met Gui at an inn. They reminisced about old times and bought wine to drink together. Zhen realized that Gui was a ghost and knew all the details of his death, so he asked, "Is the government of the underworld similar to the human world?" Gui replied, "I and you are on different paths, so how could I know? However, thinking of our old friendship and that you are a scholar, what harm is there in telling you? Generally, the underworld is strict and does not use people lightly. Only in the Tai Shan (Mount Tai) government, which oversees seventy-two departments, thirty-six prisons, and various offices and temples, do they use loyal ministers, martyrs, filial sons, and obedient grandchildren. Next are good people and virtuous officials, and even the smallest positions, such as village deities and land gods, are filled by those who are honest and have hidden virtues. Especially, they value literary positions. Recently, the Literary Office was short of officials, and they searched everywhere but could not find anyone suitable."

There are also those who recommend several individuals, although they possess great literary talent. Yet, while they were alive, they did not cultivate virtuous conduct. Some deceived the world by stealing a name, others obscured themselves and deceived others. They fawned like dogs and flattered like foxes, all of them having flaws that can be criticized. Unavoidably, one must choose the best among them, and thus, one was selected as the chief minister of speech. Recently, the tomb spirits and grave lords have accused him of falsifying the epitaphs of the deceased he wrote before death, widely receiving payment for his work, and often exaggerating praise. He confused the real with the fake, considered the foolish as wise, causing good and evil to become mixed. The underworld officials deeply detest this. They often punish according to the laws against false and vain words, sending him to the Hell of the Torn Tongue for punishment. This is a deep warning for scholars; even if there are other merits, they cannot redeem him. The Holy Emperor, considering him a close minister, showed leniency. But he again became lost in drink, made mistakes in official documents, and his crimes have reached the limit. The spirits and deities are all angry. I will investigate and impeach him, and the Heavenly Emperor is furious, so he was sent to prison. He immediately reported to the highest heaven, and the laws have been corrected. You can record my impeachment document, and show it to the villagers, so they know the laws of the underworld and how strict they are. In all writing, one must be sincere and honest, and one cannot say that actions in life are unknown in the underworld. The *Book of Deliverance* says: "The heavenly beings record people's merits and faults without error." This is certainly not an empty statement. He then presented the draft and asked Zhen to copy it.

In writing, it is recorded as follows: Duke Song Gui, the Imperial Censor of Mount Tai, on the matter of investigation and correction: I have heard that setting up positions and appointing officials is based on the universal principles of yin and yang; holding a brush and writing is indeed the duty of officials and subjects. If one neglects duties for deceitful purposes, they must be corrected and punished. No crime is greater than disrespecting superiors, and no law is more severe than deceiving the sovereign. Evil cannot be tolerated, and punishment must follow immediately. I have observed that the High Minister of the Literary Bureau, a certain person, is an ordinary and foolish scholar, who falsely associated himself with the literati and wrote insincere eulogies. After his death, he falsely gained a high reputation and was adept at gaining fame. He falsely boasted of mediocre talents and unworthily attempted tasks. Promoted from the lowest ranks and elevated from the inner circle, he was accused by the head of the clan and should have been cast out. Yet, he was pardoned and protected. He should have shown loyalty and gratitude, but the official has a tiger's skin with a sheep's nature and a wolf's heart. He did not fulfill his duties in writing but only indulged in drinking and eating, living carelessly. He acts proudly and recklessly, as usual.

Secretive and corrupt, bribery is openly practiced. Even pulling out all one’s hair wouldn’t be enough to count their crimes, and even if their bodies were ground into powder, it wouldn’t be enough to bear the punishment. They act as if no one else exists, knowing only themselves. Stubbornly refusing to repent, they accumulate evil deeds without reform. On the day of the Holy Emperor’s birth, they entered in a mysterious, supernatural manner to offer congratulations. The spirits of the three realms gathered, and the messengers of the mountains arrived together. Bells and drums hung, and the crown with a train ascended the throne. They presented memorial texts to offer blessings and praises, following the customary rituals of offering gifts. Yet, they indulged in prolonged heavy drinking, leading to mistakes at the appointed time. This caused the officials to panic and be horrified, losing their composure. They hastily gathered people to fabricate and assemble a text. Lacking respect and seriousness, the instruments of punishment are at hand; to encourage good and punish evil, to warn and instruct, it is the king’s law that demands execution. Furthermore, regarding a certain Sub-Councilor of State, they are regarded as close as one’s inner organs, treated as if they were one’s father and elder brother. They were promoted through their door, and their actions and reactions are confined by their methods. They often forget to offer advice, repeatedly offering flattery. Their conduct is not free from being tainted by corruption, and it is appropriate to punish them collectively for the sake of warning. It is appropriate to arrest and send the offenders to Fengdu (the underworld), to clearly punish their crimes, to eradicate evil, and to uphold the laws. Due to their status as officials, they await judgment.

After copying, Zhen told him, "I humbly occupy a position among scholars, ingratiate myself with salary. Now that I have resigned and returned to my hometown, I do not know what my future will surely be like. Fortunately, I have met you, and I wish to ask for guidance." Gui said, "Heaven has long been weary of the barbarian's virtue! There will be a true person who will rise to power in the Huai and Si regions, but you will not live to see it. Your descendants will enjoy the blessings of peace." Zhen said, "If so, will there be great chaos in the world soon? There will surely be war, and will I die in battle?" Gui said, "It is still far off, do not worry." Zhen insisted on asking, and Gui picked up a pen and wrote eight lines: "Encounter a crossroad for advancement, meet peace for a meager salary, fire horse moves slowly, golden rooster calls early, dig a well at the heart of the door, remove grass from the flower head, left is yin, right is yang, later release, earlier old." Ultimately, no one understood what it meant, so he put it away in his bag. He then said to Zhen, "Take care of your old friend! Strive to be good!" He bowed farewell and left, suddenly disappearing.

Afterward, Zhen was recommended again and took up the position of recorder in Quzhou, thus verifying the saying "Encountering Quzhou brings advancement." Not long after, he was temporarily appointed to oversee Xi'an County, where he contracted a wind-biased illness that persisted for several months without healing, leading to a suspension of his salary for medical treatment, thus fulfilling the prophecy of "Encountering An's fortune withered." Zhen was deeply concerned about his illness, and soon he passed away. Those interested in such matters later investigated the year of his death, which was indeed the winter of Bingwu (丙午), with Bing associated with fire and Wu with the horse. On the day of his death, Xinyou (辛酉) dawn, Xin is associated with metal and You with the rooster, corresponding to the end of the year and the beginning of the morning, all aligning with the prophecies. However, the meaning of the last four lines remains unclear. Who knew that while Zhen served as a recorder, he married a woman from Kaihua. Due to the chaos and upheaval, he could not return north and thus returned Zhen's coffin to be buried in Kaihua. Examining the characters, placing a well in the gate forms "open" (开), and removing grass from the flower forms "transform" (化). The burial site had his mother's grave to the left, representing yin, and his brother-in-law's tomb to the right, representing yang. On the mountain, there was a ruined Taoist temple, which might refer to the old man mentioned earlier? Nearby, there was a ruined Buddhist hall, which could be a prophecy of the future? After Zhen's burial, his wife and children stayed at the gravesite and eventually became residents of Kaihua. The Heavenly Dynasty pacified the heroes, and the people enjoyed peace and prosperity. Zhen had a grandson who rose to the position of Minister of Works. The words of Gui, though seemingly bizarre, proved to be accurate in every detail. This shows that one's fortunes, misfortunes, life and death, and burial are all predetermined and cannot be altered. Those who try to overcome fate with intelligence and strength often show a lack of understanding of their limitations.

#### Jiang Temple Muddy God Record In the prefecture of Mei (眉州) of Shu (蜀), about a li from the city, there is a small market by the river, with hundreds of households and a bustling trade. On the riverbank stands an ancient temple complex, which is said to be the shrine of Empress Huairui, Lady Fei. To this day, it is quite famous for its miraculous occurrences. Nearby is a wealthy family named Zhong Shengyuan (钟声远), who is both affluent and fond of propriety, and likes to invite renowned teachers. Zhong Shengyuan's sister's son, Xie Sheng Lian (谢生琏), is also from a prominent family and comes to his aunt's house to study. Xie Sheng Lian has an elegant and dignified appearance, a graceful and noble demeanor, and lacks the awkward and pedantic demeanor of a poor scholar. Everyone likes him. They play chess, drink wine, talk, and recite poetry together, fearing that he might leave. Zhong Shengyuan's western academy has a particularly splendid garden, with the Green Ripple Hall, Water Moon Pavilion, Play Fragrance Pavilion, Spring Drunken Pavilion, and Jade Screen Pavilion built within it. Xie Sheng Lian loves the garden's tranquility and stays there to rest, having been there for nearly a month. One day, he returned from outside and suddenly saw four young ladies, about 15 years old, graceful and slender, playing around the side of the Play Fragrance Pavilion. Xie Sheng Lian thought they were his cousins and hurriedly bowed to them. But they were not his cousins. The girls were not at all embarrassed or avoiding, laughing and talking as usual.

The man asked them, “Have you ladies made a mistake by coming here?” One of them replied, “We are the daughters of the Huā family from the eastern neighbor. We have long heard that your garden is beautiful and fragrant with rare flowers, so we came together to enjoy it. We did not expect to be seen by a young man; please do not be too surprised.” He thought they were neighbors visiting each other and was not surprised. As night approached and he was about to sleep, he suddenly heard the window frame creaking, as if someone were knocking or pushing. He got up to look, and it was one of the women he had seen earlier that day, who suddenly entered the room. She bowed to him, with a gentle expression and spoke softly, saying, “We are of humble appearance and delicate beauty, and it was only by chance that we were seen by your noble presence. Suddenly, our tender feelings stirred, and we could no longer restrain ourselves—it was unbearable, so we broke the rules and came to you, thus violating propriety in our pursuit of love. I respectfully bring my bedding and offer it to you.” After saying this, she immediately invited him to sleep with her, and they shared a romantic union. The man playfully asked, “Where are the other three? How come only you have come?” The woman said, “I will wait until tomorrow night and share this joy with my sisters.” Then she composed a poem on the spot, saying:

With her hairpins and phoenix ornaments locked in dust, she languidly avoids painting her eyebrows before the mirror. Who binds the white reed to seek a virtuous man? She writes her own message on a red leaf to entrust a good matchmaker. The lamp of orchid oil has not yet been extinguished, but her heart is already stirred; her steps, light as lotus petals, have already been decided. She takes his hand and asks him where they should go; deep within the crimson curtain, the jade mountain collapses in passion. Soon the moon begins to set, and the rooster's call grows louder. The woman gathers her clothes and says, "I am returning!" She then quietly leaves. The next evening, he lights incense and opens the window to wait. She arrives with another person, smiling and gently patting him, saying, "Last night's joy, I wish to share with my younger sister." She then looks at her sister and says, "Take good care of my lord, and be a good new wife." She slowly steps out. Her sister shows affection, laughing and talking intimately, sharing the same pillow and quilt, just like her older sister. Her sister is clever and also writes poetry, and she immediately composes a poem to give to him: The thin red thread of fate brings a discordant good omen, sisters looking at each other with the same feelings. Accidentally accompanying Chang'e to leave the moon palace, suddenly meeting Cen Ru at the cloud steps. In spring, the jade grass hangs over the embroidered canopy, fragrance wafts from the golden lotus as she removes her phoenix shoes. From now on, their joy in each other is like water and fish; they both wish to preserve their love for a hundred years.

After reciting, the woman slowly departed. The man urged her to come again. She said, "Do not speak too much; I will not let you sleep alone." That night, the eldest sister again brought the third aunt to the man. The man wanted to keep them all, but they declined, saying, "After you have been a new husband four times, my sisters and I will take turns serving in the bedroom, going round and round." The man then became intimate with the third aunt and asked for her poem. She replied, "I am ashamed to have no talent to compose a poem in seven steps, nor am I a match for my second sister. How could I have such ability?" The man persisted in asking, and she then recited: The fragrant room is quiet, the night is long; alone facing the dim lamp, hating the loneliness! The tide has a fixed time, one should be aware of it; the beautiful face has no charm for whom to fade? Frowning like willow leaves, gathering new black makeup; laughing while looking at peach blossoms on soft gauze.
