The karmic connection from past lives has come together in this life; heaven has taught us to be long companions. Soon, the rain and clouds dispersed, the river slanted, and the stars fell. Her makeup was still slightly disheveled, her hair messy, and her hairpins askew. She stood up, gathered her sleeves, and said to the man, "Tonight, the fourth aunt will be your partner, and my sisters cannot all come out. The eldest sister will bring her here." The next night, at the second drumbeat, the fourth aunt indeed came dressed up with her sister to the man. They performed the rites of husband and wife, made a vow as vast as mountains and seas, secretly expressed deep feelings, and also composed a regulated verse: Every spring, laziness doubles; lazily picking up the embroidery needle by the green window. Who would have thought such a rare encounter would lead to a harmonious union? Good fortune was not expected to match the auspicious signs! Hair disheveled, green buns like clouds; hands covered in red sleeves, slender and delicate. Four pearls, all priceless; who else could match the young man in all these qualities?

From this point onward, the women took turns, and every night two of them attended to him. The young scholar privately thought to himself that encountering such a rare and wonderful event, even once was already a rarity, how much more so four times! He then composed a poem titled *E'mei Ancient Sentiment* to celebrate himself. The poem reads: E'mei, the ancient prefecture, is renowned as the mightiest under heaven, with misty mountains and snowy peaks numbering in the thousands. The winding paths through the birds' trails connect to the lands beyond the Sword Gate, while the distant smoke signals of war reach into the barbarian regions. The dangerous waters of the Ba and Shu rivers are perilous to the human world, while the paths and lakes of Bo and Dian extend beyond the civilized realm. The nine tribes of Qiang and other ethnic groups come to settle, and the territories of various tribes are incorporated into the empire's borders. This land, with its strategic advantages, is known as our own, with countless mansions adorned with red doors and spears. The famous "sweating blood" horses are trained in broad daylight, while fat cattle are cooked at night. Markets open on bustling crossroads, and luxurious villas are built amidst tall trees. They meet with bows drawn on horseback, and fruits are thrown into carriages with mutual glances. In youth, one indulges in luxury and grows weary of the mundane, finding joy only in poetry and wine. A sash around the waist and a copper mountain bestowed by the family do not instill fear of poverty. Such wealth is easy to obtain, but it is hard to find a beauty like a fairy. Who would have thought that under the moonlight, one could peek into a treasure, and who would have anticipated meeting a beauty who could topple a nation? A beauty that can topple a nation and a city, with blue water chrysanthemum bracelets and red coral rings. Her eyes are full and bright like autumn water, and her eyebrows are lightly swept like spring mountains. Her eight-character eyebrows vie for beauty, and her aunties and sisters are all exceptionally beautiful. One envies the courtesans of the green towers, and one hears only of the legendary red-fan courtesan. A vow made in the intimacy of an amber pillow still remains, and the light of a candle in front of a tortoise shell curtain fades into darkness.

Lingering tenderness follows the evening rain, affectionate dreams chase the morning clouds. Would she willingly remove her jade pendant, leaving behind its fragrance, to seek a partner? Would she apply her lead and powder, enduring the pain of parting from her companions? A mirror with lily patterns shines by the window, while the subtle fragrance of cypress incense lingers in her drooping sleeves. The delicate perfume fills the fragrant chamber, its scent constant through the night until dawn. True and graceful, like swallows in formation, they line up like fish; small and melodious, like swallows in a row, they arrange themselves like geese. Fish in line, geese in formation, accompany their mates; phoenix pipes and dragon flutes produce the voice of dragons. She withdraws with a smile, her fingers tracing a chicken-head ornament, and wins the phoenix hairpin, calling forth her beauty. The dew is heavy, the stars sparse, the silver drip of the water clock sinks. Twin lotuses, entwined, wrap the embroidered quilt. The lotus is tender, the stem delicate, their beauty alike in form; the orchid's fragrance and the蕙's sweetness, their beauty united in heart. Cultivated and graceful, their charm is manifold; from day to night, could they ever envy one another? A secret promise brings fear of the mother's suspicion, and a hidden sorrow—would it ever be shared with others? Who would know of these hidden feelings and secret vows? As long as heaven and earth endure, so shall our love last a thousand years. I wish to be a butterfly, forever following one another; I wish to be mandarin ducks, never to part. Cui Wenjun of the Zhuo family was unlike those of her neighborhood; Nanwei and Xi Shi were not of the same kind. From one door came four beauties, each graceful and exquisite, combining both singular and dual perfection. The wife of a wandering son walks alone, lonely and forsaken. Her heart breaks at the sound of the cuckoo bird, crying from branch to branch as spring comes and goes.

After the work was completed, he wrote it out and showed it to the women. The women eagerly looked at and passed it around, praising it with one voice, thinking it was a rare and harmonious work. Only the eldest sister remained silent, and after a while, she sighed and said, "The four of us are sisters-in-law, all unmarried women, and we have not yet discussed marriage. Yesterday, I happened to peek into the garden and was drenched in dew. I am honored that you did not abandon me and have shown me deep affection. But I fear that time is fleeting and the good time will be lost easily. You will eventually have to marry, and I will not be able to follow you. I can weave a brocade for my husband, but it is just empty skill like that of Jielan. I cannot leave my body to go to my husband, lacking the ability of Qian Nv. It only leads to the separation of the phoenix and the peacock, and the avoidance of the swallow and the wild goose. Endless sorrow, constant distant thoughts. Reflecting calmly on today's deep joy, I fear it will become a great disaster in the future." The other sisters heard this and all sighed and withdrew.

After another year, the protagonist's parents indeed sent someone to take him back to complete the marriage. Upon hearing this, the four women all came to bid farewell to the protagonist, staying overnight in the study. The protagonist treated each one with warmth and kindness, treating them equally. Just before dawn, the fourth aunt told the protagonist, "The eldest sister's words from the past have proven true. According to fate, there is still one year of缘分 (fate or destiny) left. May you enjoy a harmonious marriage, with the happiness of married life being the greatest joy in life. Consider our humble status and do not abandon us. After we become married, please come again if it is convenient, my sisters and I will still be eagerly waiting for you under the Green Screen Pavilion." She immediately took out a pair of gold hairpins as a gift. The third sister also presented jade hair ornaments, silver bracelets, and earrings, saying, "Return these to your wife, as a token of our friendship." They all shed tears and parted. The protagonist packed everything into a book case. By the time he returned home, the wedding was imminent. The wedding was over, and the household was very suitable, but the thoughts of the four women were never forgotten. After the month of the wedding, the wife returned to her parents' home, leaving the protagonist alone in bed. Suddenly, he dreamed of meeting the four women again, and they interacted as usual. The third aunt said, "It has been a long time since we last saw you, and there is no joy to be had. Please perform a dance of the returning breeze." She then shook her green dress and flipped her silk sleeves, even Zhao Feiyan's grace and Gongsun Zhi's agility were not enough to match her wonder. After the dance, the eldest sister began to sing a song of the returning breeze, saying:

There is a virtuous woman, a beauty of the state, wearing the moon as a pendant and binding herself with orchids and sweet flag. She lifts her light body like a dancer on the palm of the hand, spinning her long sleeves before the banquet table. Like a startled crane, she gracefully moves around, and suddenly like a crane, she dances gracefully. Her cloud-like hair cascades, and her jade earrings dangle. The embroidered seat is adorned with pearl hairpins. She twists and turns, beautiful and charming, her beauty is unparalleled, and her grace is beyond description. Suddenly she lowers and raises herself, and the performance ends. The good night seems endless. The second aunt and the fourth aunt also said to each other, "Singing and dancing, enough to console separation. What should we do?" They took a jade flute and gave it to her, saying, "You are deeply skilled in this, wish not to be stingy. Your sister will sing along, isn't that good?" The sister leaped up, saying, "Indeed!" After a short while, she played the flute three times. Its sound was clear and harmonious, gentle and charming, melancholic and quiet, like the cold cicada in the evening dew, like the fresh breeze riding the autumn clouds. The sister also gathered her eyebrows, sang and harmonized. She sang: Fingers like jade, face like ice, expressing deep thoughts and revealing inner feelings. The lingering sound lingers on, stopping the colorful clouds in the bright moon.

The second song goes: Dewdrops fall, and the sound of the flute is clear; the soft and harmonious call of the phoenix, gentle and peaceful, echoes through the air. Joy has not yet reached its peak, yet emotions linger. The third song goes: The purple flute sounds mournfully, and the night remains silent; the fragrant incense stick burns slowly, and the candlelight casts shadows. The river seems to be about to disappear, and the night draws to a close. Let us enjoy ourselves casually for a moment. Removing the ornamental hairpins and gathering the clear vessel, spreading out the bedding and returning to the inner chamber. Lying close together like a pair of mandarin ducks, the shortness of time indicated by the water clock contrasts with the length of enjoyment. Only feeling sad as the sun rises over the mythical tree of the sun. Just as I was listening, suddenly the horn sounded from the watchtower, and the bells rang from the Buddhist temple. Pushing aside the pillow and stretching, I realized it was all a dream in the South柯. I remembered the words of the songs and wrote them down. I entrusted the completion of the task to my uncle and went to his house. The girls were fortunate to have me return, and their affection for me was even greater than before. I told them about the dream, and one of them said, "This is because the couple misses each other deeply, so it appears in dreams. There is nothing to be surprised about." I became attached to them and stayed only in the study room for more than half a month, not seeing my uncle. My uncle suspected me. One night, he secretly left and observed what I was doing. He saw me playing with the girls under the moon, talking and laughing, enjoying ourselves. He suddenly entered and called out to me, and we were startled and scattered. He then questioned me, but I finally refused to explain in detail. My uncle told his wife, "The garden is wide, and there are many trees and bamboo. Could there not be spirits of flowers and the moon, or water and stone monsters? Lian is also handsome, with good looks, and could he not be bewitched by them? He must be sent back immediately, or he will fall ill if he stays too long." So he ordered a servant to send me home. After arriving home, within half a year, due to thinking of the girls, I indeed fell seriously ill.

He was in a dazed state, his speech incoherent, and he lay in bed for a long time without recovery. Soundfar went personally to see him and reported all previous events to his adoptive parents. After repeated questioning, the sick person finally revealed the truth and showed the objects he had taken, including poems and a hairpin made of clay. Upon seeing them, his adoptive father realized he was being haunted. They then visited the garden together with the maternal uncle, but found no trace. They went to the Flower Petal Temple to draw a fortune-telling sign and passed by a small room in the east corridor, which was dimly lit and rarely visited. Upon entering, they saw an inscription reading "Position of the Goddess of Mount Wu," with four beautiful women sculpted inside. The eastern woman was missing a hairpin, the two on the right were missing bracelets on their arms, the two on the left were missing earrings, and one on the left had two flower decorations missing from her face. His adoptive father was greatly alarmed, took the clay objects, and placed them back in their original positions, where they matched perfectly. He then broke the image by hand and ordered the servant to throw it into the river. After this, the sick person recovered within a month, and the strange and ghostly phenomena ceased.

The Screen of the Lotus Flower In the year Xinhao of the Zhizheng era, there was a man named Cui Ying from Zhenzhou, who was extremely wealthy. Due to his father's influence, he was appointed as the military commander of Yongjia in Wenzhou, Zhejiang, and took his wife Wang with him to assume office. On their way, they passed by Tianshan Mountain in Suzhou, where they moored their boat for a brief rest, purchasing paper money, sacrificial animals, and wine to offer at a temple. After completing the offering, they had a small drink together in the boat. The boatman saw that their drinking vessels were all made of gold and silver, and suddenly conceived evil thoughts. That night, he drowned Cui Ying in the water and killed his maidservants and servants. He then told Wang, "Do you know why you are not being killed? My second son has not yet taken a wife. Now, he is rowing a boat to Hangzhou with others, and he will return in one or two months to marry you. You will then be part of my family, so just be at ease and have no fear." After saying this, he took all their possessions and referred to Wang as his new wife. Wang pretended to agree, and she tried her best to manage the affairs, showing utmost kindness and consideration. The boatman secretly rejoiced at having obtained a wife, and gradually became familiar with her, no longer being wary or cautious. After about a month, it happened to be the Mid-Autumn Festival. The boatman prepared a lavish banquet of food and drink, drinking heavily and getting drunk. Wang waited until he was asleep, then cautiously got out of the boat and walked for two or three miles, suddenly losing her way. All around were watery marshes, with only reeds and water plants stretching as far as the eye could see. Moreover, she was from a good family, with slender and delicate legs, unable to bear the hardship of traveling. Fearing that they might come looking for her, she then ran as fast as she could.

After a long time, the eastern sky began to lighten, and looking far into the woods, Madam Wang saw houses and hastened to seek refuge there. Upon arrival, the gate was still closed, and the faint sound of bells and chanting could be heard. After a short while, the gate was opened, and it turned out to be a nunnery. Madam Wang entered directly, and the abbess asked the reason for her arrival. Madam Wang dared not tell the truth and instead said, "I am from Zhenzhou. My father-in-law traveled to Jiang-Zhe for official business and brought the family along. He arrived at his post but my husband passed away. I have been widowed for several years. My father-in-law then remarried me to the magistrate of Yongjia as his second wife. The first wife is fierce and difficult to deal with, subjecting me to countless beatings and insults. Recently, he resigned from his position, and the boat is moored here. Because of the Mid-Autumn Festival moon viewing, he ordered me to fetch a golden cup to pour wine. Unintentionally, I dropped it into the river, and he wanted to kill me. I fled to this place for safety." The abbess said, "Madam, you dare not return to the boat, your hometown is far away, and you wish to find another match, but finally lack a good matchmaker. Alone and destitute, where can you rely on?" Madam Wang could only weep.

The nun then said, "I have a piece of advice to offer, I wonder what your esteemed opinion is?" The woman replied, "If my teacher has something to advise, I will have no regrets even if it costs my life!" The nun said, "This place is situated in a remote coastal area where human footsteps rarely reach. Living beside reeds and water plants, being friends with seagulls and herons, we are fortunate to have a few fellow practitioners, all over fifty years old, and their attendants are also very simple and honest. Although you are young and beautiful, your fate and timing are unfortunate. Why not let go of attachment and delusion, realize that the body is illusory, wear the monastic robe and shave your head, and enter monastic life? The monk's bed and the Buddha's lamp, breakfast and evening porridge, passing the days following the Dharma, wouldn't it be better than being a favored concubine, suffering in this life, and making enemies in the next life?" The woman bowed and thanked her, saying, "That is what I wish to do." She then shaved her head in front of the Buddha and took the Dharma name Hui Yuan. Wang read books and learned to write, and was skilled in both calligraphy and painting. Within a few months, she thoroughly studied Buddhist scriptures and was greatly respected and treated well by the abbot. All matters, big or small, unless Wang approved, nobody dared to act on their own. She was gentle, kind, and virtuous, and everyone loved her. Every day she would pay over a hundred bows before the Bodhisattva Guanyin, secretly confiding her innermost feelings, even in the coldest winters and hottest summers, she never stopped. Afterward, she would immediately retreat to a secluded room, and few people saw her face.

After more than a year, someone suddenly came to the temple to visit, left offerings, and then departed. The next day, the person brought a scroll of painted lotus flowers as a gift, and the old nun hung it on a plain screen. As Wang passed by and saw it, he recognized the handwriting as that of a talented person and asked about its origin. The temple master replied, "It was recently donated by a benefactor." Wang asked, "What is the benefactor's name? Where does he live now? What does he do for a living?" The master answered, "He is Gu Axiu from the same county. His brothers make their living by plying boats. Recently, he has been doing well, but people often say he robs and plunders on the rivers and lakes. I don't know if that is true." Wang then asked, "Has he ever come here before?" The master replied, "He comes here rarely." Wang immediately remembered the person. He then took a brush and wrote on the screen, saying: 岁余，忽有人至院随喜，留斋而去。明日，持画芙蓉一轴来施，老尼张于素屏。王过见之，识为英笔，因询所自。院主曰：“近日檀越布施。”王问：“檀越何姓名？今住甚处？以何为生？”曰：“同县顾阿秀，兄弟以操舟为业。年来如意，人颇道其劫掠江湖间，未知诚然否？”王又问：“亦尝往来此中乎？”曰：“少到耳。”即默识之。乃援笔题于屏上曰：

In his youth, he wielded the brush of Zhang Chang with grace and elegance, his skill in painting living things not inferior to Huang Quan. He painted lotuses with the most vivid and beautiful colors. Who would have thought that such a beautiful color could bring a grievance of life and death! The powdered colors depict a desolate scene that seems like an illusion. Now it is wandering and who will pity it. The plain screen is lonely, accompanying the dry meditation. The bond of this life has been severed, I wish to form a new bond in the next life. The poem is likely a *Ling Jiang Xian* (临江仙). The nuns do not understand what it means. One day, suddenly in the city, there was a man named Guo Qingchun, who came to the temple for some other matter. He saw the painting and the inscription, and was delighted by its exquisite beauty, so he bought it to keep as a fine object. Coincidentally, the Grand Censor Gao Nalin, who had retired to Suzhou, was collecting paintings and calligraphy. Guo Qingchun presented the screen to him, and Gao placed it in his inner hall but did not have time to inquire about its details. Suddenly, someone outside was selling four pieces of grass script calligraphy. Gao took them to look at them, and found the style similar to Huaisu but clear and vigorous, not vulgar. He asked, "Who wrote this?" The seller replied, "It is mine, a student of calligraphy." Gao looked at his face and found him not an ordinary person. He immediately asked about his hometown and name, but the man frowned and replied, "My surname is Ying, my given name is Junchen, and I come from Zhenzhou. Because of my father's favor, I was appointed as the magistrate of Yongjia. I took my family with me to take up the post, but I was not careful, and was robbed by boatmen, sinking me into the water. My family's wealth, my wife and concubines, I no longer cared about. Fortunately, I had learned to swim when I was young, and I swam underwater. When I felt I was far away, I climbed ashore and went to a commoner's house, but my body was completely wet, and I had no money on me.

Thanks to the kind host, he exchanged his clothes, was treated with food and drink, and given money for travel. The host sent him off with these words: "After being robbed by bandits, according to the law, one should report to the authorities; we dare not keep you, for fear of being implicated." Ying then asked for directions and left the city, reporting to the Pingjiang Road authorities. Now waiting for a year, no news has come, and he survives by selling his calligraphy, not daring to claim he is skilled in writing. Unintentionally, he wrote a bad letter, which reached the imperial review. The official heard his words and deeply pitied him, saying, "Since you are like this, there is nothing to be done! And let you stay in my western study to teach my grandchildren calligraphy, is it not acceptable?" Ying was very fortunate. The official invited him into the inner hall and shared a drink with him. Suddenly, Ying saw a lotus on the screen, and tears welled up in his eyes. The official asked him curiously. Ying said, "This is one of the lost items from the boat, written by me. How did it end up here?" He recited the words again and said, "It was written by my wife." The official asked, "How do you recognize it?" Ying replied, "Recognize the handwriting. And the meaning of the words is clear; it is undoubtedly written by a clumsy woman." The official said, "If so, I will take on the responsibility of catching the thieves. You should keep it secret." Then, the official invited Ying to stay under his roof.

The next day, the official secretly summoned Qingchun to question him. Qingchun replied, "I bought it from a nunnery." The official then subtly interrogated the nun: "From whom did you get it? Who inscribed or composed it?" Several days later, she reported, "From the residence of Gu Axiu in the same county, the nun Huiyuan inscribed it." The official sent someone to persuade the abbot: "My wife enjoys reciting Buddhist scriptures but has no one to accompany her. She heard that Huiyuan has attained enlightenment and now wishes to treat her as a teacher. I hope you will not refuse." The abbot refused. However, when Huiyuan heard this, she deeply wished to leave, perhaps using this opportunity to take revenge. The nun could not refuse. The official ordered her to be carried there and had the wife share her quarters with her. On a leisure day, the wife asked about her family background in detail. Wang wept and told the truth, also mentioning the matter of the inscribed lotus, saying, "The thief is not far away. Only you can inform the official. If you can catch the culprit, clear your past disgrace, and thus repay your husband, then the official's favor will be great!" But she did not know that her husband's fate was already decided. The wife told the official, and said that she reads books and is virtuous, definitely not a girl from a humble family. The official knew for sure that she was Ying's wife and ordered the wife to treat her well, barely speaking to Ying. The official investigated Gu's residence and movements but dared not act rashly. He only had the wife secretly encourage Wang to grow her hair and return to her initial attire.

After half a year, the successful candidate Xue Li Puhua became an Inspector of Prefectures. Puhua was a former subordinate of a high-ranking official named Gao, and he knew of his skillful hand. He fully informed Puhua, who secretly arrested him. The imperial edict and family wealth were still there, but the whereabouts of Wang were unknown. After thorough interrogation, it was said: "I truly intended to keep her for my second son and did not take precautions anymore. Unexpectedly, she escaped on the Mid-Autumn Festival of that year, and no one knows where she went." Puhua then placed him under the harshest punishment and gave the original property to Ying. Ying was about to bid farewell to the official before taking up his post, and the official said, "Wait and arrange a marriage for you, marry first and then leave, it's not too late." Ying replied, "My wife who shared poverty with me for a long time. Unfortunately, she has wandered to another place, and her fate is uncertain. If I go there alone, delayed by time, if heaven and earth show mercy and she is still alive, perhaps I hope for the reunion of husband and wife. Grateful for your kindness, even death cannot forget, the words about remarrying, are not what I desire."

The official was moved to tears and said, "Your noble friendship is so great, Heaven must surely provide some aid. How dare I press you hard. But allow me to see you off with a banquet, then you may depart." The next day, a feast was held, and officials and renowned scholars from the prefecture gathered. The official raised his cup and told the guests, "Today, I am concluding my earthly bond with County Magistrate Cui." The guests did not understand. The official then had Huiyuan brought forth, and she was his former wife. The couple held each other and wept bitterly, not expecting to meet again. The official recounted the entire story and showed the guests a screen with the phrase "Fulfil one's earthly bond," which was a line from his former wife's poetry, and Huiyuan was the name his former wife had changed to. Everyone in the hall was moved to tears and praised the official's great virtue as beyond reach. The official gave Ying one servant and one maid and provided funds to send him on his way. After Ying completed his term, he passed through Wu again, but the official had already passed away. The couple wept bitterly, as if they had lost a close relative, and established a water and land斋 for three days and nights at the tomb to pay their respects before leaving. The Wang family thus maintained a continuous斋 and chanted the name of Guanyin without interruption.

A Talented Scholar Named Lu Zhongyan Composed a Song About the Painted Lotus Screen, to Record the Events, and It Is Recorded to Warn the World: Painting the lotus, I dare not inscribe on the screen! The blood and tears on the screen are as red as the flowers. Withered leaves and dry branches are both desolate, torn silk and scattered ink are all fallen apart. The flowing water separates life and death, alone and in solitude, I become a wanderer. As a wanderer, to whom can my remains be entrusted? The soul in the spring (grave) does not return, the beautiful figure in the painting is still like yesterday. Still like yesterday, my heart is wounded, how can I endure the autumn rain and frost again! Rather than follow a boatman on the rivers and lakes, I willingly follow the Medicine King in the sacred place. The Medicine King is inherently compassionate, compassionately pitying all beings, the soul wishes to be pulled out, the lonely widow relies on being led. The lotus flower is charming, the husband personally painted it. The flower withers because the stem is broken, drying up is caused by injuring the seedling, the heart is still bitter even though the petals are dry, the root rots and the hatred is hard to erase. It is said that Han Hong wept at Zhangtai, who would have expected to meet Wen Xiao in the Jia tent. The lotus flower indeed has meaning, the lotus cannot be abandoned. Fortunately, the moon and the pearl are reunited, love each other and do not abandon each other. Who can listen to my lotus flower poem? Husbands and wives in the world should not quarrel, look at this lotus flower, it is truly pitiable.

### 秋千会记 In the year 1298 AD, during the reign of Emperor Chengzong of the Yuan Dynasty, Bo Luo, a descendant of the former ruling family of the Jin Dynasty, was appointed as the Minister of the Imperial Household. Alongside him were Yin Du La, who served as a deputy minister, and Dong Ping Wang Rong Fu, who served as an official. The three families lived in a row on the west side of Hai Zi Bridge. Bo Luo, born into a family of high officials, enjoyed extreme wealth and power. His mansion was grand and magnificent, unmatched by any other noble family. Despite his high status, he was well-read and skilled in writing, respected scholars, and was widely praised for his virtues. Behind his private residence, he had a garden dedicated to apricot trees, inspired by the famous line from a poem by Ye Shao-weng: "The spring scenery cannot be confined within the garden; a branch of red apricot flowers extends over the wall." The garden was renowned for its unique flowers and beautiful pavilions, surpassing those of all other noble families. Every spring, Bo Luo's sisters and daughters would invite the wives and daughters of the deputy minister and the official to the garden for a swing festival. A grand banquet was set up, and they laughed and joked all day. Each family would also set up a feast every other day. The festival lasted from late February until after the Qingming Festival, known as the Swing Festival. One day, while passing by the garden, the son of the deputy minister of the Military Council, Tie Mu Er Bu Hua, heard laughter. He leaned forward on his horse to look at the garden and saw the women competing in swinging, with the joy and excitement at their peak. He peeked from under the willow trees and saw that all the women were extremely beautiful. He stayed for a long time, but was noticed by the gatekeeper, who ran to report to Bo Luo. When Bo Luo searched for him, he was gone.

Bai Zhu returned and reported to his mother in detail. The mother understood and then sent a matchmaker to the Xuanwei family to propose marriage. Xuanwei said, "Is he not the boy who peered over the wall? I am just choosing a son-in-law, you can send him to come and see, if he is indeed good, then I will agree." The matchmaker returned and reported back, and Tongqian dressed Bai Zhu and sent him there. Xuanwei saw that he was a handsome young man and felt somewhat pleased. However, he did not know of his talents and learning, so he tested him by saying, "You like to watch the swing, take this as the theme, 'Pusaman' as the tune, can you compose a Southern song?" Bai Zhu took up his brush and wrote in the national script: Please note that the phrase "以国字写之" is translated as "wrote in the national script," which refers to the traditional Chinese script. If you need a more specific or modern equivalent, please let me know.

A woman with delicate fingers, like tender shoots, and swallows flying in pairs under the east wind. Boasting about beauty and competing in height, she ties her skirt tighter. She sleeps soundly on a bed, letting her golden hairpin fall. She pushes aside the pillow and wakes up late, as the moon rises through the gauze window. The Xuanwei admired his quickness but feared it might be pre-planned or done by someone else. Therefore, he hosted a grand banquet for him. During the banquet, he ordered him to write another *Man Jiang Hong* about the oriole. Bai Zhu wiped the Xianteng paper and wrote in Chinese characters, presenting it to the Xuanwei. The Xuanwei was pleased and said, "I have found a son-in-law!" He agreed to marry his third wife's daughter, Suge Shili, to him and called the wife and her daughter out to meet Bai Zhu. The other daughters also peeked through the window and secretly congratulated Suge Shili, saying, "It can be said that 'the door is full of joy, and the son-in-law is nearly riding a dragon.'" They chose a day to send the betrothal gifts, with abundant gifts and elegant words, spreading throughout the capital as a grand event. Bai Zhu's poem about the oriole is appended here: On a clear day, the spring light is bright, and the blue sky is newly washed. Just as peach cheeks are half revealed, and the oriole's song begins. Alone in bed, she suddenly hears the quiet strings, and through the curved screen, she listens to the fine sound of the flute. She loves the soft and gentle tongue that resonates with the east wind, even more charming. After waking from a dream, idle worries linger. The remaining peach blossoms fade, and the heavy doors close. The clever and fragrant melody, ten times more graceful. Entering the willows and passing through the flowers, coming and going, seeking a true friend is truly impossible. Looking up to the imperial garden, when will she be able to find a mate? Her heart is distant.

Shortly thereafter, the official became unrestrained and undisciplined, ultimately failing due to corruption. He was imprisoned in the Yushi Tai prison and fell ill while in custody. As a high-ranking official, he was released by special grace and returned home for treatment, but within a short period, he passed away. The entire household fell ill, leaving only Baisi alive. The family's wealth and influence completely collapsed. The Xuanwei official planned to summon Baisi back home to educate and support him, but the three influential ladies insisted and refused. Although there were many favored concubines, the three ladies held exclusive power and favor. Seeing that other concubines' daughters returned to wealthy families, they were shocked that their son-in-law's family had fallen into such poverty and decisively decided to break the engagement. Suge Shili advised, saying, "To marry is to form a bond; once an alliance is made, it cannot be changed. The son did not fail to see the prosperity of his sisters' families and also envied them. But once a thread is tied, even gods and spirits cannot deceive. How can one abandon him because of his poverty and low status?" The parents did not listen and instead discussed marrying him to the son of Pingzhang Kuokuo. The ceremonial rituals were even more elaborate than before. When the marriage was consummated, Suge Shili was on his way halfway and secretly untied his foot scarf, hanging himself in the sedan chair. He died by the time they arrived. The lady took her beloved daughter back in a sedan chair and used all the dowry and the groom's gifts to prepare the funeral, temporarily placing the deceased in the Qing'an Buddhist temple.

Bai Zhu heard of the change and, that night, secretly went to mourn it, knocking on the coffin and saying, "Bai Zhu is here." Suddenly, a voice from within the coffin responded, "Open the coffin, and I will live." He looked all around the four corners, noting that the lacquer nails were firmly fastened, making it impossible to open. He then planned with the monk, saying, "Spare no effort; the sin of opening the coffin, I will take full responsibility, and I will not burden you. We will share all the contents." The monk, who had always known about the elaborate burial, also had the intention to gain something and so chopped off the lid. The woman indeed came to life, and both were extremely happy. He then took off half of the gold bracelets and jewelry to thank the monk. Calculating the rest, they still amounted to tens of thousands of strings of cash. He then entrusted the monk to buy lacquer and repair the coffin, so that the matter would not be revealed. Bai Zhu then took Suge Shili to Shangdu, where they stayed for a year, and no one knew about it. The items they carried were abundant, and Bai Zhu also taught several Mongol students, having a monthly salary, which made their household comfortable.

Unexpectedly, the official left office in Kai Ping, and upon arriving, immediately sought a guest teacher, but there were extremely few scholars in the capital. Someone said, "Recently, there was a scholar from the capital who settled here with his family. He is also a colored person and taught privately in the community. He is truly knowledgeable." The official wanted to hire a private tutor, and only this person was suitable. He called him immediately, and it was Baizhu. The official thought he must have died in poverty, but Baizhu appeared well-mannered and composed, which was strange. The official asked, "How did you end up here? And whom did you marry?" Baizhu told the truth. The official did not believe him and ordered him to be carried to the place, where it was found to be Zhen Sugeshili. The whole family was shocked, both happy and sad. However, they still feared that he was a ghost impersonating a person, deceiving the young. They secretly sent someone to Qing'an to ask the monk, and his words were the same. Then they opened the coffin, and it was found to be empty. They returned to report to the official, who felt ashamed and sighed, treating him even better, taking him as a son-in-law, and he lived out his life there.

Bai Zhu had three sons: the eldest, Jiaohua, served up to the position of Left Censor of the Pacification Commission of Liaoyang and its Surroundings but died young. The second son was Manggu Dai, and the youngest was Heisi. Both became members of the imperial guard and carried the emperor's personal weapons. Manggu Dai died first. Heisi reached the position of Chief of the Privy Council. When the imperial army arrived in Yan, Emperor Toghon Temür ruled from the Qingning Hall, gathering the empresses, concubines, and the crown prince to discuss how to avoid the war. Heisi and Chancellor Shilumen cried and advised, saying, "The world is the world of Emperor Shizu. It should be defended to the death." The emperor did not listen. At midnight, they opened Jiande Gate and fled. Heisi followed into the desert, and his fate is unknown. The Tale of Jia Yunhua's Return to Life Wei Peng, courtesy name Yuyan, was originally from Juzhu. His ninth-generation ancestor, Fei Qing, served as Vice President of the Censorate during the reign of Emperor Gaozong of Song. Because he criticized Qin Hui for mismanaging the country, he was demoted to the position of magistrate of Xiangyang, died, and was buried on White Horse Mountain, after which his descendants remained there. The clan grew prosperous, their wealth comparable to that of a noble, and especially so during the Yuan dynasty. Wei Peng's father, Wuchen, was a provincial governor in Jiangzhe during the early years of the Yuan dynasty's reign of Emperor Renzong, and Wei Peng was born in the government office while his father died. His mother, Lady Xiao of Yingguo, took Wei Peng and his two brothers, Nie and Qi, along with the coffin, back to Xiangyang. Wei Peng, at five years old, had mastered the Five Classics, and at seven, could write essays. His skin was radiant, and his eyes and eyebrows were like paintings. The villagers called him a prodigy. During the reign of Emperor Huizong of Yuan, he failed multiple times in the imperial examinations and deeply resented it.

He once said, "A true man should easily achieve fame and success, yet and yet cannot even obtain a degree!" Then he sighed deeply. Lady Xiao, upon hearing this, feared that he would become depressed and fall ill, so she commanded him, saying, "Qiantang, your father's hometown is Tongxiang. All the renowned teachers and scholars of this era are mostly former students or officials of your father. Go and seek their teachings, perhaps there will be some success. Especially in the southeastern region, which is a great province with extraordinary landscapes. Which can broaden the mind and inspire poetic expression. Go forth! Do not limit yourself to one place." Then he took out a sealed letter from his chest and gave it to him, saying, "When you are free from studying there, you should visit the former Chancellor Jia Pingzhang's wife, Lady Mo of Xingguo, and present this to her to discuss your marriage. I have my reasons, be careful not to open it rashly." The son left, privately opened the seal, and learned that before he was born, his mother had a prearranged marriage agreement with her. Overjoyed, he hastened to set out. The letter from Lady Mo, attached below:

Yi Gong, with folded sleeves, bows twice in deep respect and presents this letter before the desk of Lady Tai of the State of Xing: Yi Gong has been parted from you for fifteen years, separated by thousands of miles, each under a different heaven, and we have not heard from each other. I recall the harmonious will of Heaven and Earth, the gentle and wise arrangements of fate — joy fills the gate, blessings reach the inner chambers, and how can one adequately express the admiration and envy I feel! As for Yi Gong, having lost the one who was my heaven, I have maintained my virtue, and my family, young and old, have managed to live in a state of rough peace — not worth mentioning to Lady Tai. Yet I think of my late husband’s colleague and my late husband, who were officials, though colleagues in office, they were like brothers in sentiment. I have been treated by Lady Tai as a sister-in-law. At the time I became pregnant, we each made vows. Lady Tai once cited the story of Emperor Guangwu of Han and General Zhu Fu, pointing to my womb and saying: “If a son is born, my daughter will marry him; if a daughter is born, my son will marry her.” Afterwards, the gods opened my heart, and Heaven arranged this union — joy came to our household with the birth of a son, and our humble home gained a strong male heir. Unfortunately, before this could be realized, my husband passed away. I took care of the orphans and carried his coffin back for burial, but the mountains and rivers were far, and we had no chance to meet. Now, my son has reached the age of manhood, and my daughter, I suppose, is also of marriageable age. If they have not yet been betrothed, I hope they will follow the old promise. Therefore, I dare to submit this letter, conveying my sincere feelings, and have had this son personally deliver it to you. If he reaches your doorstep, I hope you will look upon him kindly. I await your golden promise, and bow in anticipation of your reply. Until we meet, as I close this letter, I feel deep sorrow. I remain, respectfully, Yi Gong.

The protagonist received his command and prepared to depart the next day. After two months, he arrived in Hangzhou and rented a residence near the northern gate from an old woman. The woman was hospitable, and the protagonist found himself quite at ease. After several days, once his lodging was settled, he gradually began to go out. He visited his old friends, but none of them were there. All he saw were the beautiful lakes and mountains, with clear and beautiful scenery filling his view. The sound of carriages and horses was loud, and the music and singing filled his ears. The protagonist then composed a poem titled "Man Ting Fang" to record the beauty of the place, and he wrote it on the paper window of his rented residence. The poem reads: A powerful province in the world, a famous county in Zhejiang, since ancient times only speaks of Qiantang. Clear water and beautiful mountains, people and things are extraordinary. How many noble mansions, in the midst of the crowd, compete to play string and wind instruments. A young guest, casually carrying a green qin, goes everywhere playing the "Seeking the Phoenix" tune. Wandering and laughing at oneself, have not achieved fame and success, who will give a red leaf? Do not need to be sad, the willows are tender and the flowers are fragrant. Hearing that the Blue Bridge is nearby, wish to drink nectar in this life. At that time, Yun Ying looked at it, Pai Hang was overjoyed.

An elderly woman from the neighborhood saw him and asked, "Does this belong to the young man?" The young man did not answer. The woman said, "Young man, do you think the old woman is not a connoisseur of music? Generally speaking, *yuefu* poetry should be subtle and profound. This poem is good, but it lacks charm. The works of Ouyang Xiu, Yan Shu, Qin Guan, and Huang Tingjian are perhaps not like this." Hearing this, the young man was greatly surprised and replied with thanks, "I have made many mistakes with my shallow and unrefined words." He then inquired about the woman's background and learned that she had once been the favorite concubine of Chancellor Da Mu. After the chancellor's death, she was married into a common family. She is now old, but she is well-versed in poetry and literature, skilled in music and rhythm, enjoys conversation, and is adept at embroidery. She frequently visits the homes of high-ranking officials and serves as a teacher to women, and everyone calls her Madam Bian. The young man said, "Then Chancellor Da Mu was indeed a contemporary of my father, the Political Advisor, and Grand Councillor Jia." The woman was surprised and asked, "Are you the son of Political Advisor Wei?" The young man replied, "Yes." The woman said, "Indeed, you are the very person Confucius referred to when he said one should praise one's own children." She then offered the young man a drink, and he was able to ask her about the chancellor's former colleagues. The woman said, "They are all gone now, except for the Jia family." The young man said, "My mother has a letter for them. Could you deliver it on my behalf?" The woman agreed.

The young man then asked, "After the chancellor abandoned his salary for several years, who is still alive now? How are they doing?" The old woman replied, "The chancellor has one son named Lin, courtesy name Lingzhao. He also has a daughter named Pingping, courtesy name Yunhua. She was born when her mother dreamed of a peacock carrying a peony bud and placing it in her womb. In terms of appearance, she is like a peach blossom reflected in spring water; in terms of demeanor, she is like a cloud greeting the morning sun. Her fingers are as slender as carved jade, and her hair is tied up like graceful silk. In composing lyrics and setting music, she is difficult to follow in the footsteps of Li Qingzhao; in weaving brocade and embroidery, no one can match Su Ruolan! Lady Xing deeply loves her and has her study under me. I consider myself inferior to her. Moreover, Lady Xing is diligent and skilled in managing her property. She wears pearl-adorned shoes and tortoiseshell hairpins, not falling short of her former prosperity; she enjoys a life of luxury and abundance, much like the past. When the young man heard this, he knew she must be the one destined for a prearranged marriage and was eager to go. However, the old woman was suffering from eye disease and could not proceed, so he stopped. Lady Xing was surprised that the old woman had not come for a long time and sent her maid Chunhong to inquire at the old woman's house. At that time, the old woman's eyes had improved and wanted to go with the young man, but he happened to be out. The old woman then followed Chunhong and went to Lady Xing's house to thank her and also to tell her about the matter of the letter sent by Wei Sheng's mother. Lady Xing was startled and said, "I was just thinking about it, and now here you are. Quickly summon him for me, do not delay!" Chunhong received the command and went again to invite the young man, who agreed to go with her.

Having reached the gate, Hong entered first. Soon after, two servants in green robes led the student to the main hall, where he stood at the eastern steps. Duchess Xing, dressed in her ceremonial attire, emerged and sat in the hall. The student bowed twice. The lady said, "When did Wei come?" The student replied, "Just a few days ago." She gestured for him to sit on a jeweled chair before her in the west. After tea, the lady said, "I remember when we parted, you were still in swaddling clothes, but now you have grown up so much!" She comforted and encouraged him greatly, and also inquired about the well-being of Lady Xiao and the children. The student replied that they were all well. The lady recounted old stories as if they were happening now, but she did not mention the betrothal made by pointing to the bellies. The student was puzzled by this, so he turned to the old servant Qingshan who had followed him, opened his bag, and handed up his mother's letter. The lady opened the envelope, read it, put it in her sleeve, and said nothing.

A short while later, a young child emerged, graceful as jade. The lady ordered the child to bow to the man. The man returned the bow. The lady said, "This is a little boy; we should teach him to return the courtesy, but he is too young to understand such formalities." She then instructed her maid, Autumn Moon, to call Pingping. Soon, an elderly woman led two maidservants, escorting a woman who approached slowly and gracefully from behind a screen embroidered with flowers. She bowed before the person in front of her. The man hesitated, wanting to stand up and step back. The lady said, "It's okay! She is just a young lady." After the bow, she stood to the right of the lady's seat. The elderly woman also remained seated at the side. The man secretly glanced at Pingping and was struck by her extraordinary beauty, which even compared to Xi Shi and Luo Shen, it would be hard to say which was more beautiful. His soul and spirit were transported, his heart and eyes were drawn, and fearing that the lady would notice, he immediately stood up and excused himself. The lady said, "The previous Chancellor viewed the previous Censor as family, and your mother also regards me as a sister."

Since the death of my two fathers, the two families have been separated, like fish sinking and geese disappearing, with no news or messages heard. I originally thought we would never see each other again in this life, but who would have thought that in my remaining years I would see such excellence? My old heart is filled with joy and comfort, how can it be fully expressed! Is the young master so unfeeling? The guest bows and returns to his seat, dare not refuse again. Madame Xing looks at Pīng entering, as if she were instructing someone to prepare the feast. At that moment, the feast begins, with all kinds of dishes from both land and sea laid out. The lady-in-waiting personally pours wine for the guest, who kneels to receive and drinks. She then orders Lin and Pīng to take turns pouring wine. When Pīng's wine comes, the guest declines by saying, "I just arrived from afar and have been away from alcohol for a long time, so I cannot handle the wine now." Pīng holds the cup and bows twice. The guest wants to look at her closely but she firmly declines and dares not drink first. Madame Xing says, "You should kneel and pour." Pīng then kneels, and the guest hastily accepts and drinks it all in one go.

Píngpíng picked up her cup and went to the lady's presence, pouring the remaining wine onto the table and saying, "My brother has not finished drinking, may I have another cup?" The lady smiled and said, "We have just become siblings, and we already feel affection for each other. How could the young gentleman suddenly stop?" The old woman also encouraged them from the side, and the young man finished his drink. The lady then asked the old woman, "Since the young gentleman has stayed in your home, why did you not inform us earlier? You should have offered him a full cup." The old woman laughed and drank. After the banquet, she said she would return home. The lady said, "The young gentleman should not return to the inn. He can stay at my humble home." The young man slightly declined. The lady said, "My home is poor and quiet, I hope you will not find it distasteful." She immediately called her servants Tuōhuān and Yítóng, who led the young man to a room in the east wing outside the front hall to stay. The young man entered the door and saw screens, curtains, beds, and bedding, writing desks and washbasins, writing brushes, inkstones, zithers, and chess sets—everything was fully prepared. The old woman's belongings were also there. The young man had settled down and met a beautiful woman, feeling both surprised and happy, he could not sleep. He composed a poem titled "Wind Enters the Pine" and wrote it on a white wall while drunk. The poem says: Twelve green cities overlook the lake's edge, the mountains and waters are even more clear and beautiful. This land has been a place of ancient prosperity, the scenery is good, and there is singing and music all day long. Peach and plum trees by Su Xiaozhi's house, people living by Po Gong's dike. Silk windows and embroidered curtains lock the graceful lady, yet she is as distant as heaven. Hongniang does not send a letter to Zhang Sheng, the story of the western chamber, only fears it is a false tale. How could it compare to the bronze mirror cast, which brings immediate reunion!

That evening, Pinging returned to her room and entrusted the young man with a significant task. She then called her servant girl Zhu Ying and asked, "Is Brother Wei awake?" Zhu Ying replied, "I don't know." Pinging instructed her, "Go to the side room and check." After a long time, Zhu Ying returned and reported, "Young Master Wei is softly reciting under the candlelight, as if deep in thought. He then took up his brush and wrote several lines on the wall. I looked at it carefully and it is a poem titled 'Wind Enters the Pine.'" Pinging asked, "Do you remember it?" Zhu Ying replied, "I have remembered it." She then recited the poem once. Pinging then wet her brush, unfolded the double phoenix cloud-patterned paper, followed the rhyme scheme, and composed a poem in a short time. She sealed it and gave it to Zhu Ying, saying, "In the morning, when you serve Young Master Wei his morning tea and he washes his face, give this to him." Zhu Ying put the poem in her bag. The next morning at dawn, Zhu Ying followed the instructions. When Young Master Wei had finished washing, Zhu Ying took out the sealed poem and handed it to him, saying, "Miss Pinging has a message for you, there is a letter to deliver." Young Master Wei hastily took and looked at it, and it was a response to his own poem written on the wall titled 'Wind Enters the Pine.' The poem read: The jade maiden lives by the Han River, her beauty and talent are as fresh as spring. Heaven has endowed her with a graceful demeanor. She has excellent talent and skill in music. Her literary talent is like stars in her chest, her words flow like clouds and smoke from her pen. The new jade from Lantian shines beautifully, the warm sun brightens the clear sky. She should visit the palace of the moon, she should personally teach the *Nirvana* dance. It would be good to ask Chang'e, why isn't the ice wheel (moon) made round.

The student read it several times, unable to put it down, and understood that Ping's emotions were especially intense. He then carefully stored it in his scholar's bag. Just as he was about to inquire in detail about Ping's character, the lady-in-waiting had already sent a servant to summon him. The student went in with the servant, and the lady-in-waiting greeted him upon his arrival, saying, "My young master has come here on the orders of your mother, to travel and study far from home. You must not waste your time and let your days go by idly. There is a great scholar, Mr. He, here, and his disciples number in the hundreds. If you follow him, you will surely benefit. The gift-giving ceremony, I have already prepared." After eating, she asked him to leave. Upon seeing Ping again, all his thoughts turned to ashes; he no longer sought fame or recognition, thinking only of the clouds and flowers. Unaware that the lady-in-waiting had pressured him to study, he reluctantly agreed, but did not go often. Reflecting that although the lady-in-waiting loved him greatly, she never mentioned marriage, and instead made him believe Ping was his sister, he suspected something was amiss but had no way to question it. He then secretly went to the temple dedicated to Wu Zixu to seek a dream, and received a divine message: "The person in the Snow Washing Hall is reborn, and only under the moon will he meet Chang'e." Upon waking, he did not understand the meaning, but privately remembered it. One day, while casually touring West Lake with friends, Ping waited until the student was not around, took her maid Lan Tiao, and secretly went to his room, reading through all his writings. She found a copy of *The Story of the Crimson Red* and smiled at Tiao, saying, "My young master reads this book, won't it corrupt his mind?" She then playfully wrote two quatrains on the screen of his bed. The poems read:

A clean desk and bright windows, without a speck of dust; daily companionship with sages and virtuous people. A simple and uncluttered study room, without any extraneous items; only book spines and the refined scholar are present. Flowers and willows are fragrant and lush in February; famous gardens still have branches of peonies. Do you still know the elegant Du Mu? Do not regret arriving late for the spring season.

Until dusk, the man returned home, saw the poem, realized it was written by Ping, and deeply regretted going out, as he could not see her. He then continued the rhyme, using Zhao Mengfu’s style of running cursive script, and wrote the poem on a piece of ornate paper to reply to Ping. The poem read: Ice-like skin and jade-like bones emerge from the dust, Across the water, she stands gracefully, yet unattainable. Leaving behind several lines of pearls and jade, Entrusting these words to a true lover. The young peach tree is just beginning to bloom, It doesn’t allow deep red to fully bloom. Because it blooms easily but fades quickly, The Eastern Lord intentionally delays the blooming. After finishing, he had no means to send it. As he hesitated, suddenly Chunhong arrived and said to the man, “Madam has heard that you returned from West Lake and fears you are overcome by wine. She has sent me to bring you a cup of Wuyi Dragon Pearl tea to drink.” The man was very pleased and immediately took a sip. He then moved closer to Chunhong and sat beside her, smiling and saying, “Pingping regards me as her elder brother. Why are you so reluctant to become my temporary wife for a moment?” Chunhong turned pale and said, “Madam manages the household with strict discipline. The maidservants only serve and obey. How dare I offer my bed to you, thereby defiling your noble virtue?” The man replied, “The peach and plum blossoms in the east garden, though brief, are still spring. What harm is there?” And so, he became intimate with Chunhong. He also said to her, “I have a letter for Pingping. Can you deliver it for me?” Chunhong replied, “How dare I not accept the task? I will deliver it as soon as possible.” Chunhong entered and met Ping in the tea hall, giving her the letter. Ping quickly placed it in her bosom and instructed Chunhong not to reveal it. Returning to her room, she read the letter, which contained two quatrains in response to hers. After reading, she sighed, “Clear and flowing, it is like her character.” Before she could finish speaking, she heard Madam call, “There is a guest.”

Peng stepped out, and it was her brother-in-law, Mo Youren, who had come from Gaotown to visit. The wife, therefore, set up a banquet to entertain him, and the husband also sat down with them. The wife, feeling both sorrow and joy at the long separation with her brother-in-law, expressed mixed emotions. The aunt and niece encouraged each other with drinks, and unconsciously they became drunk. Since Mo Youren had traveled far, riding horseback, he was tired and could not handle more alcohol, urgently wanting to rest, he pleaded with the wife. The wife then ordered Tuohan to help Mo Youren to rest in a small room south of the guest hall. The husband also came out and stood alone in the main hall. Soon, the wife also felt dizzy and wanted to sleep, so she went to bed first. Only Peng supervised the maids to clean up the dishes and lock the doors. Zhu Ying held a candle and accompanied Peng to patrol the main hall. Seeing the husband standing alone, she was surprised and said, "Brother, haven't you gone to bed yet? Why are you lingering here?" The husband replied that he was very thirsty, sought water but couldn't find any. Peng immediately ordered Zhu Ying to go to the kitchen to get tea. She then took the candle from Zhu Ying and placed it on the table, where the wind blew the candle, causing the wax to melt and drip. Peng used a golden scissors to trim the candle and said, "Are you also romantic?" The husband replied, "Don't you know the poem by Li Yishan? 'Spring silkworms spin silk until they die, and the candle tears dry up only when it burns to ash.'"

Peng said, "Yi Shan is just a wayward youth, why do you hold such deep affection?" The speaker replied, "People have the same heart, and the heart has the same desire. How can this be a fault of Yi Shan?" Peng asked, "Then are you also a follower of Yi Shan?" The speaker said, "My romantic and subtle thoughts, I believe, surpass his." Peng remarked, "If what you say is true, then you are indeed a refined and elegant person. But the good line says 'those who labor with their minds.' What exactly do they labor for? Don't you know that Li Shangyin also had such thoughts?" The speaker answered, "It's because the room is close but the person is far away." Peng did not respond, but pointed to the guqin hanging on the wall and asked, "Are you good at playing this?" The speaker said, "I was deeply interested in this skill from a young age. Miss also knows how to play it." Peng said, "It's just a gesture, how dare I say I can?" Soon, Zhu Ying brought tea, and Peng stood up to hand it to the speaker. The speaker thanked her, saying, "Why be so formal?" Peng replied, "Loving one's parents and respecting one's elder brother, it is proper to do so." The speaker tried to sit close and speak, but Peng quickly gathered herself and said, "Tonight is deep into the night, you should return to your room. There will be an opportunity tomorrow night, I will come to listen to the music, please don't go anywhere else." She then bowed and retreated. The next day, the lady was drunk and could not get up. At dusk, Peng secretly went to the side room.

The protagonist, 生, was anxiously awaiting, standing still in front of the steps, when suddenly he saw 婷 arrive. Overwhelmed with joy, he immediately embraced her and brought her inside. Once seated, 生 wiped the table and lit incense, then opened a brocade bag to take out the finely crafted 天风环佩琴, a guqin. He asked 婷 to play it. 婷, bashful, firmly refused. 生 then adjusted the tuning pegs and tuned the strings, playing the *Guān Jū* (*The Osprey*) to move her. 婷 remarked, "Every nuance of *yín*, *náo*, *chuò*, and *zhù* is exquisite, but the sound is too clever, and the pressing of the strings is slightly light!" 生 greatly admired her words and insisted on seeing her finger techniques, urging her repeatedly. 婷 then instructed 朱樱 to fetch the guqin, placing it on the table in front of her. She played the *Zhì Cháo Fēi* (*The Pheasant's Dawn Flight*) in response. 生 said, "Your finger techniques are superb, but this piece is somewhat overly sensuous in its sound." 婷 replied, "A woman without a husband, the lyrics are sorrowful, the sound mournful and plaintive—how can there be anything sensuous about it?" 生 said, "Unless it is the wife of a herdsman, how could such a piece be so exquisite?" 婷 remained silent, only offering a slight smile. That evening, their conversation became more intimate, and they spoke of feelings deeply. Just as the madam woke up, she called for 婷 to bring ginseng soup. 婷, frightened, hurried away. 生 was bewildered and lost in himself, his spirit completely gone, his face as pale as ashes, greatly disappointed. He then composed a poem titled *As If in a Dream* to lament his state. The poem reads: **Translation of the poem:** As if in a dream, I wander alone, The night deep, the moon dim. Joy turned to sorrow, heart lost, Words unspoken, tears unshed. The guqin's melody, soft and clear, Yet it could not mend my broken heart. The evening passed, the madam woke, And I was left to face the cold, empty room. In this fleeting moment, I see The fragility of love and hope. As if in a dream, I drift away, Leaving behind the echoes of my heart.

A Fine Moon, a Gentle Breeze, a Beautiful Night — I dreamed of the base of King Chu’s terrace. The clouds were thin and rain failed to form; the hoped-for meeting once again became empty words! A mistake, a mistake — I must endure this with my eyes wide open! At dawn, I rose, straightened my clothes and hat, and hurried to my wife’s chamber to inquire after her well-being. I went out to the main hall, turned behind it, followed the winding alley, intending to visit Peng’s chamber. I became lost and returned. Arriving before the Qingning Pavilion, I rested briefly. At that time, Peng was sitting in the pavilion, her hairpin lowered, her hair coiled in twin twists, wearing embroidered shoes. I immediately hid myself outside the door, peering through a crack, but I was seen by Peng’s maid, Fu Fu, who reported it to Peng. Peng became greatly angry and was about to rise and report to the wife. I, in great fear, told Peng, “I came here to inquire after your well-being, but got lost on the way. Could I, out of brother-sister affection, bear to see you embarrassed?” Peng replied, “A man has no reason to enter the central hall; how much more so to directly enter another’s private chamber? I will forgive you this time, but do not come again.” I bowed repeatedly in apology. Peng said, “I was only trying to scare you — no need for deep thanks!” She then pointed to a small clay pot near the pavilion, where a plant called Rui Xiang was growing, and told Fu Fu, “Take this plant and bring it to my brother’s bedroom — to be a companion for the solitary.” I said, “If I obtain this one plant, I will store it in a golden house.” Peng smiled and nodded in agreement. Fu then took the flower and escorted me out. Knowing that Fu was Peng’s attendant, I immediately took a few coins from my pouch and gave them to her, hoping she would pass on letters and secretly convey my affection. Fu bowed and accepted them, and from that moment on, I gained her favor.

After the protagonist had been away from home for more than two months, the Cold Food Festival had just passed, and the Qingming Festival had arrived. The hostess prepared food and wine, invited neighbors and elderly women, and took the protagonist out of the city to sweep the tombs. However, Pengping, who had recently recovered from a minor illness, could not join the outing. The protagonist noticed that Pengping was not going and pretended to leave. The hostess tried to keep him. He said, "Mr. He has just sent someone to call me, and I cannot refuse. I didn't get a chance to pay respects to the spirits of the ancestors, and I feel very regretful!" The hostess replied, "Since Mr. He has called, you should go quickly." The protagonist left, and the hostess also got into a carriage, with the entire family following. Only Fu Fu and the young servant Lan Tiao were left behind to accompany Pengping. The protagonist waited until the hostess was far away, then slowly returned. He reached the main hall, but the door was closed, and he could not enter. He wandered under the eaves. Fu Fu heard footsteps and thought it was a guest, so she opened the door and asked who it was. It turned out to be the protagonist. He quickly grabbed Fu Fu's sleeve, asked about Pengping's whereabouts, and wanted to see her.

Fu said, "The young lady is intelligent and well-versed in literature and etiquette. She is modest and cautious, always staying within the confines of her room. She is pure, quiet, and refined, with an air of dignity that makes her inviolable. How dare I rashly lead the master into something disrespectful, like offending Xi Shi?" The young man replied, "I consider our encounter as fated, even more so than the legendary matchmaker Hongniang from *The Story of the Western Chamber*. Now that you say this, I feel deeply disappointed!" Fu pondered for a while and said, "Although she adheres to propriety, her inner emotions are deeply felt. I have seen her look at herself in the mirror and turn to me and say, 'How do I compare to Chang'e, the moon goddess?' I replied, 'Isn't this boasting?' She then said, 'Chang'e is beautiful, but she cannot bear to be alone!' From this, we can see that she can be moved by emotion." The young man said, "What should we do now?" Fu said, "I have a handkerchief made of Wu silk. Please write a love poem on it, and I will show it to her. You should follow me closely and observe from behind. If she is moved, the plan will surely succeed." The young man happily took up his brush and wrote the poem, giving it to Fu. The poem reads:

The Silk of the Dragon Palace The silk originally comes from the dragon palace, always in the hands of a beautiful lady. It is reserved for the night of the wedding, when it is used to wipe the fresh blood on the branches of a crabapple tree. The good fortune of the handkerchief entering, the man follows closely behind, reaching the pine hall. Peng is leaning against the railing, playing with the new willows in the courtyard, saying, "The green shade is so lush!" She then recites a line from a poem by Xin Qiji: "Do not lean on the dangerous railing, for the setting sun is where the willows and smoke break the heart." The man quickly steps forward, placing his hand on her back and asking, "What is the purpose of breaking one's heart?" Peng is surprised and says, "The madman has come here again?" The man replies, "Is it indeed like this for the madman? Han Shou stole fragrant flowers, Sima Xiangru washed dishes, and the madman acts as such." Peng then orders Fu to bring tea. Fu pretends to drop the handkerchief on the ground, and Peng picks it up and reads the poem on it, becoming angry and saying, "This must be what my brother did, how dare this little girl be so reckless? I will take it to tell my mother." The man apologizes profusely and even kneels down. Peng smiles and puts the handkerchief in her bosom, saying, "Do not say more, let us sit together, enjoy a short moment of happiness, if my mother returns, then it will be too late." The man is greatly delighted and sits down. Peng calls Fu to bring out the delicacies and wine, personally holding a golden lotus cup and pouring wine to encourage the man. The man refuses to drink, but Peng persists in her persuasion. The man thanks her, saying, "Even if I eat a little, I will get drunk, no need for wine." He drinks a few cups, then orders the food and drink to be removed, and Peng complies.

The protagonist drew close to the woman and sat beside her, saying to her, "I have come here on behalf of my kind and loving parents to arrange this marriage. I have traveled a thousand miles, facing great difficulties by land and sea. Now, my lady, you have not spoken a single word about our previous agreement, and I fear you may have other plans. The situation may change, and you may want to be treated as siblings, which is clear in your intentions. You are indifferent and treat me like a stranger, which is quite uninteresting. I have long planned to return home, but I have hesitated because I have not spoken to you. Now, I am fortunate to meet you again, and it is difficult to meet again. My inner thoughts and feelings, you now know, so please clearly inform me whether our relationship will be harmonious or not. Do not just make me a stranded guest in Zhou South." The woman, upon hearing this, patted her thigh and sighed, saying, "Am I made of wood and stone? Your words, brother, do not understand me! Since I met you, I have forgotten my meals and neglected my duties. My heart is moved, and my spirit is tired. I sleep late at night and rise early in the morning, thinking only of the gentleman. Considering myself as a worthless plant, I am fortunate to serve in the inner chamber and live with you for a hundred years. However, I fear that heaven does not grant people convenience, and we may not have a good beginning and a good end. Zhang Gong and Shen Chun are clear examples. If you do not reject me, I can hold the broom forever, and we should not act lightly but plan for a hundred percent success." The protagonist said, "If we wait for the six rites of marriage to be completed, I will be long dead. You should pity me and not be stingy with tonight!" The woman had not yet responded when the servant Lan Tiao reported that the lady had returned. The protagonist hurried out in a fluster. That day was the third month, the day of Bingwu.

Early morning of the Ding Wei day, the protagonist entered to pay respects, and the lady said, "Yesterday, due to the tomb-sweeping, I visited several temples by the lake. The scenery was so beautiful that I couldn't take it all in, and what I regretted most was that the person I was waiting for was not here." The protagonist nodded and withdrew. Upon reaching the side door of the central hall, he met Ping, surrounded by her maids, who stood before and behind her, both of them staring at each other without exchanging a word. The protagonist returned to his room feeling dejected and recited a line from Cui Hao's poem "Yellow Crane Tower," saying, "Where is my hometown at dusk? The misty waves on the river make me sad!" Just as Ping passed by the window, she heard him and called out through the window, "Why do men feel so deeply attached to their homeland?" The protagonist replied, "The matter is complicated, and ultimately, I cannot achieve my goal. Staying here is not beneficial; it would be better to return." Ping said, "In a short while, I will send Fu to visit you." After saying this, she left. After breakfast, Fu indeed came and told the protagonist, "Miss Ping has a letter for you." The protagonist opened it and found a poem inside: Spring light is fleeting, and there may not be much more of it. Do not let such a fine night pass in vain. Sending words to the gallant scholar, let us make it so that tonight we see Chang'e.

After finishing, the student was so delighted that he could not restrain himself; he earnestly watched the sun slant westward, eagerly awaiting the arrival of night. Unexpectedly, just before noon, his friend Jin Zai Rong arrived and persuaded him to go to Pingkang. The student refused on the grounds of other matters, but Jin would not allow it, and in the end, he reluctantly went along. Upon arriving, there was a courtesan named Xiu Mei, who understood poetry and verse, and had long admired talented men. Seeing the student’s elegant bearing, she urged him to drink from a large cup, and Jin joined in the loud drinking. The student had no intention of drinking, but being pressed by the two of them, he became heavily drunk and returned home. He spread out a purple silk mat and lay on the ground beside the stone balustrade in front of his room. By the time the evening moon had brightened, his wife was asleep, and Peng took the opportunity to keep their appointment. But she found the student sound asleep, his breath reeking of alcohol. She called to him, but he did not respond. Disheartened, she walked down the steps. She slowly entered the student’s room, took a fine brush, and wrote a quatrain on his silk skirt, then put down the brush and left. The poem read: Mist and clouds of dawn and dusk seldom remain in place, In vain does the divine maiden descend from Wu’s peak. Xiangwang was indeed heartless, Drunk and lying in the glow of the moon and flower shadows. At the fifth watch, when the sky was bright, the student had also sobered up. He took a step into the shade of the flowers, but saw fallen blossoms on his sleeve and dew wetting his clothes. He recalled the time of his appointment with Peng, and burst into tears. While still brooding in sorrow, a sudden wind blew at his garment hem, and as the hem flipped up, the writing became visible. He raised his eyes and saw it was a seven-character quatrain, written by Peng. Deeply grieved, he lamented the lost opportunity, realizing he had been misled by others and had deeply disappointed Peng. He then cut off the skirt’s hem, mounted it on a scroll, and hung it on the wall. Still following the original rhyme, he composed another poem and sealed it to send to Peng. The poem read:

Wandering aimlessly like floating reeds and drifting duckweed, I mistakenly entered the misty peaks of Penglai. My ordinary bones have not yet become immortal, still bound to earthly affairs; strong winds sweep me into a dreamlike state. After the poem, there is another verse titled "Recalling Qin E" which says: Spring is desolate; pitifully, one has broken a promise to a beautiful woman! The promise to a beautiful woman, this time it is decided, do not break the promise. Though there might be a cure for love sickness, one should know that the body is too thin to be cured. The body is too thin; tears fall secretly at night. One day, I suddenly heard the lady calling Chunhong and saying, "The anniversary of the death of the Viceroy is approaching, and we should follow the usual customs. You can go to the house of the respected elder Yao, our neighbor, to ask when the Buddhist gathering at the Golden Mountain will be held, and also inquire about the public offering of merit for the Viceroy to seek blessings from the afterlife." Chunhong returned shortly with the message: "The gathering will start on the twenty-fifth of this month, coinciding with the temple's anniversary, lasting for three days and nights. If the public wishes to offer merit, they must strictly observe fasting and abstinence beforehand. On that day, please attend the Buddhist assembly, burn incense, and pay respects to the Buddha. Only after the ceremony is completed should you return." On the appointed day, the lady finished her preparations and then went to Yao's residence. The lady and her husband both accompanied her to the door, allowing them to enter together. As they passed by the husband's bedroom, he tried to invite her in, desiring to write about a romantic encounter. The lady earnestly declined, saying, "My humble and weak body, how could I be so selfish?"

During the day, with many servants and concubines around, if we were to meet during the intimate moment when passion is at its peak, I would be in a state of intoxication or dream, and how can I guarantee there would be no other concerns? It would be better to wait until tonight.
